  • connection-graph
  • wiring-plan
  • failure-carrier
  • extract-graph-ports
  • extract-graph-edges
  • are-graph-ports-well-typed
  • are-graph-edges-well-typed
  • is-graph-acyclic
  • get-graph-entry-steps
  • get-graph-exit-steps
  • is-true

Build Wiring Plan

Assembles a Wiring Plan from a Connection Graph by extracting ports/edges, checking typing, and ensuring acyclicity. Returns either the constructed plan or a Failure Carrier explaining why wiring failed.

Proof

  • Uses: port and edge extraction plus typing and cycle predicates to validate the graph.
  • Shows that: only well-typed, acyclic graphs yield wiring plans; otherwise a failure is produced.

title: Call id: call role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: operator-id alias: target
  • id: argument-list alias: args outputs:
  • id: runtime-value alias: result transformation:
  • call
  • dispatch-operator
  • target
  • args proof_refs:
  • operator-id
  • argument-list
  • dispatch-operator

Call

Primitive opcode that dispatches to a referenced operator with supplied arguments inside the nested-array interpreter, using the earned operator signatures for type/arity discipline.

Proof

  • Uses: operator identifiers and argument lists to route invocation through dispatch-operator.
  • Shows that: calls are mediated by the dispatcher, keeping execution within the documented operator set.

title: Canonical Representative Operator id: canonical-representative-operator role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Canonical Representative Operator

Given a recognition class, produce the most stable nucleus representative—a normal form.

Definition

  • Inputs: recognition (possibly an equivalence class under nucleus).
  • Output: canonical representative (e.g., minimal/most stable element) in the Recognition Carrier.
  • Behaviour: apply nucleus and simplification rules to collapse redundancies.

Invariants

  • Idempotent: applying twice yields the same representative.
  • Uniqueness: representatives are unique up to earned equivalence.

Proof

  • Uses: recognition lattice and nucleus to collapse to normal form.
  • Produces: canonical recognition.
  • Shows that: normal forms exist for earned recognition classes.

title: Causal Guard id: causal-guard role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Causal Guard

Ensures rollbacks and trace manipulations respect causality constraints.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Trace Shuffle Class and a Time Slice.
  • Output: causal validation (or refusal) that the shuffle does not introduce new influence paths across the rollback boundary.
  • Behaviour: checks that reordering via shuffle preserves dependency/causality relative to the time slice; if violated, guard fails and advises rollback rejection.

Invariants

  • Soundness: accepted shuffles cannot create new causal paths across the slice.
  • Stability: guards compose over multiple shuffles and slices.

Proof

  • Uses: Trace Shuffle Class and Time Slice.
  • Shows that: causal constraints can be enforced when manipulating traces with rollbacks.

title: Causal Ordering Operator id: causal-ordering-operator role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Causal Ordering Operator

Extracts a Lamport-like happens-before ordering from flow and observation recognitions.

Definition

  • Inputs: traces/observations in a Trace Shuffle Class.
  • Output: recognition encoding causal order (happens-before relation).
  • Behaviour: derive partial order from observation dependencies and shuffle constraints; encode as recognition.

Invariants

  • Reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive partial order.
  • Stable under shuffle equivalence.

Proof

  • Uses: trace/observation dependencies to build order.
  • Produces: causal order recognition.
  • Shows that: causality can be extracted recognitionally from traces.

title: Channel Consistency Operator id: channel-consistency-operator role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Channel Consistency Operator

Ensures messages that haven’t crossed nucleus boundaries are preserved across snapshots.

Definition

  • Inputs: Message Channel and Channel State.
  • Output: recognition indicating whether channel state is consistent with snapshots (no lost/duplicated messages).
  • Behaviour: compare send/receive records within the channel state; ensure unmatched messages are captured appropriately.

Invariants

  • Soundness: flags inconsistencies; passes consistent channels.
  • Stability: consistent channel remains consistent under snapshot composition.

Proof

  • Uses: channel/message records to verify integrity.
  • Produces: recognition of channel consistency.
  • Shows that: channel correctness can be validated recognitionally for snapshot assembly.

title: Characteristic Map id: characteristic-map role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Characteristic Map

Constructs the characteristic map of a subobject into the recognition classifier (Omega) in the relational sheaf topos.

Definition

  • Inputs: a monomorphism/subobject in the relational sheaf topos, expressed via the Subobject Classifier.
  • Outputs: its characteristic morphism into the Recognition Classifier.
  • Behaviour: assigns to each element/section the recognition (truth value) indicating membership in the subobject; compatible with base change via Base Change Square.

Invariants

  • Classifier property: the subobject is the pullback of true along the characteristic map.
  • Naturality: characteristic maps commute with pullbacks (Base-Change).

Proof

  • Uses: Subobject Classifier and Recognition Classifier to define characteristic maps.
  • Shows that: every subobject admits a unique characteristic map satisfying the classifier property, stable under base change.

title: Check Capability id: check-capability role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Check Capability

Verifies that a capability gate is satisfied for a privileged operation.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Capability Gate and a capability token (gate recognition).
  • Output: gate witness if valid; otherwise failure/denial.
  • Behaviour: compare token to gate requirements; allow/deny operation.

Invariants

  • Soundness: only valid tokens pass.
  • Non-bypass: denial when token missing/invalid.

Proof

  • Uses: gate recognition to match capability.
  • Produces: capability gate witness.
  • Shows that: capability enforcement is consistent and checkable.

title: Check Edge Typing id: check-edge-typing role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: edge-set alias: edges
  • id: port-set alias: ports outputs:
  • id: recognition alias: predicate transformation:
  • call
    • graph-edge-type-check
    • edges
    • ports proof_refs:
  • edge-set
  • port-set
  • recognition
  • graph-edge-type-check

Check Edge Typing

Validates that every edge in an Edge Set connects ports with matching carrier types from the Port Set. Returns a predicate recognition witnessing well-typedness (top if all edges are compatible, otherwise a counterexample).

Proof

  • Uses: port carrier assignments to compare each edge’s source/target types via graph-edge-type-check.
  • Shows that: wiring plans only proceed when every edge respects port typing.

title: Check Ergodicity id: check-ergodicity role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Check Ergodicity

The Check Ergodicity operator witnesses ergodicity for the fixed kernel, equating time and space averages.

Definition

  • Inputs: Fixed Kernel with Mixing Property.
  • Outputs: Ergodicity Property and the same fixed kernel.
  • Behaviour: checks that mixing implies ergodicity: time averages equal space averages for observables under this kernel.

Invariants

  • Confirms mixing implies ergodicity for the fixed kernel.
  • Keeps the kernel within its earned carrier.

Proof

Uses the Ergodicity act applied to a fixed kernel with mixing property to yield the ergodicity property. Shows that ergodicity is earned without leaving the fixed-kernel carrier.

title: Check Gate Eligibility id: check-gate-eligibility role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Check Gate Eligibility

Checks whether a recognition qualifies for entry into a gated subprofile/modal layer.

Definition

  • Inputs: a recognition and gate criteria encoded in Gate Recognition.
  • Output: gate recognition if eligible; otherwise failure/neutral.
  • Behaviour: evaluate recognition against gating constraints; confirm or deny access.

Invariants

  • Soundness: admits only recognitions satisfying gate predicates.
  • Stability: eligibility decisions are preserved under allowed flow/nucleus within the gated layer.

Proof

  • Uses: gate predicates over the recognition carrier.
  • Produces: gate recognition witness.
  • Shows that: gating can be enforced via recognitional checks.

title: Check Matching Family id: check-matching-family role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: coverage-site alias: site
  • id: matching-family alias: family outputs:
  • id: matching-family alias: validated transformation:
  • call
  • validate-matching-family
  • site
  • family proof_refs:
  • coverage-site
  • matching-family
  • validate-matching-family

Check Matching Family

Verifies that a family of sections over a cover is matching (agrees on overlaps) inside the Coverage Site so it can be glued by a Sheaf.

Definition

  • Inputs: a cover {Ui → U} in the coverage site and candidate sections over each Ui.
  • Outputs: a Matching Family witness if overlap conditions hold.
  • Behaviour: checks pairwise overlaps and triple coherences; if all match, packages the family with its cover so gluing operators can construct a global section.

Preconditions

  • Restriction maps for the cover are defined and coherent on the coverage site.
  • Sections are well-typed presheaf assignments over each patch.

Postconditions

  • Produced matching-family data is stable under further restriction/refinement.
  • Failure to match is explicit: the operator refuses to produce the structure.

Proof

  • Uses: coverage/overlap data from the Coverage Site and restriction maps encoded by the Sheaf.
  • Shows that: matching families can be validated as inputs to sheaf gluing, ensuring the sheaf condition operates on explicit compatibility data earned from the coverage site (objects/covers/restrictions/overlaps).

title: Check Noninterference id: check-noninterference role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Check Noninterference

Verifies that information-flow constraints enforce noninterference across channels/ports.

Definition

  • Inputs: labelled Message Channels, Ports Carrier, and an Information Flow Constraint.
  • Output: updated constraint witness if flows are compliant; otherwise failure.
  • Behaviour: check that low-observable outputs are independent of high inputs per policy.

Invariants

  • Soundness: passes only if noninterference holds.
  • Compositional: checks compose over channel networks.

Proof

  • Uses: policy lattice to test flows.
  • Produces: constraint witness.
  • Shows that: noninterference can be validated at the math level.

title: Check Parameterized Base Change id: check-parameterized-base-change role: operator status: plan derived_from: math/operator/base-change-square inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Check Parameterized Base Change

Computes and verifies base-change commutation for executable pullback/pushforward in the parameterized sheaf setting.

Definition

  • Inputs: a pullback square in the parameterized coverage site, plus a subobject or recognition represented via the parameterized classifier payloads.
  • Outputs: a Parameterized Base-Change Condition witness if pullback ∘ pushforward equals pushforward ∘ pullback; otherwise the operator fails to produce the structure.
  • Behaviour: runs restriction/direct-image routines along both routes of the square, compares classifier payloads, and records provenance of both paths.

Preconditions

  • Pullback and pushforward implementations are available from the parameterized sheaf and are deterministic for the supplied payloads.

Postconditions

  • On success, the produced witness can be cached and reused under refinements of the square.
  • On failure, callers can surface the mismatch for debugging or refinement.

Proof

  • Derived from: Base-Change Square by executing the abstract commutation check with concrete parameterized sheaf routines.
  • Shows that: the base-change law can be validated at the calculus level, supplying executable witnesses for substitution stability.

title: Check Parameterized Matching Family id: check-parameterized-matching-family role: operator status: plan derived_from: math/operator/check-matching-family inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Check Parameterized Matching Family

Executable checker for matching families over a Parameterized Coverage Site, refining the math-level compatibility test with concrete restriction functions.

Definition

  • Inputs: a cover {Ui → U} with executable restriction maps and candidate section payloads over each Ui.
  • Outputs: a Parameterized Matching Family if pairwise and triple-overlap agreements hold; otherwise the operator refuses to produce the structure (caller surfaces the failure).
  • Behaviour: iterates over cover indices, computes overlaps via the coverage site’s constructors, applies restriction functions, and checks equality of serialized sections; caches coherence witnesses for reuse by gluing and path composition lifts.

Preconditions

  • Restriction and overlap functions are total for the supplied cover.
  • Section payloads conform to the presheaf encoding in the parameterized sheaf.

Postconditions

  • Produced matching families are stable under cover refinement and transport along parameterized morphisms.
  • On failure, no matching-family structure is emitted; callers may log incompatibility for retry.

Proof

  • Derived from: Check Matching Family by instantiating covers, overlaps, and equality checks with executable functions from the parameterized coverage site and sheaf.
  • Shows that: matching-family validation is computable in calculus and supplies certified inputs for gluing and path-composition lifts.

title: Check Parametric Uniformity id: check-parametric-uniformity role: operator status: plan derived_from: acts/context-adjunction-witness inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Check Parametric Uniformity

The Check Parametric Uniformity operator asserts the parametricity property: terms related in one indexed context remain related under all reindexings of the context geometry.

Definition

  • Inputs: an Indexed Category over the Index Base and its corresponding Parametric Semantics carrier.
  • Behaviour: checks, using the Context Adjunction Witness, that reindexing along any base morphism preserves the parametric semantic relations.
  • Outputs: the same Parametric Semantics equipped with a uniformity witness.

Invariants

  • Reindexing respects the flow–nucleus adjunction.
  • Related arguments remain related under all reindexings in the index base.

Proof

  • Derived from: Context Adjunction Witness, which supplies the unit/counit equations for reindexing.
  • Uses: Indexed Category to transport contexts and Parametric Semantics as the relations being preserved.
  • Shows that: when reindexing is governed by the adjunction witness, the parametric semantics remain invariant across base morphisms, yielding the uniformity certificate.

title: Check Port Typing id: check-port-typing role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: port-set alias: ports outputs:
  • id: recognition alias: predicate transformation:
  • call
    • primitive-port-type-check
    • ports proof_refs:
  • port-set
  • recognition
  • primitive-port-type-check

Check Port Typing

Validates that each port’s carrier and direction match the inducing operator signature in the Port Set. Returns a predicate recognition witnessing well-typedness.

Proof

  • Uses: operator signatures associated with ports to check carrier/direction assignments via the port type check.
  • Shows that: ports are individually well-typed before edge wiring proceeds.

title: Classify Subobject id: classify-subobject role: operator status: plan derived_from: math/structure/subobject-classifier inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Classify Subobject

Constructs characteristic maps for subobjects using the recognition algebra and the subobject classifier.

Definition

  • Inputs: a mono m: A → B in the earned topos; the Relational Logic Algebra and Subobject Classifier.
  • Outputs: characteristic map chi_m: B → Omega exhibiting A as the pullback of true.
  • Behaviour: map elements/sections of B to recognitions indicating membership in the subobject; encode as chi_m into Omega.

Invariants

  • Correctness: chi_m satisfies the classifier property (pullback square with true recovers A).
  • Naturality: characteristic maps are stable under pullback.
  • Logical alignment: chi_m respects the logical operations of the recognition algebra.

Proof

  • Uses: Relational Logic Algebra to interpret membership as recognition; Subobject Classifier to target characteristic maps.
  • Shows that: characteristic maps constructed from recognitions satisfy the subobject classifier axioms, enabling classification of subobjects by earned recognitions.

title: Coarse-Grain Section id: coarse-grain-section role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Coarse-Grain Section

Coarse-grains a hypertensor section from a fine scale to a coarser scale using a scale embedding.

Definition

  • Inputs: fine Scale Window, a Scale Embedding into a coarser window, and a Hypertensor Section on the fine window.
  • Output: coarse-grained Hypertensor Section on the coarse window.
  • Behaviour: restrict along the embedding, stabilise via nucleus if needed, and push forward along the embedding to the coarse window.

Invariants

  • Preservation: fixed shapes/recognitions are preserved under coarse-graining.
  • Monotonicity: coarse-graining cannot create new fine-scale detail.
  • Compatibility: commutes with coverage and sheaf restrictions.

Proof

  • Uses: Scale Embedding and Hypertensor Section to define the map.
  • Produces: coarse section for RG or multiscale analysis.
  • Shows that: coarse-graining is a well-defined operator respecting earned sheaf/coverage structure across scales.

title: Coherent Collapse id: coherent-collapse role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Coherent Collapse

Collapse of recognitions in a pattern-preserving way—coherence conditions akin to categorical coherence.

Definition

  • Inputs: recognitions with dependency/pattern structure from Pattern Carrier.
  • Output: collapsed recognition preserving those dependencies.
  • Behaviour: apply nucleus collapse subject to coherence constraints so dependent recognitions collapse compatibly.

Invariants

  • Dependency preservation: dependent pieces collapse consistently.
  • Stability: collapse results respect pattern structure.

Proof

  • Uses: nucleus and pattern dependencies to constrain collapse.
  • Produces: coherent collapsed recognition.
  • Shows that: collapses can enforce coherence conditions.

title: Collapse Loop Operator id: collapse-loop-operator role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Collapse Loop Operator

Collapses loops of recognitions into fundamental “shape classes” (π₁ analogue).

Definition

  • Inputs: loops in Loop Space / paths in the Fundamental Groupoid.
  • Output: recognition of the loop’s homotopy class (shape class).
  • Behaviour: classify loops up to homotopy, report class as recognition.

Invariants

  • Homotopy invariance: loops in same class yield same recognition.
  • Groupoid compatibility: composition corresponds to class composition.

Proof

  • Uses: loop/fundamental groupoid structure to identify homotopy classes.
  • Produces: recognition marking the class.
  • Shows that: fundamental group(oid) information can be captured recognitionally.

title: Collect Snapshot State id: collect-snapshot-state role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: recognition alias: recognitions
  • id: evaluation-state alias: eval-state outputs:
  • id: snapshot alias: snapshot
  • id: failure-carrier alias: failure transformation:
  • call
    • read-snapshot-state
    • recognitions
    • eval-state proof_refs:
  • recognition
  • evaluation-state
  • snapshot
  • failure-carrier
  • read-snapshot-state

Collect Snapshot State

Definition

  • Inputs: Recognition Carrier, Evaluation State.
  • Outputs: Snapshot content or Failure Carrier.
  • Behaviour: reads current recognitions and evaluation state to produce snapshot content compatible with the snapshot schema. Emits FailureCarrier if state cannot be read or is inconsistent.

Invariants

  • Point-in-time consistency: recognitions and evaluation state are captured without interleaving mutations.
  • No mutation of inputs; failures leave state unchanged.

Proof

  • Uses: machine state read, validation, and snapshot construction.
  • Shows that: collecting machine carriers yields snapshot content or an explicit failure when state is inconsistent.

title: Comonad Reindexing id: comonad-reindex role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Comonad Reindexing

Definition

  • Inputs: a morphism in the Index Base and a comonadic object in the source Comonad Fiber.
  • Outputs: the pullback of that comonadic object into the target fibre, preserving the comonad structure.
  • Behaviour: lifts the math-layer Reindexing operator so that pulling back along a base morphism commutes with the fibrewise comonad action.

Invariants

  • Functorial: respects identity and composition of base morphisms.
  • Comonad-compatible: preserves counit and comultiplication of the fibrewise comonad.

Proof

  • Uses: Reindexing to transport comonadic recognitions along base morphisms while preserving comonad laws.
  • Shows that: when fibres carry a comonad, the reindexing functor lifts to comonadic objects while maintaining counit/comultiplication, producing the fibrewise comonad in the target fibre.

title: Compare Section Restrictions id: compare-section-restrictions role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: hypertensor-site alias: site
  • id: hypertensor-section alias: section-a
  • id: hypertensor-section alias: section-b outputs:
  • id: recognition alias: equality-witness transformation:
  • return
  • equality-witness proof_refs:
  • hypertensor-site
  • hypertensor-section

Compare Section Restrictions

Restricts two Hypertensor Sections along a cover in the Hypertensor Site and checks equality of all restrictions, yielding an equality witness when they coincide.

Proof

  • Uses: restriction maps defined by the hypertensor site to project sections to cover elements, then compare.
  • Shows that: agreement on a cover implies global equality, supporting the sheaf locality property.

title: Compose Frame id: compose-frame role: operator status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/build-evaluation-context inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Compose Frame

Partial composition compose : Frame × Frame ⇀ Frame for frames from EvaluationContext.

Statement

Whenever compose(F1, F2) is defined,

plug(compose(F1, F2), t) = plug(F1, plug(F2, t))

and composition is associative where defined, with empty as neutral.

Proof

Sketch: define compose by nesting holes along the trace spine, using frames generated by BuildEvaluationContext. Show associativity and neutrality of empty from SeedEvaluationContext by structural induction on frames and the definition of plug.

title: Compose Paths id: compose-paths role: operator status: plan derived_from: machine/operator/path-compose-runtime inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Compose Paths

SDK-level function that invokes the kernel Path Compose Runtime operator against the Coverage Runtime Type.

Definition

  • Inputs: two SDK path handles with aligned endpoints and a Coverage Runtime Type instance providing covers and restriction APIs.
  • Outputs: a composed path handle or a structured failure mirroring FailureCarrier.
  • Behaviour: validates endpoint and cover alignment, forwards to the kernel operator, and surfaces provenance/log tokens for downstream sheaf gluing.

Preconditions

  • Client holds read/write access to the supplied coverage runtime instance.
  • Paths have been normalized to the same discretization parameters.

Postconditions

  • On success, the composed path handle is registered in the runtime matches log; on failure, no runtime mutation occurs except a failure entry.
  • Returned handles reference stable SDK types without leaking kernel details.

Proof

  • Derived from: Path Compose Runtime by exposing its call signature and failure contracts as a stable SDK function.
  • Shows that: programs can compose paths through the coverage runtime without bypassing computer-layer abstractions.

title: Compressibility Recognition id: compressibility-recognition role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Compressibility Recognition

Recognises whether a trace can be simplified under shuffle/nucleus without loss.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Trace Shuffle Class.
  • Output: recognition indicating compressibility/incompressibility.
  • Behaviour: attempt to reduce the trace via shuffle equivalences and nucleus; if equivalent shorter trace exists, mark as compressible.

Invariants

  • Soundness: only truly reducible traces are marked compressible.
  • Monotonicity: adding more steps cannot make an incompressible trace compressible without additional equivalences.

Proof

  • Uses: Trace Shuffle Class to explore equivalences.
  • Produces: Recognition Carrier marker.
  • Shows that: compressibility is recognisable within the earned trace/shuffle structure.

title: Compute Attracting Basin id: compute-attracting-basin role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Compute Attracting Basin

Computes the basin of recognitions converging to a fixed shape.

Definition

  • Inputs: an attractor recognition/shape, flow rules, and nucleus.
  • Output: Recognition Attracting Basin containing recognitions that converge to the attractor.
  • Behaviour: iterate flow/nucleus from candidate states; include those that converge to the target.

Invariants

  • Correctness: basin includes all and only recognitions converging to target.
  • Stability: basin closed under flow/nucleus toward attractor.

Proof

  • Uses: flow dynamics and nucleus convergence.
  • Produces: attracting basin.
  • Shows that: basins are computable from attractors.

title: Compute Collapse Manifold id: compute-collapse-manifold role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Compute Collapse Manifold

Computes the collapse manifold for a recognition.

Definition

  • Inputs: recognition, flow rules, and nucleus.
  • Output: Collapse Manifold containing all recognitions that collapse to the same limit.
  • Behaviour: explore flow extensions, apply nucleus, collect recognitions with identical limits.

Invariants

  • Correctness: manifold matches shared collapse limit.
  • Stability: manifold closed under flow/nucleus to the limit.

Proof

  • Uses: flow and nucleus operations.
  • Produces: collapse manifold witness.
  • Shows that: collapse equivalence classes are computable.

title: Compute Divergence Angle id: compute-divergence-angle role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Compute Divergence Angle

Computes the angle-like divergence between two flow directions at a recognition.

Definition

  • Inputs: a recognition, two admissible flow directions, and a Relational Metric.
  • Output: Recognition Divergence Angle.
  • Behaviour: measure directional difference using metric-induced inner product or analogous relational measure.

Invariants

  • Zero when flows coincide; respects symmetry in symmetric settings.
  • Monotone with increasing directional difference.

Proof

  • Uses: flow directions and metric to compute divergence.
  • Produces: divergence angle witness.
  • Shows that: flow directional differences are measurable.

title: Compute Fixed Reach id: compute-fixed-reach role: operator status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/fixed-reach inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Compute Fixed Reach

The Compute Fixed Reach operator witnesses which fixed invariant states become reachable under the earned fixed flow.

Definition

  • Inputs: Fixed Invariant State.
  • Outputs: Fixed Invariant State that is reachable by the fixed flow.
  • Behaviour: applies the fixed flow induced by Fixed Reach to evolve invariant states while staying within the fixed profile.

Invariants

  • Reachable states remain within the fixed profile and preserve fixed shape recognitions.
  • No evolution outside the fixed invariant carrier; only admissible fixed-flow steps are allowed.

Proof

By construction from acts/fixed-reach, this operator restricts the earned flow to fixed invariant states. The act ensures reachability stays inside the fixed profile, so inputs and outputs remain in the carrier.

title: Compute Flow Potential id: compute-flow-potential role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Compute Flow Potential

Computes flow potential for recognitions to guide scheduling/optimisation.

Definition

  • Inputs: recognition, flow rules, and Entropy Functional.
  • Output: Flow Potential value.
  • Behaviour: estimate entropy production/resistance along likely flow paths; assign potential.

Invariants

  • Consistency: higher potentials correspond to higher expected entropy/effort.
  • Stability: potential recomputed coherently as flow rules change.

Proof

  • Uses: flow and entropy to estimate potential.
  • Produces: potential assignment.
  • Shows that: potential is well-defined and useful for planning.

title: Compute Influence Kernel id: compute-influence-kernel role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Compute Influence Kernel

Computes how recognitions transform others under flow.

Definition

  • Inputs: source recognitions and Flow Context.
  • Output: Influence Kernel mapping sources to induced changes in targets.
  • Behaviour: perturb source, propagate via flow, measure effect on targets, and record in kernel.

Invariants

  • Consistency with flow composition.
  • Monotonicity: larger perturbations yield larger influences.

Proof

  • Uses: flow dynamics to measure influence.
  • Produces: influence kernel.
  • Shows that: differential effects can be captured recognitionally.

title: Compute MultiAgent Fixed Shape id: compute-multiagent-fixed-shape role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Compute MultiAgent Fixed Shape

Computes shapes stable under joint flows of multiple agents.

Definition

  • Inputs: a set of Agents and their joint Social Flow.
  • Output: MultiAgent Fixed Shape.
  • Behaviour: iterate joint flow to find invariant recognitions/sections.

Invariants

  • Stability: outputs remain unchanged under further joint flow.
  • Robustness: nearby states converge to the fixed shape under joint flow.

Proof

  • Uses: social flow to test invariance.
  • Produces: fixed shapes.
  • Shows that: multi-agent invariants can be computed from joint dynamics.

title: Compute Neighborhood id: compute-neighborhood role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Compute Neighborhood

Computes the relational neighborhood (open ball) of a recognition.

Definition

  • Inputs: a recognition in the Recognition Carrier.
  • Output: a Relational Neighborhood consisting of flow-successors within one nucleus class.
  • Behaviour: apply one-step Flow Context, close under nucleus, collect resulting recognitions.

Invariants

  • Minimal: contains exactly the immediate flow/nucleus successors.
  • Monotone: neighborhoods expand (never shrink) as flow is iterated.

Proof

  • Uses: Flow Context and the recognition lattice.
  • Produces: Relational Neighborhood.
  • Shows that: neighborhoods are computable and form the basis for relational topology/metric definitions.

title: Compute Profile Mixing id: compute-profile-mixing role: operator status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/profile-mixing inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Compute Profile Mixing

The Compute Profile Mixing operator applies the profile mixing act to push entropy forward along a profile kernel chain until stationarity.

Definition

  • Input: a Profile Kernel Chain.
  • Output: an updated chain reflecting mixing towards stationarity.
  • Behaviour: composes profile dynamics and profile entropy per acts/profile-mixing.

Invariants

  • Entropy increases until a stationary profile is reached.
  • Chain remains within the earned carrier.

Proof

Direct application of acts/profile-mixing; inherits mixing properties and preserves the chain carrier.

title: Compute Reachability Radius id: compute-reachability-radius role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Compute Reachability Radius

Computes the minimal flow distance between two recognitions.

Definition

  • Inputs: source/target recognitions, flow rules, and Relational Metric.
  • Output: Reachability Radius.
  • Behaviour: search admissible flow paths, take minimal metric length.

Invariants

  • Minimality: reported radius is minimal over admissible paths.
  • Symmetry if flow is reversible; otherwise directed radius.

Proof

  • Uses: flow/metric to compute shortest paths.
  • Produces: radius measure.
  • Shows that: reachability cost is computable for planning/scheduling.

title: Compute Recognition Normal Form id: compute-recognition-normal-form role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Compute Recognition Normal Form

Computes the canonical normal form of a recognition via nucleus and logical simplification.

Definition

  • Inputs: a recognition in the Recognition Carrier.
  • Output: its Recognition Normal Form.
  • Behaviour: apply nucleus closure, simplify using logical laws (idempotence, absorption, distributivity, implication adjunction) until fixed.

Invariants

  • Idempotent: normalising twice yields the same result.
  • Deterministic: output depends only on the input recognition.

Proof

  • Uses: nucleus and logic algebra simplifications.
  • Produces: canonical normal form.
  • Shows that: normalisation terminates and yields a unique representative up to earned equivalence.

title: Compute Recognition Polytope id: compute-recognition-polytope role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Compute Recognition Polytope

Computes the closure of a finite set of recognitions under join/meet to form a recognition polytope.

Definition

  • Inputs: finite generator set in Recognition Carrier.
  • Output: Recognition Polytope.
  • Behaviour: iteratively apply join/meet until closure stabilises.

Invariants

  • Finite termination: closure stabilises after finite steps.
  • Minimality: yields smallest closed set containing generators.

Proof

  • Uses: recognition lattice operations.
  • Produces: polytope closure.
  • Shows that: finite generators yield a closed polytope via joins/meets.

title: Compute Recoverability Index id: compute-recoverability-index role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Compute Recoverability Index

Computes the recoverability index for a collapsed recognition.

Definition

  • Inputs: a collapsed recognition and its preimage structure under Nucleus.
  • Output: Recoverability Index.
  • Behaviour: count/measure distinct preimages or reconstruction potential; assign index value.

Invariants

  • Correctness: index reflects actual preimage multiplicity.
  • Monotone: coarser collapses reduce index.

Proof

  • Uses: nucleus mapping to analyse preimages.
  • Produces: recoverability index.
  • Shows that: information recoverability is computable.

title: Compute Relational Metric id: compute-relational-metric role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Compute Relational Metric

Computes flow-based distances between recognitions.

Definition

  • Inputs: recognitions and admissible Flow Context.
  • Output: a Relational Metric.
  • Behaviour: search for shortest admissible flow paths modulo nucleus equivalence; assign distances; store as metric data.

Invariants

  • Triangle inequality via path concatenation.
  • Zero distance on nucleus-equivalent recognitions.
  • Monotone with respect to adding admissible flow steps.

Proof

  • Uses: flow/neighborhood structure to define path lengths.
  • Produces: Relational Metric.
  • Shows that: distances are well-defined from flow dynamics.

title: Compute Section Normal Form id: compute-section-normal-form role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Compute Section Normal Form

Computes the canonical normal form of a hypertensor section.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Hypertensor Section.
  • Output: its Section Normal Form.
  • Behaviour: apply nucleus collapses and simplification rules until fixed; emit canonical representative.

Invariants

  • Idempotent and unique up to earned equivalence.
  • Deterministic given the section.

Proof

  • Uses: nucleus and simplification rules.
  • Produces: normal form.
  • Shows that: normalization terminates and yields a canonical form.

title: Compute Tower Residue id: compute-tower-residue role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Compute Tower Residue

Computes the residual recognitions lost when collapsing a tower to a lower level.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Modality Tower (or similar tower) and a projection to a lower layer.
  • Output: Tower Residue capturing recognitions discarded by the projection.
  • Behaviour: compare recognitions before/after collapse; collect differences as residue.

Invariants

  • Soundness: residues exactly the recognitions not preserved.
  • Monotonicity: deeper collapses accumulate residues.

Proof

  • Uses: tower projection structure.
  • Produces: residue data.
  • Shows that: lost information can be explicitly recorded.

title: Confluence id: confluence role: operator status: plan derived_from: acts/ensure-confluence inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Confluence

Definition

  • Inputs: divergent reduction traces witnessed by Complete Trace and Step Observation.
  • Outputs: evidence that the traces rejoin, recorded by Ensure Confluence.
  • Behaviour: asserts that any pair of reductions from a common source has a common successor (Church–Rosser).

Invariants

  • Common successor exists for any divergent one-step reductions.
  • Reduction relation remains deterministic up to confluence; observation traces agree after join.

Proof

  • Derived from: Ensure Confluence.
  • Shows that: with complete traces and step observations, divergent reductions rejoin, establishing confluence of the earned reduction relation.

title: Consolidate Memory id: consolidate-memory role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Consolidate Memory

Collapse a live episodic trace into a persistent memory unit.

Definition

  • Inputs: an Episodic Trace and access to Save Context.
  • Output: a Memory Carrier packaging the episode for persistence.
  • Behaviour: apply save-context to the episode’s contexts/recognitions, apply baseline/limit operations on the tail, and store the results.

Invariants

  • Fidelity: saved memory reloads to an equivalent episode.
  • Completeness: all necessary context/recognition/tail data are captured.

Proof

  • Uses: Episodic Trace and Save Context.
  • Produces: Memory Carrier.
  • Shows that: episodes can be consolidated into persistent memory consistent with earned save/load semantics.

title: Construct Glued Section id: construct-glued-section role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: matching-family alias: family outputs:
  • id: hypertensor-section alias: section transformation:
  • call
  • amalgamate-matching-family
  • family proof_refs:
  • matching-family
  • hypertensor-section

Proof

  • Uses: overlap coherence in the matching family to assemble section values.
  • Shows that: a glued section exists and is unique up to earned equivalence when matching conditions hold.

Construct Glued Section

Builds a global Hypertensor Section from a validated Matching Family over a cover.

Proof

  • Uses: cover and overlap coherence recorded in the matching family to assemble a unique amalgam.
  • Shows that: when matching conditions hold, an amalgamated section exists and is unique up to earned equivalence, satisfying the gluing half of the sheaf condition.

title: Construct Struct id: construct-struct role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: runtime-value alias: fields outputs:
  • id: runtime-value alias: struct transformation:
  • call
    • build-struct
    • fields proof_refs:
  • build-struct
  • runtime-value

Construct Struct

Assembles a keyed aggregate from field pairs provided to struct. Placeholder for the concrete record-construction routine in the interpreter.

Proof

  • Uses: Build Struct to build the record.
  • Shows that: struct construction is delegated to a documented helper; this operator wires it into the interpreter semantics.

title: Context Adjunction id: context-adjunction role: operator status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/context-adjunction-witness inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Context Adjunction

Adjunction between context flow and context nucleus, earned by ContextAdjunctionWitness and DynamicResiduation.

Statement

Natural bijection for contexts C, D:

Hom(flow(C), D) ≅ Hom(C, nucleus(D))

Proof

Sketch: DynamicResiduation provides the hom-redistribution; ContextFlow and ContextNucleus are the endofunctors. The bijection yields the adjunction, with unit/counit witnessing the monad/comonad induced by the adjunction.

title: Context Renewal id: context-renewal role: operator status: plan derived_from: acts/renew-context inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Context Renewal

Definition

  • Inputs: baseline remainder from Baseline Remainder and a context to renew.
  • Outputs: renewed context carrying the baseline remainder forward.
  • Behaviour: renews a context (via Renew Context) while preserving bindings and baseline tail.

Invariants

  • Baseline remainder preserved across renewals/extensions.
  • Bindings introduced before renewal remain valid afterward.

Proof

  • Derived from: Renew Context.
  • Shows that: renewing contexts with baseline tails yields contexts consistent with future extensions and bindings, preserving the baseline remainder.

title: Coordination Operator id: coordination-operator role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Coordination Operator

Attempts to align recognitions across agents by flowing until a stable intersection is reached.

Definition

  • Inputs: a set of Agents and their channels.
  • Output: updated Agents with maximally aligned recognitions.
  • Behaviour: iterate Social Flow and project onto the shared intersection of epistemic/norm profiles; stop at fixed point or failure.

Invariants

  • Convergence: if a stable intersection exists, iterations converge.
  • Monotonicity: shared recognitions expand monotonically across iterations.

Proof

  • Uses: Social Flow and agent data.
  • Produces: aligned agents.
  • Shows that: coordination can be modelled as a flow to stable intersections.

title: Counterfactual Operator id: counterfactual-operator role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Counterfactual Operator

Constructs the closest alternative world consistent with a hypothetical.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Branching Time World and a hypothetical modification to a Time Slice.
  • Output: modified Relational World pushed forward under flow from the altered slice.
  • Behaviour: modify the slice, apply Flow Context forward along the branch, and collapse via nucleus to obtain the counterfactual world.

Invariants

  • Minimal change: alters only what is necessary to satisfy the hypothetical.
  • Consistency: resulting world still respects flow/nucleus and branch causality.

Proof

  • Uses: Branching Time World and Flow Context.
  • Produces: Relational World representing the counterfactual.
  • Shows that: counterfactual reasoning fits within earned temporal branching and flow semantics.

title: Cross-Scale Influence id: cross-scale-influence role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Cross-Scale Influence

Measures how changes at fine scale alter coarse-scale recognitions.

Definition

  • Inputs: fine/coarse levels in the Scale Tower and a Hypertensor Section.
  • Output: recognition reflecting sensitivity of coarse recognitions to fine perturbations (scale-gradient).
  • Behaviour: perturb fine level, rescale to coarse, measure resulting change, express as recognition.

Invariants

  • Monotonicity: larger fine changes yield larger coarse influence.
  • Stability: zero influence when fine changes collapse under rescaling.

Proof

  • Uses: rescaling across scales and recognition comparison.
  • Produces: Recognition Carrier measure.
  • Shows that: cross-scale effects are recognisable and quantifiable.

title: Curvature Recognition id: curvature-recognition role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Curvature Recognition

Detects nontrivial holonomy as relational curvature.

Definition

  • Inputs: holonomy data from Holonomy Operator.
  • Output: recognition indicating presence/absence/degree of curvature.
  • Behaviour: interpret nontrivial holonomy as curvature witness in the recognition lattice.

Invariants

  • Flatness: zero holonomy yields neutral/flat recognition.
  • Monotonicity: greater holonomy yields stronger curvature recognition.

Proof

  • Uses: Holonomy Operator.
  • Produces: Recognition Carrier element indicating curvature.
  • Shows that: curvature can be recognised via accumulated holonomy in the relational sheaf setting.

title: Debias Flow id: debias-flow role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Debias Flow

Flow that attempts to cancel bias via normalisation and context flow.

Definition

  • Inputs: biased epistemic profile (from Bias Operator) and Flow Context.
  • Output: debiased Epistemic Profile.
  • Behaviour: apply inverse/normalising transform to undo bias where possible, propagate through context flow, and stabilise.

Invariants

  • Correctness: applying bias then debias approximates identity when invertible.
  • Stability: debiasing preserves admissible recognitions; never invents new recognitions beyond the unbiased base.

Proof

  • Uses: Bias Operator and Flow Context.
  • Produces: Epistemic Profile.
  • Shows that: biases can be countered within the earned flow framework.

title: Decode Snapshot id: decode-snapshot role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: snapshot alias: snapshot outputs:
  • id: recognition alias: recognitions
  • id: evaluation-state alias: eval-state
  • id: failure-carrier alias: failure transformation:
  • return
    • recognitions
    • eval-state
    • failure proof_refs:
  • snapshot
  • recognition
  • evaluation-state
  • failure-carrier

Decode Snapshot

Decodes a stored Snapshot into recognitions and evaluation state, or failure if reconstruction is impossible.

Invariants

  • Verifies snapshot integrity and schema before reconstruction.
  • Produces recognitions/state or a failure; inputs remain unchanged.

title: Depth First Search id: depth-first-search role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: edge-set alias: edges outputs:
  • id: recognition alias: traversal transformation:
  • return
  • traversal proof_refs:
  • edge-set

Proof

  • Uses: adjacency over the edge set to traverse successors, marking visited and back-edges.
  • Shows that: traversal order with back-edge hooks can be produced as a recognition, supporting cycle detection.

Depth First Search

Performs a depth-first traversal over an Edge Set, producing a recognition of the traversal order with back-edge detection hooks.

Proof

  • Uses: adjacency in the edge set to explore successor edges, marking visited nodes and back-edges.
  • Shows that: the traversal output can be consumed by cycle detection operators to witness cycles or confirm acyclicity.

title: Derive Happens-Before id: derive-happens-before role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Derive Happens-Before

Derives the happens-before partial order from traces and channels.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Trace Shuffle Class and Message Channels with send/receive events.
  • Output: Happens-Before Relation.
  • Behaviour: combine program order within traces and channel order to form the transitive closure.

Invariants

  • Partial order properties hold.
  • Invariant under shuffle-equivalent interleavings.

Proof

  • Uses: trace ordering and channel ordering to build relation.
  • Produces: causality relation.
  • Shows that: causality extraction is well-defined and stable under interleavings.

title: Detect Conflicts id: detect-conflicts role: operator status: someday derived_from: computer/function/evaluate-step inputs:

  • computer/library/type/calendar-schedule outputs:
  • computer/library/type/conflict-report
  • computer/type/computer-failure transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Detect Conflicts

Detects overlaps and constraint violations in a calendar schedule.

Definition

  • Inputs: Calendar Schedule.
  • Outputs: Conflict Report or Computer Failure.
  • Behaviour: scans schedule for overlapping events, violated dependencies, or policy conflicts; emits conflict report or failure if analysis cannot complete.

Invariants

  • No state mutation; conflicts/failures are diagnostic outputs.
  • Deterministic analysis for a given schedule.

Proof

  • Derived from: Computer Evaluate Step.
  • Shows that: conflict detection can be realised as a computer evaluation step producing conflict recognitions/failures without altering state.

title: Detect Critical Modality id: detect-critical-modality role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Detect Critical Modality

Detects modalities whose collapse changes fixed-point structures.

Definition

  • Inputs: Modal Structure.
  • Output: Critical Modality witness for modalities with global impact.
  • Behaviour: simulate collapsing/adding modalities; observe changes in fixed points/invariants; mark critical ones.

Invariants

  • Soundness: only modalities whose change alters fixed points are marked.
  • Stability: criticality persists under equivalent modal presentations.

Proof

  • Uses: modal analysis and fixed-point calculations.
  • Produces: critical modality witness.
  • Shows that: sensitivity to modality changes can be detected recognitionally.

title: Detect Equivalence Shock id: detect-equivalence-shock role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Detect Equivalence Shock

Detects sudden nucleus-equivalence of distinct recognitions after perturbation.

Definition

  • Inputs: recognitions and a flow/perturbation path.
  • Output: Equivalence Shock witness when two recognitions collapse unexpectedly.
  • Behaviour: track equivalence classes along flow; flag abrupt mergers.

Invariants

  • Soundness: flags only when equivalence arises abruptly.
  • Persistence: post-shock equivalence holds under further flow.

Proof

  • Uses: flow evolution and nucleus checks.
  • Produces: equivalence shock record.
  • Shows that: sudden equivalence events are detectable.

title: Detect Flow Attractor id: detect-flow-attractor role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Detect Flow Attractor

Detects flow attractors by analysing basins of attraction.

Definition

  • Inputs: flow rules and candidate recognitions/basins.
  • Output: Flow Attractor witness if convergence holds.
  • Behaviour: compute attracting basin via Compute Attracting Basin; verify stability of candidate; mark as attractor.

Invariants

  • Soundness: only true attractors are marked.
  • Stability: attractor classification persists under small flow perturbations.

Proof

  • Uses: basin computation and fixed-point checks.
  • Produces: attractor witness.
  • Shows that: convergence targets can be identified via basin analysis.

title: Detect Graph Cycles id: detect-graph-cycles role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: edge-set alias: edges outputs:
  • id: recognition alias: cycle-witness transformation:
  • call
    • graph-cycle-search
    • edges proof_refs:
  • edge-set
  • graph-cycle-search

Proof

  • Uses: graph cycle search over the edge set to produce a cycle witness or bottom.
  • Shows that: cycle presence is captured as a recognition, enabling acyclicity checks.

Detect Graph Cycles

Produces a recognition witness of a directed cycle in an Edge Set, or the bottom recognition if none exist. Intended to be negated to assert acyclicity.

Proof

  • Uses: edge adjacency to search for back-edges via graph-cycle-search (depth-first or equivalent), emitting a recognition of the discovered cycle or bottom if none.
  • Shows that: any returned witness certifies the presence of a cycle; absence (bottom) is the condition used by is-graph-acyclic.

title: Detect Phase Synchronization id: detect-phase-synchronization role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Detect Phase Synchronization

Detects phase-synchronized recognitions under harmonic flow.

Definition

  • Inputs: sections/recognitions and Harmonic Flow.
  • Output: Phase-Synchronized Recognition witnesses if phases remain locked.
  • Behaviour: track phase relationships across harmonic modes under evolution; mark synchrony when maintained.

Invariants

  • Robustness: synchrony persists under small perturbations.
  • Stability: synchrony recognition is stable under continued harmonic flow.

Proof

  • Uses: harmonic flow to evaluate phase coherence.
  • Produces: synchrony witness.
  • Shows that: phase synchronization can be recognised in the harmonic setting.

title: Detect Phase Transition id: detect-phase-transition role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Detect Phase Transition

Detects critical points where recognition families change topology/structure under parameterised flow.

Definition

  • Inputs: parameterised recognitions/flows.
  • Output: Relational Phase Transition witnesses at critical parameters.
  • Behaviour: monitor invariants across parameters; flag abrupt changes.

Invariants

  • Soundness: marks only genuine critical changes.
  • Stability: phases away from critical points remain consistent.

Proof

  • Uses: flow/invariant analysis across parameter space.
  • Produces: phase transition markers.
  • Shows that: critical phenomena are detectable in the relational setting.

title: Detect Plan Conflicts id: detect-plan-conflicts role: operator status: someday derived_from: computer/function/evaluate-step inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Detect Plan Conflicts

Analyzes a program wiring plan for overlaps or constraint violations.

Definition

  • Inputs: Program Wiring Plan.
  • Outputs: success (no conflicts) or Computer Failure describing conflicts.
  • Behaviour: checks typing, dependency ordering, and policy/trace constraints; emits failure if conflicts are found.

Invariants

  • No state mutation; diagnostics only.
  • Deterministic for a given plan.
  • Failure modes: type mismatch, unsatisfied dependencies, policy/trace constraint violations, cycles.

Proof

  • Derived from: Computer Evaluate Step.
  • Shows that: conflict detection can be performed as an evaluation step yielding explicit failures without altering state.

title: Detect Recognition Catalysis id: detect-recognition-catalysis role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Detect Recognition Catalysis

Detects recognitions that enable otherwise impossible flow or collapse.

Definition

  • Inputs: candidate catalyst, target process (flow/collapse).
  • Output: Recognition Catalysis witness if catalyst enables the process.
  • Behaviour: test process with/without candidate; record enabling effect.

Invariants

  • Soundness: marks only genuine enablers.
  • Persistence: catalytic effect consistent across admissible contexts.

Proof

  • Uses: flow/collapse operations to test catalysis.
  • Produces: catalyst witness.
  • Shows that: enabling recognitions can be detected.

title: Diagonal Recognition id: diagonal-recognition role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Diagonal Recognition

Recognises self-application of recognitions (diagonal/self-reference) and seeks fixed points.

Definition

  • Inputs: recognition R in the Recognition Universe.
  • Output: base recognition representing R applied to itself, collapsed via Nucleus.
  • Behaviour: form R(R), apply nucleus to stabilise; fixed points indicate self-referential stability.

Invariants

  • Fixed points: R(R) that stabilises indicates self-consistency.
  • Paradox control: nucleus prevents trivialisation.

Proof

  • Uses: recognition universe and nucleus.
  • Produces: base recognition of self-application.
  • Shows that: diagonal/self-reference can be represented safely.

title: Dialetheia Recognition id: dialetheia-recognition role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Dialetheia Recognition

Recognises stable coexisting contradictions in a belief base.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Belief Base.
  • Output: recognition indicating presence of contradictions that persist under inference (paraconsistent truths).
  • Behaviour: detect pairs of recognitions p and ¬p both supported in the base; mark them as dialetheias.

Invariants

  • Persistence: dialetheias remain unless revision removes one side.
  • Compatibility: integrates with recognition logic without triviality.

Proof

  • Uses: Belief Base and the recognition lattice.
  • Produces: Recognition Carrier element marking contradictions.
  • Shows that: paraconsistent coexistence can be recognised explicitly.

title: Dialogue Update id: dialogue-update role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Dialogue Update

Coordinated epistemic updates between agents engaged in dialogue.

Definition

  • Inputs: two or more Agents and a Message Channel carrying utterances as observations.
  • Output: updated Agent states after processing the dialogue turn.
  • Behaviour: treat each utterance as an Observation, apply Epistemic Update to speaker and listener, and optionally broadcast refined recognitions back through the channel.

Invariants

  • Order-respecting: updates follow channel order; no out-of-order beliefs.
  • Convergence: repeated application can reach a fixed point (consensus) if no contradictions remain.
  • Monotonicity: each turn refines (does not expand) epistemic possibilities.

Proof

  • Uses: Agent, Message Channel, Epistemic Update.
  • Produces: updated Agent.
  • Shows that: dialogue induces coupled epistemic updates consistent with channel observations and agent flow/norm structure.

title: Discover Carrier id: discover-carrier role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Discover Carrier

Attempts to identify previously unknown or unregistered carriers present in a sheaf section, surfacing them as recognitions or failures.

Definition

  • Inputs: recognition criteria for target carrier shapes/types, a Quasicrystalline Hypertensor Sheaf Section, ambient Policy Regime, and the current Trace Carrier.
  • Outputs: Recognition Carrier entries for discovered carriers or a Failure Algebra element when discovery fails or is disallowed.
  • Behaviour:
    1. Enumerate candidate subwindows/subsections from the section’s coverage site and policy-visible regions.
    2. For each candidate, apply earned recognition operators (e.g. Recognize Observation, Recognize Context) using the supplied criteria.
    3. If a candidate matches, emit a recognition carrier tagged with the trace span and policy witness; otherwise accumulate a failure witness (policy violation, shape mismatch, or unsupported carrier).
    4. Aggregate results as a recognition set plus failure diagnostics.

Invariants

  • Safe probing: no mutation of the section or trace; respects policy/trace constraints while enumerating candidates.
  • Determinate: given the same section/policy/criteria, produces the same recognitions/failures up to nucleus equivalence.
  • Explicit failure on unknown/unsupported carrier types.

Proof

  • Uses: recognition operators on the sheaf section, plus policy/trace carriers, to decide admissibility and shape matching.
  • Shows that: systematic probing of admissible subwindows with earned recognition operators yields either recognition carriers for discovered shapes or failure witnesses when discovery is disallowed or unsupported.

Dispatch Expression

Dispatches an expression to the appropriate evaluator (literal, operator call, struct construction, control form) within an Evaluation State and Evaluator.

Proof

  • Uses: pattern matching on expression shape (literal, call, struct, control) within an evaluation state to select the evaluator.
  • Shows that: expression evaluation can be factored through a single dispatch point, keeping the kernel small; cases will be refined explicitly.

title: Dispatch Operator id: dispatch-operator role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: operator-id alias: target
  • id: argument-list alias: args outputs:
  • id: runtime-value alias: result transformation:

    • sig
      • call
        • resolve-operator-signature
        • target
    • checked-args
      • call
        • validate-arguments
        • sig
        • args
