      • call
        • apply-operator
        • target
        • checked-args
    • return
      • result proof_refs:
  • operator-id
  • argument-list
  • runtime-value
  • operator-signature
  • resolve-operator-signature
  • validate-arguments
  • apply-operator

Dispatch Operator

Placeholder for invoking an operator by id with arguments. To be refined with signature/arity/type checks and actual invocation semantics.

Proof

  • Uses: operator identifiers and argument lists.
  • Shows that: calls can be routed through a single dispatcher in the kernel; further refinement will add signature checks and actual operator invocation.

title: Distributed Path Compose id: distributed-path-compose role: Compose paths across a distributional coverage site with refinement/gluing. status: plan inputs:

  • id: distributional-coverage-site name: site
  • id: path-payload name: path-a
  • id: path-payload name: path-b outputs:
  • id: path-payload name: composed transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Distributed Path Compose

Orchestrates the parameterized path composition lift across a Distributional Coverage Site.

Definition

  • Inputs: two routed path payloads whose endpoints align and whose cover identifiers share a refinement; access to restriction services for both ends.
  • Outputs: a composed path payload with provenance showing which nodes executed reparameterization and gluing.
  • Behaviour: schedules restriction to a common overlap, invokes the Path Composition Lift with distribution-aware parameters, then replicates the result to interested ports.

Preconditions

  • Coverage metadata is synchronized across nodes so refinement/overlap tables are consistent.
  • Failure channels connected to propagate incompatibility or missing overlaps.

Postconditions

  • Successful composition yields a path payload ready for machine-level runtime execution; failures are emitted with enough provenance for replay.
  • Matching-family caches in the distributional coverage site are updated or invalidated coherently.

Proof

  • Uses: path-composition-lift plus overlap/restrict operators on the distributional coverage site to refine/glue paths.
  • Shows that: refining both paths to a common overlap and gluing yields a composed path with recorded provenance, suitable for downstream execution.

title: Distribution Routing id: distribution-route role: Enqueue distribution packets on compatible channels. status: plan inputs:

  • id: distribution-packet-carrier name: packet
  • id: distribution-channel-carrier name: channel outputs:
  • id: distribution-channel-carrier name: channel' transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Distribution Routing

Routes packets to channels respecting port compatibility, trace, and storage constraints.

Definition

  • Inputs: Distribution Packet.
  • Outputs: updated Distribution Channel with packet queued, or no-op if incompatible.
  • Behaviour: select channels whose ports are compatible with the packet endpoints and whose trace/storage constraints are satisfied; enqueue packet preserving trace order and integrity.

Invariants

  • Port compatibility: only enqueue on matching port types/policy.
  • Trace preservation: channel trace spans updated consistently.
  • Integrity: storage checks must pass before enqueue.

Proof

  • Uses: channel/packet compatibility checks and enqueue semantics to preserve trace/integrity.
  • Shows that: routing packets along compatible ports with trace/integrity constraints refines the distribution acts.

title: Do id: do role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: argument-list alias: expressions
  • id: evaluation-state alias: state outputs:
  • id: runtime-value alias: result transformation:
  • let
    • results
      • call
        • map-evaluate
        • expressions
        • state
    • return
      • call
        • last
        • results proof_refs:
  • map-evaluate
  • last
  • argument-list
  • runtime-value
  • evaluation-state

Do

Sequence operator that evaluates an Argument List of expressions in order and yields the value of the last one.

Proof

  • Uses: evaluation over expression lists (map-evaluate) and selection of the last value (last).
  • Shows that: sequencing expressions yields the final expression’s value, providing ordered evaluation within the nested-array interpreter.

title: Documentation Measurement id: documentation-measure role: operator status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/function/evaluate-step inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Documentation Measurement

The Documentation Measurement operator executes the measurement stage of the Documentation Program over a Documentation Workspace. It refines the computer Evaluate Step by running a documentation measurement (for example, a linter) to compute metrics about frontmatter, links, and layer discipline.

Inputs

  • Documentation Workspace (documentation corpus plus dependency graph).
  • Documentation Style constraints to enforce vocabulary/metadata rules.

Outputs

  • Documentation Measurement Report summarising blocking/refactor counts, issue frequencies, and top destabilizing files.
  • Computer Failure when the workspace cannot be evaluated (corrupt payload, invalid wiring, or measurement failure).

Behaviour

  1. Validate the snapshot against style constraints (frontmatter present, links resolvable, layer/kind consistency).
  2. Run measurement (the linter) over the snapshot to collect counts and scores.
  3. Produce a measurement report ordered by proximity to acts (most destabilizing last), including per-file YAML frontmatter conformance and broken-link details.
  4. Emit Computer Failure if the measurement cannot complete.

Proof

Refines computer/function/evaluate-step.md by fixing the evaluation domain to documentation workspaces and the computation to measurement (linter) execution. The refinement preserves evaluate-step semantics (single-step evaluation with possible failure) while specialising inputs/outputs to documentation carriers.

title: Documentation Nucleation id: documentation-nucleation role: operator status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/function/wiring inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Documentation Nucleation

The Documentation Nucleation operator executes a Documentation Program to rewrite documentation workspaces toward atomic, well-linked forms while preserving the wiring constraints inherited from the computer layer.

Inputs

  • A Documentation Program (an acyclic wiring over documentation-maintenance operators).
  • Style constraints from Documentation Style.
  • Documentation Workspace + Documentation Session.
  • Documentation Command stream describing pending rewrites.

Outputs

  • A rewritten Documentation Program whose workspace and ports satisfy the style discipline.
  • Updated Documentation Transcript capturing applied commands.
  • Computer Failure when wiring or recognition obligations fail.

Behaviour

  1. Validate the documentation program wiring (acyclic, port/arity correctness).
  2. Traverse the workspace, applying recognition and evaluation steps to enforce frontmatter, linking, and vocabulary invariants.
  3. Apply Documentation Command stream to split/rewire snapshots until each corresponds to an earned structure/operator (or is explicitly marked provisional).
  4. Emit Computer Failure when a wiring edge cannot be satisfied or recognition rejects a command.

Proof

Refines computer/function/wiring.md by constraining the operator set to documentation-maintenance operators and constraining ports to documentation carriers. Because the underlying plan remains acyclic and uses the same kernel semantics, the nucleation operator preserves the wiring invariants while specialising behaviour to documentation rewrites and transcript capture.

title: Documentation Refinement id: documentation-refinement role: Refine documentation graph nodes to eliminate defects while preserving layer/dependency rules. status: plan inputs:

  • id: documentation-graph name: doc-graph outputs:
  • id: documentation-graph name: refined-graph transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Documentation Refinement

The Documentation Refinement operator is the system-level flow over the documentation graph that identifies defective docs and steps them toward an atomic, well-typed state.

Definition

  • Predicates:
    • WellTyped(d): the doc respects its layer/kind, states exactly one structure/operator, and uses only earned vocabulary.
    • Defect(d): negation of WellTyped(d), classified into defect kinds (atomicity, instantiation, metadata, vocabulary) ordered by priority.
  • Function Next(d): selects nearest defective docs from d, ordered by proximity to acts and defect priority.
  • Relation Refine ⊆ |D| × |D|: single refinement steps (splitting, tightening deps, aligning content).

Behaviour

  • Given a slice of the documentation graph, compute Next, choose a target, apply a Refine step to reduce defects, and update the graph.

Invariants

  • Refinement steps reduce or eliminate defects.
  • Layer and dependency rules remain satisfied after each step.

Proof

  • Uses: detect-doc-defects to classify defects, select-next-doc to prioritise targets, and apply-refine-step to perform defect-reducing edits on the Documentation Graph.
  • Shows that: each refinement step reduces defects while preserving layer/dependency/vocabulary invariants, yielding a progressively refined documentation graph.

title: Encode Section id: encode-section role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Encode Section

Applies the section encoding schema to produce a canonical serialized form.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Hypertensor Section and the Section Encoding Schema.
  • Output: same section annotated with canonical encoding (or a digest).
  • Behaviour: order fields per schema, serialize, and attach hashes/digests.

Invariants

  • Round-trip fidelity: decode(encode(section)) = section.
  • Canonical: same section yields identical encoding.

Proof

  • Uses: schema rules to serialize consistently.
  • Produces: encoded section data.
  • Shows that: sections can be deterministically encoded.

title: Encode Snapshot Payload id: encode-snapshot-payload role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: snapshot alias: snapshot outputs:
  • id: snapshot alias: encoded
  • id: failure-carrier alias: failure transformation:
  • return
    • encoded
    • failure proof_refs:
  • snapshot
  • failure-carrier

Encode Snapshot Payload

Validates snapshot schema and computes serialization/integrity data, producing an encoded snapshot or failure.

Invariants

  • Preserves fidelity: encoded payload round-trips to the same snapshot content.
  • Integrity-ready: returns hash-ready payload alongside any validation failure.

title: Encode Snapshot id: encode-snapshot role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: snapshot alias: snapshot outputs:
  • id: snapshot alias: encoded
  • id: failure-carrier alias: failure transformation:
  • call
    • primitive-encode-snapshot
    • snapshot proof_refs:
  • snapshot
  • failure-carrier
  • primitive-encode-snapshot

Encode Snapshot

Serializes and validates snapshot fields (id, version, timestamp, provenance, hash, recognitions, evaluation state), yielding an encoded snapshot or failure.

Proof

  • Uses: schema validation/hashing via the primitive encoder.
  • Shows that: encoding is explicit and failure is surfaced without mutation.

title: Enforce Linearity id: enforce-linearity role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Enforce Linearity

Enforces linear/affine resource constraints during flow/interleaving.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Linearity Constraint and flow steps to be executed.
  • Output: updated constraint witness ensuring no duplication/discard violations.
  • Behaviour: check each flow step; reject or mark violations if linear resources are copied/discarded improperly.

Invariants

  • Soundness: all accepted flows respect linearity.
  • Compositional: constraints update coherently across interleavings.

Proof

  • Uses: linearity annotations and flow steps.
  • Produces: constraint witness or failure.
  • Shows that: linearity can be enforced in concurrent flows.

title: Enforce Local Rules id: enforce-local-rules role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Enforce Local Rules

Checks/enforces quasicrystalline local rules on hypertensor sections.

Definition

  • Inputs: a section over a window and a set of local rules from Quasicrystalline Constraint.
  • Outputs: verified/annotated constraint satisfaction.

Invariants

  • Local: enforcement uses only finite window data.
  • Stable under restriction: if a section satisfies rules on a window, its restrictions satisfy them on sub-windows.

Proof

  • Uses: Quasicrystalline Constraint.
  • Shows that: local rules can be enforced/checked on sections, yielding the quasicrystalline subclass.

title: Ensure Arity And Types id: ensure-arity-and-types role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: operator-signature alias: signature
  • id: argument-list alias: args outputs:
  • id: argument-list alias: checked-args transformation:
  • return
    • args proof_refs:
  • operator-signature
  • argument-list
  • judgment-typing

Ensure Arity And Types

Checks arity and carrier compatibility of an argument list against an operator signature. The check is structural (matching declared input count and carrier ids) and either returns the arguments unchanged (when valid) or would route to failure in a fuller evaluator.

Proof

  • Uses: the typing judgement inside Operator Signature.
  • Shows that: argument conformity (arity and carrier match) is decidable before execution, preventing malformed calls from entering operator transformations.

title: Ensure Overlap Agreement id: ensure-overlap-agreement role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: coverage-site alias: site
  • id: matching-family alias: family outputs:
  • id: matching-family alias: family transformation:
  • call
    • validate-overlaps
    • site
    • family proof_refs:
  • coverage-site
  • matching-family

Proof

  • Uses: overlap charts and restriction maps from the coverage site to compare restricted sections.
  • Shows that: the family satisfies pairwise/triple coherence, a prerequisite for sheaf gluing.

Ensure Overlap Agreement

Verifies that the sections in a Matching Family agree on all overlaps defined by the Coverage Site.

Proof

  • Uses: overlap charts and restriction maps from the coverage site to compare restricted sections via validate-overlaps.
  • Shows that: the family satisfies pairwise/triple coherence, a prerequisite for sheaf gluing.

title: Entropy Lens id: entropy-lens role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Entropy Lens

Transforms a recognition by weighting it through entropy—an entropy-filter map.

Definition

  • Inputs: recognition and Entropy Functional.
  • Output: entropy-weighted recognition (e.g., emphasising low/high entropy components).
  • Behaviour: apply entropy-derived weights/modifiers to the recognition’s components.

Invariants

  • Normalisation: transformed recognition remains in carrier.
  • Monotone: entropy weighting respects ordering if defined.

Proof

  • Uses: entropy functional to guide transformation.
  • Produces: filtered recognition.
  • Shows that: entropy can filter/modulate recognitions.

title: Epistemic Update id: epistemic-update role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Epistemic Update

Update an epistemic profile with a new observation by restricting to compatible states and flowing evidence into context.

Definition

  • Inputs: an Epistemic Profile and an Observation.
  • Output: an Epistemic Profile narrowed to states consistent with the observation.
  • Behaviour:
    1. Restrict the epistemic profile to recognitions compatible with the observation.
    2. Apply Flow Context to propagate the evidence through the profile’s context/nucleus.
    3. Optionally normalise with the know modality for the agent.

Invariants

  • Consistency: updated profile contains only states consistent with the observation.
  • Monotone: successive updates refine (never enlarge) the epistemic profile.
  • Stability: updates commute with admissible reindexing in the profile topos.

Proof

  • Uses: Epistemic Profile as carrier, Observation as evidence, and Flow Context to propagate updates.
  • Produces: refined Epistemic Profile.
  • Shows that: epistemic evidence can be integrated via restriction and flow, preserving modal/recognitional invariants.

title: Equate Judgment id: equate-judgment role: operator status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/normalized-judgement-equivalence inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Equate Judgment

The Equate Judgment operator establishes judgmental equivalence (CIU-style) over judgment triads.

Definition

  • Input: two judgment triads.
  • Output: a judgment triad witnessing their equivalence.
  • Behaviour: applies normalized/contextual/witness equivalence acts to show the triads are equivalent; closes the loop via recognize-judgment triad.

Invariants

  • Equivalent judgments share witnesses and observational content.
  • Equivalence respects head/tail/contextual cases.

Proof

Derived from the judgment equivalence acts (normalized, contextual, witness), showing the required cases and returning a triad-level equivalence witness.

title: Evaluate Arguments id: evaluate-arguments role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: argument-list alias: expr-list
  • id: evaluation-state alias: state outputs:
  • id: runtime-value alias: values transformation:
  • return
  • values proof_refs:
  • argument-list
  • evaluation-state
  • runtime-value
  • evaluate-expression

Evaluate Arguments

Applies evaluate-expression to each expression in an Argument List using the provided evaluation state, returning the list of values.

Proof

  • Uses: interpreter loop to iterate over the list with the shared evaluation state.
  • Shows that: sequencing over lists is available as a documented operator.

title: Evaluate Computer Step id: evaluate-step role: One-step evaluator over a wiring plan with explicit failure logging. status: plan inputs:

  • id: evaluation-state name: state
  • id: wiring-plan name: plan
  • id: trace-carrier name: trace outputs:
  • id: evaluation-state name: state'
  • id: failure-algebra name: failure transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Evaluate Computer Step

Single-step evaluator over an earned Evaluation State following a Wiring Plan, with failures recorded in the Failure Algebra.

Definition

  • Inputs: evaluation state containing ready queue, bindings, wiring-plan handle, and trace cursor; the wiring plan; the current trace.
  • Outputs: updated evaluation state or failure element.
  • Behaviour:
    1. Read the head task from the ready queue using the wiring plan’s topological order.
    2. Collect inputs by looking up bound carriers on incoming ports; if a port is unbound or type-mismatched, emit failure (missing-input/type-mismatch).
    3. Dispatch to the referenced earned operator (recognition, storage, wiring, or judgement evaluation) passing the collected carriers.
    4. Update bindings with the outputs, advance the trace cursor, and enqueue newly-ready tasks; on operator failure, record the failure in the failure algebra and leave bindings unchanged.

Invariants

  • Subject reduction/progress preserved by using only earned operators.
  • On failure, no state mutation of bindings; failure is logged in the failure algebra with trace provenance.
  • Deterministic: same wiring plan, inputs, and trace yield nucleus-equivalent successor state.

Proof

  • Uses: Wiring Plan for ordering, earned evaluation operators for computation, and Failure Algebra for error accumulation.
  • Shows that: one-step evaluation following the wiring plan preserves typing and either advances the evaluation state or records a failure without mutating bindings.

title: Evaluate Expression id: evaluate-expression role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: expression alias: expr
  • id: evaluation-state alias: state outputs:
  • id: runtime-value alias: value transformation:
  • call
    • dispatch-expression
    • expr
    • state proof_refs:
  • evaluation-state
  • dispatch-expression
  • expression
  • runtime-value

Evaluate Expression

Evaluates a nested-array expression in an Evaluation State, producing its value. This is the core interpreter step over the documentation DAG within the earned evaluator.

Proof

  • Uses: dispatch-expression to interpret literals, operator calls, structs, control forms, etc., relative to the evaluation state/evaluator.
  • Shows that: every expression can be reduced to a value using only documented operators and earned carriers; no hidden primitives are assumed.

title: Evaluate Free Energy id: evaluate-free-energy role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Evaluate Free Energy

Typed evaluation of the free energy functional on a thermodynamic state.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Thermodynamic State and the Free Energy Typed Operator.
  • Output: recognition value (energy/ordering) in the Recognition Carrier.
  • Behaviour: combine entropy/observable values per the free-energy definition; emit typed recognition.

Invariants

  • Type safety: only applicable to thermodynamic/observable carriers.
  • Monotonicity: respects entropy/observable algebra laws.

Proof

  • Uses: free-energy typed operator on the thermodynamic state.
  • Produces: recognition result.
  • Shows that: free-energy evaluation is well-defined and typed.

title: Evaluate Judgement id: evaluate-judgement role: operator status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/evaluate-judgement inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Evaluate Judgement

Evaluates flowed judgements as stabilised values, earned by EvaluateJudgement.

Proof

Sketch: when a judgement flows (FlowJudgement), apply EvaluateJudgement using earned value recognitions (RecognizeValue). Show the step maps a judgement term to a value in JudgmentValue, respecting the evaluation structure.

title: Evaluate Observable id: evaluate-observable role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Evaluate Observable

Evaluates an observable operator on a section, producing a typed recognition result (probability/entropy/free energy).

Definition

  • Inputs: a section in Hypertensor Section and an operator from Observable Typed Operator.
  • Output: recognition value in Recognition Carrier.
  • Behaviour: apply the observable algebra operation per typing rules; emit recognition representing the numeric/semantic result.

Invariants

  • Type safety: only well-typed observables applied.
  • Semantic correctness: result matches observable algebra semantics.

Proof

  • Uses: observable typed operators and section inputs.
  • Produces: recognition result.
  • Shows that: observable computations can be performed soundly within the type system.

title: Evaluate Section Step id: evaluate-section-step role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: hypertensor-section alias: section
  • id: evaluation-context-stack alias: stack outputs:
  • id: hypertensor-section alias: next-section
  • id: evaluation-context-stack alias: next-stack transformation:
  • let
    • frame
      • call
        • top-frame
        • stack
    • reduced
      • call
        • apply-frame
        • frame
        • section
    • next-stack
      • call
        • update-stack
        • stack
        • reduced
    • return
      • reduced
      • next-stack proof_refs:
  • hypertensor-section
  • evaluation-context-stack
  • top-frame
  • apply-frame
  • update-stack

Evaluate Section Step

Small-step evaluation of a hypertensor section under an evaluation context stack.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Hypertensor Section and an Evaluation Context Stack.
  • Outputs: updated section and stack after one evaluation step.
  • Behaviour: apply the top frame to the section using admissible Flow Context; update the stack accordingly (push/pop).

Invariants

  • Progress: either a step occurs or the section is in normal form (per stack).
  • Preservation: evaluation step stays within admissible flow/nucleus on the section.
  • Determinism: given section and stack, the next state is determined.

Proof

  • Uses: evaluation frames (stack) and flow rules on sections.
  • Produces: next section/stack state.
  • Shows that: small-step semantics over sections is well-defined and respects section invariants.

title: Evaluate Section id: evaluate-section role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: hypertensor-section alias: section
  • id: evaluation-context-stack alias: stack outputs:
  • id: hypertensor-section alias: result-section
  • id: evaluation-context-stack alias: result-stack transformation:
  • let
    • step
      • call
        • evaluate-section-step
        • section
        • stack
    • return
      • step proof_refs:
  • evaluate-section-step
  • hypertensor-section
  • evaluation-context-stack

Evaluate Section

Big-step evaluation of a hypertensor section to a value/normal form.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Hypertensor Section and an Evaluation Context Stack.
  • Output: evaluated section in normal form (or failure), with stack emptied.
  • Behaviour: iterate Evaluate Section Step until normal form or failure per failure algebra.

Invariants

  • Termination on finite sections or diverges; preserves types when typed.
  • Determinism given an evaluation strategy.

Proof

  • Uses: small-step semantics to compose big-step.
  • Produces: final section result.
  • Shows that: big-step evaluation is well-defined and aligns with small-step rules.

title: Evaluate With Bindings id: evaluate-with-bindings role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: evaluation-state alias: state
  • id: binding-environment alias: bindings
  • id: expression alias: body outputs:
  • id: runtime-value alias: result transformation:
  • let
    • extended
      • call
        • extend-bindings
        • state
        • bindings
    • result
      • call
        • evaluate-expression
        • body
        • extended
    • return
      • result proof_refs:
  • evaluation-state
  • binding-environment
  • expression
  • extend-bindings
  • evaluate-expression

Evaluate With Bindings

Evaluates an expression under provided bindings (environment), producing a runtime value.

Proof

  • Uses: bindings to extend the evaluation environment when interpreting the expression.
  • Shows that: local bindings can be incorporated into evaluation in the kernel.

title: Evaluation Context Compatibility id: evaluation-context-compatibility role: operator status: plan derived_from: acts/build-evaluation-context inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Evaluation Context Compatibility

Definition

  • Inputs: earned evaluation contexts from Build Evaluation Context.
  • Outputs: guarantee that reducibility/normalization proofs respect these contexts.
  • Behaviour: ensures operational steps are compatible with the evaluation-context structure.

Invariants

  • Evaluation contexts remain well-formed under reduction/normalization.
  • Flow/normalization steps preserve context shape.

Proof

  • Derived from: Build Evaluation Context.
  • Uses: Flow Normalization Compatibility to align flow with normalization inside contexts.
  • Shows that: reducibility and normalization respect earned evaluation contexts in the judgement calculus.

title: Evaluation Normalisation tags:

  • evaluation
  • normalization layer: internal-theorem status: plan depends_on:
  • math/structure/evaluation-state
  • math/operator/strong-normalization
  • math/operator/confluence claims:
  • type: operator judgement: norm : EvalState ⇀ EvalState selects normal states when they exist

Evaluation Normalisation

Statement

There exists a partial operator

norm : EvalState ⇀ EvalState

that selects the normal state reached by iterating step when strong normalization applies.

Definition

  • Iterates step from an initial state; by strong normalization, a normal state is reached when the program is in the earned fragment.
  • By confluence, this normal state is unique up to earned equivalence; norm selects it.

Uniqueness

With EvalState, step, strong normalization, and confluence fixed, norm is unique in the earned sense.

title: Evaluation Step tags:

  • evaluation
  • reduction layer: internal-theorem status: plan depends_on:
  • math/structure/evaluation-state
  • theorem/internal/substitution-discipline
  • theorem/internal/reduction-normalization-structure claims:
  • type: operator judgement: step : EvalState ⇀ EvalState

Evaluation Step

Statement

There exists a partial operator

step : EvalState ⇀ EvalState

that applies the earned primitive reduction to the focused term and updates the evaluation state accordingly, using substitution discipline and reduction structure.

Definition by acts

  • Applies the primitive step relation to the focused term.
  • Uses substitution discipline to compute the new term.
  • Returns a state with the same or updated frame per admissible frame operations from the evaluation-context structure.

Uniqueness

Given EvalState, substitution discipline, and reduction structure, step is fixed up to earned equivalence.

title: Exchange Flow id: exchange-flow role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Exchange Flow

Applies a crossflow exchange when legal, producing shuffle-equivalent outcomes.

Definition

  • Inputs: two flow segments and an CrossFlow Exchange witness.
  • Output: Trace Shuffle Class representing the exchanged interleavings.
  • Behaviour: swap flow order when allowed; otherwise emit violation/neutral.

Invariants

  • Commutation: valid exchanges yield equivalent traces up to shuffle/nucleus.
  • Detection: rejects exchanges that violate commutation constraints.

Proof

  • Uses: crossflow exchange data to decide swaps.
  • Produces: shuffle class of exchanged flows.
  • Shows that: flow swapping is controlled by earned exchange laws.

title: Execute Command id: execute-command role: operator status: someday derived_from: computer/function/evaluate-step inputs:

  • computer/library/type/mud-session
  • computer/library/type/mud-command
  • computer/library/type/mud-world-state outputs:
  • computer/library/type/mud-session
  • computer/library/type/mud-world-state
  • computer/type/computer-failure transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Execute Command

Applies a validated MUD Command to the MUD World State within a MUD Session.

Definition

  • Inputs: MUD Session with ready command queue and a MUD Command to execute; world bindings.
  • Outputs: updated session and world state, or Computer Failure.
  • Behaviour:
    1. Check preconditions (permissions, cooldowns, reachability).
    2. Apply world mutation (move, speak, combat, item use) respecting trace ordering and measurement/recognition invariants.
    3. Update session bindings (location, effects) and enqueue resulting events.
    4. On failure, emit failure and roll back any partial mutation.

Invariants

  • Subject reduction: world/session remain well-typed under MUD schemas.
  • Ordering: execution respects evaluation/trace order; no out-of-order writes.
  • Failure atomicity: failures leave world/session unchanged.

Proof

  • Derived from: Computer Evaluate Step.
  • Uses: MUD Session, MUD Command, MUD World State.
  • Shows that: refining the computer evaluation step with MUD-specific semantics yields safe command execution over the world/session carriers.

Execute Transformation

Runs the documented transformation attached to an operator id on a validated argument list.

Proof

  • Uses: the interpreter’s transformation table keyed by operator-id.
  • Shows that: every earned operator can be reduced mechanically once its id and arguments are known.

Expression Children

  • Shows that: expression arguments can be extracted for evaluation/dispatch.

Expression Tag

Returns the tag/head of a normalised nested-array Expression.

Proof

  • Uses: expression structure.
  • Shows that: the tag can be extracted for dispatch.

title: Extend Bindings id: extend-bindings role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: evaluation-state alias: state
  • id: runtime-value alias: bindings outputs:
  • id: evaluation-state alias: extended transformation:
  • call
    • merge-bindings
    • state
    • bindings proof_refs:
  • evaluation-state
  • runtime-value
  • merge-bindings

Extend Bindings

Produces a new Evaluation State whose bindings map is extended with the supplied binding map.

Proof

  • Uses: the binding map structure in evaluation-state.
  • Shows that: local bindings can be layered without mutating the original state.

title: Extend Semantics id: extend-semantics role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Extend Semantics

Applies controlled extensions to a computer wiring plan’s semantics (e.g., adding new annotations or derived edges) under explicit policy/trace constraints.

Definition

  • Inputs: Wiring Plan, Evaluation State, current Policy Regime, and Trace Carrier.
  • Outputs: updated wiring plan or Failure Algebra element.
  • Behaviour:
    1. Validate proposed extensions (annotations, derived edges) against the wiring plan typing rules and policy constraints.
    2. For each extension, check acyclicity and port-type compatibility; reject those that violate constraints, recording failure with trace provenance.
    3. Acceptable extensions are added to the wiring plan with audit tags; the evaluation state’s ready queue is updated if new edges enable tasks.

Invariants

  • No arbitrary operator insertion; only permitted extensions/annotations.
  • Acyclicity and typing preserved; failures on violation.
  • Changes are explicit and auditable (trace-tagged).

Proof

  • Uses: wiring plan typing/acyclicity rules and policy regime constraints to filter extensions.
  • Shows that: applying only admissible extensions preserves wiring plan invariants; violations are reported via the failure algebra without altering the plan.

title: Extract Aperiodic Germ id: extract-aperiodic-germ role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Extract Aperiodic Germ

Extracts a minimal aperiodic window that can generate a global section.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Hypertensor Section with aperiodic features.
  • Output: Aperiodic Germ.
  • Behaviour: search for minimal windows exhibiting aperiodicity sufficient for reconstruction; validate via sheaf gluing.

Invariants

  • Minimality: produced germ cannot be shrunk further.
  • Reconstruction: germ extends to the original section via sheaf/coverage rules.

Proof

  • Uses: section/window coverage and aperiodicity checks.
  • Produces: minimal germ.
  • Shows that: aperiodic seeds can be extracted for reconstruction.

title: Extract Graph Edges id: extract-graph-edges role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: edge-set alias: edges transformation:
  • return
    • edges proof_refs:
  • connection-graph
  • edge-set
  • primitive-extract-graph-edges

Extract Graph Edges

Extracts the Edge Set from a Connection Graph, capturing endpoint port ids and carrier types.

Proof

  • Uses: edge data stored in the graph.
  • Shows that: edges can be validated and checked for acyclicity separately from other graph data.

  • id: connection-graph alias: graph outputs:
  • id: port-set alias: ports transformation:
  • return
    • ports proof_refs:
  • connection-graph
  • port-set
  • primitive-extract-graph-ports

Extract Graph Ports

Extracts the Port Set from a Connection Graph, expanding operator signatures into typed input/output ports.

Proof

  • Uses: step signatures in the graph to enumerate ports.
  • Shows that: port data can be separated for independent typing/wiring checks.

title: Primitive Failure Absorb id: primitive-failure-absorb role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: runtime-value alias: payload outputs:
  • id: runtime-value alias: result transformation:
  • return
  • result proof_refs:
  • runtime-value

Primitive Failure Absorb

Implements the absorption law of the failure algebra: if payload contains a failure element, returns that failure; otherwise returns the original payload.

Proof

  • Uses: failure carrier inspection.
  • Shows that: absorption can be realized as a primitive check.

title: Find Fixed Profile Chain id: find-fixed-profile-chain role: operator status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/ensure-fixed-point inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Find Fixed Profile Chain

The Find Fixed Profile Chain operator computes a fixed distribution for the Profile Kernel Chain.

Definition

  • Inputs: Profile Kernel Chain.
  • Outputs: stabilised Profile Kernel Chain representing the fixed point.
  • Behaviour: applies fixed-point machinery from Ensure Fixed Point to the profile kernel chain to compute its fixed distribution.

Invariants

  • Fixed chain is unique up to stabilised equivalence.
  • Preserves interaction with fixed geometry at each profile level; respects flow/nucleus compatibility of the underlying kernels.

Proof

Derived from acts/ensure-fixed-point, which guarantees existence/uniqueness of a fixed distribution for the chain under the earned conditions.

title: Find Hypertensor Fixed Point id: find-hypertensor-fixed-point role: operator status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/ensure-fixed-point inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Find Hypertensor Fixed Point

The Find Hypertensor Fixed Point operator computes a fixed point of a composed hypertensor dynamics endomorphism.

Definition

  • Input: a composite of hypertensor endomorphisms.
  • Output: a fixed endomorphism/section representing a stationary state.
  • Behaviour: applies fixed-point machinery to the endomorphism monoid to find a stabilised element.

Invariants

  • Fixed point is nucleus-equivalent to any other earned fixed point under the same dynamics.
  • Respects sheaf structure and tower/modal constraints.

Proof

By acts/ensure-fixed-point, fixed points exist for the earned endomorphism compositions under the given conditions. This operator packages that fixed-point computation over the hypertensor sheaf.

title: Fixed Observation Normalization id: fixed-observation-normalization role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Fixed Observation Normalization

Statement

The act fixed-normalize-observation applied to fixed observables earns that their associated reduction traces stabilize along StepTrace.

Proof sketch

FixedNormalizeObservation = (Normalization FixedObserve) collapses any admissible reduction on fixed observables; since domains/codomains are in the fixed profile, the normalization is internal to that profile.

Uniqueness

Given FixedObservationCarrier and the normalization act, this property is fixed up to earned equivalence.

title: Witness Fixed Observation id: fixed-observation-witness role: operator status: plan derived_from: acts/fixed-witness-observation inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Witness Fixed Observation

Statement

The act fixed-witness-observation restricted to Fixed Observation witnesses that contextual equivalence on the fixed profile can be tested using only fixed observations.

Definition

  • Inputs: Fixed Observation and the base Observation Witness over the full profile.
  • Outputs: Fixed Observation Witness, certifying contextual equivalence in the fixed profile.
  • Behaviour: applies Fixed Witness Observation to restrict the general witness to fixed observations, demonstrating that the fixed-profile carrier suffices.

Invariants

  • Uses only fixed-profile observations; no unfixed observations are admitted.
  • Witnesses remain within the fixed observation carrier and respect fixed-profile restrictions.
  • Restriction-compatible: the fixed witness is obtained by restricting the full observation witness; no new observational power is introduced.

Proof

  • Derived from: Fixed Witness Observation.
  • Uses: Observation Witness as the full-profile witness to restrict.
  • Shows that: restricting the witness to Fixed Observation suffices to decide contextual equivalence in the fixed profile because the equate-contexts act is closed under fixed-profile restriction; outputs are the fixed observation witnesses used for that decision.

title: Flow Context id: flow-context role: operator status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/context-flow inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Flow Context

The Flow Context operator evolves a Context along the earned profile flow.

Definition

  • Input: a context recognition.
  • Output: a context recognition advanced along the profile flow.
  • Behaviour: applies the flow profile induced by acts/context-flow to carry contexts forward without leaving the earned context carrier.

Proof

By construction from acts/context-flow, this operator is the earned flow on contexts. It preserves the admissible structure of contexts while advancing them along the induced profile flow.

title: Flow Drift id: flow-drift role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Flow Drift

Detects gradual change under flow, measured as nucleus-stable shift per step.

Definition

  • Inputs: recognition and Flow Context; metric via Relational Metric.
  • Output: recognition of drift magnitude/direction (e.g., per-step displacement).
  • Behaviour: measure displacement after flow step(s), collapse via nucleus, express as recognition.

Invariants

  • Monotone with accumulated steps.
  • Zero drift for fixed points; bounded for stable flows.

Proof

  • Uses: flow and metric to quantify drift.
  • Produces: drift recognition.
  • Shows that: slow changes under flow are measurable and recognisable.

title: Flow Interference Cost id: flow-interference-cost role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Flow Interference Cost

Evaluates cost/penalty of superposed flows using interference patterns and flow potential.

Definition

  • Inputs: two flows, their Flow Interference Pattern, and current Flow Potential.
  • Output: updated potential reflecting interference cost.
  • Behaviour: detect constructive/destructive interference; adjust potential to reflect scheduling cost/benefit.

Invariants

  • Increased potential for destructive/interfering patterns.
  • Composable: multiple interference assessments compose additively/monotonically.

Proof

  • Uses: interference pattern and potential to compute cost adjustment.
  • Produces: updated flow potential.
  • Shows that: interference effects can be incorporated into cost planning.

title: Flow Judgment id: flow-judgment role: operator status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/flow-judgement inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Flow Judgment

The Flow Judgment operator transports judgment traces through evaluation contexts.

Definition

  • Input: a judgment triad trace.
  • Output: a judgment triad trace advanced along evaluation context flow.
  • Behaviour: applies the flow-judgment act to move judgment traces through evaluation contexts, preserving admissibility.

Invariants

  • Flow preserves judgment structure and admissibility.
  • Judgments remain within the triad carrier.

Proof

Derived from acts/flow-judgement, which defines how judgment traces evolve under evaluation context flow.

title: “Flow\u2013Normalization Compatibility” id: flow-normalization-compatibility role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Flow–Normalization Compatibility

Statement

On a stratified ∞-site with admissible morphisms Adm and normal-form map N∞ : Adm → Adm, any earned flow F_t (compatible with stratification, coverage, and admissibility) commutes with normalization up to nucleus equivalence:

F_t ∘ N∞ ≃ N∞ ∘ F_t on Adm

Proof sketch

  • By flow–nucleus compatibility, F_t preserves nucleus classes on admissible morphisms and respects restriction/gluing.
  • By the normal-form structure, N∞ picks a canonical representative in each rewrite class.
  • Applying F_t then N∞, or N∞ then F_t, yields nucleus-equivalent morphisms because F_t cannot leave the admissible/coverage constraints and normalization collapses within each flow orbit.

Uniqueness

Given the earned flow–nucleus compatibility and the normal-form map, this commutation property is fixed in the earned sense.

title: “Flow\u2013Nucleus Commutation” id: flow-nucleus-commutation role: operator status: plan derived_from: acts/commute-flow-and-nucleus inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Flow–Nucleus Commutation

Statement

Flow and nucleus commute on admissible morphisms: applying the nucleus before or after flow yields the same stabilised recognition. The acts also witness the adjunction between flow and nucleus on admissible morphisms.

Definition

  • Inputs: Admissible Morphism.
  • Outputs: commutation/adjunction witness (law) for flow/nucleus on admissible morphisms.
  • Behaviour: enforces that N ∘ F = F ∘ N on admissible morphisms and that flow/nucleus form an adjoint pair, per the earned acts.

Invariants

  • Commutation holds: flow and nucleus can be reordered on admissible morphisms without changing the result.
  • Adjunction holds: flow is left adjoint to nucleus on admissible morphisms, as witnessed by the earned acts.

Proof

  • Derived from: Commute Flow and Nucleus.
  • Uses: Admissible Morphism to scope the commutation/adjunction.
  • Shows that: the commutation and adjunction laws earned by the acts apply to admissible morphisms, fixing the flow–nucleus interaction up to earned equivalence.

Given these acts, the flow–nucleus commutation/adjunction is fixed up to earned equivalence.

title: “Flow\u2013Nucleus Curvature” id: flow-nucleus-curvature role: operator status: plan derived_from: acts/comonad-curvature inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Flow–Nucleus Curvature

Statement

The earned acts extend the flow–nucleus pair to composite profiles and record the curvature (obstruction to strict idempotence).

Definition

  • Inputs: Admissible Morphism.
  • Outputs: curvature witness for the flow–nucleus comonad/monad pair on composite profiles.
  • Behaviour: lifts flow/nucleus to composite profiles and measures curvature via Comonad Curvature.

Invariants

  • Curvature is computed within the admissible morphism class and respects composite profile structure.
  • Captures the obstruction to strict idempotence/commutation of flow/nucleus on composite profiles.

Proof

  • Derived from: Comonad Curvature and the composite-profile lifts (form-geometry, flow-composite-profile, geometry-composite-profile).
  • Shows that: extending flow/nucleus to composite profiles yields a curvature witness on admissible morphisms, fixing the obstruction data up to earned equivalence.

title: “Flow\u2013Nucleus Distribution” id: flow-nucleus-distribution role: operator status: plan derived_from: acts/distribute-flow inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Flow–Nucleus Distribution

Statement

The earned acts show that nuclei and flows distribute over finite meets/joins and shuffles.

Definition

  • Inputs: Admissible Morphism, Trace Shuffle Class.
  • Outputs: Admissible Morphism after distributing flow/nucleus over joins/meets/shuffles.
  • Behaviour: applies the earned distribution acts (Absorb Closure, Distribute Interior, Equalize Nucleus Distribution, Distribute Flow) to show flow/nucleus commute with lattice operations and trace shuffles on admissible morphisms.

Invariants

  • Distribution holds: applying flow/nucleus before or after joins/meets/shuffles yields equivalent admissible morphisms.
  • Stability: resulting morphisms remain admissible and respect flow–nucleus compatibility.

Proof

  • Derived from: Distribute Flow and related distribution acts.
  • Shows that: flow/nucleus distribute over earned lattice operations and shuffles on admissible morphisms, fixing the distributive laws up to earned equivalence.

title: “Flow\u2013Nucleus Fixed Points” id: flow-nucleus-fixed role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Flow–Nucleus Fixed Points

Statement

The earned acts show that persistent iteration of flow and nucleus stabilises, the composites coincide, and fixed points transport along admissible morphisms.

Proof sketch

  • fix-flow, fix-nucleus, and fix-flow-nucleus show stabilization and coincidence of fixed points.
  • transport-flow-fix and transport-nucleus-fix carry fixed points along admissible morphisms.

Uniqueness

Given these acts, the fixed-point behaviour is fixed up to earned equivalence.

title: Flow Variance id: flow-variance role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Flow Variance

Computes how recognitions diverge under iterative flow—variance of uncertainty.

Definition

  • Inputs: an Uncertainty Profile and Flow Context.
  • Output: updated Uncertainty Profile reflecting spread after flow steps.
  • Behaviour: iterate flow, measure change in entropy/dispersion, update profile.

Invariants

  • Non-negativity: variance measures are ≥ 0.
  • Monotone w.r.t. informative collapse: variance decreases when flow collapses to fixed points.

Proof

  • Uses: uncertainty profile and flow dynamics.
  • Shows that: divergence/spread under flow is measurable in the relational setting.

title: Forgetting Operator id: forgetting-operator role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Forgetting Operator

Performs controlled loss of detail: projects recognitions onto persistent profiles and drops ephemeral parts.

Definition

  • Inputs: Temporal Recognizers and Persistent Recognition Carrier.
  • Output: pruned Temporal Recognizers retaining only persistent components.
  • Behaviour: identify non-persistent details, discard them, and stabilise the remaining recognitions.

Invariants

  • Safety: persistent/core recognitions are preserved.
  • Monotonic loss: forgetting only removes detail; never adds information.

Proof

  • Uses: temporal recognizers and persistent carriers to decide what to keep.
  • Produces: pruned temporal recognizers.
  • Shows that: forgetting can be formalised as projection onto persistent profiles.

title: Free Energy Evaluate id: free-energy-evaluate role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Free Energy Evaluate

Evaluates the free energy functional on a thermodynamic state.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Free Energy Functional and a Thermodynamic State.
  • Output: free-energy valuation (numeric or relational) suitable for comparing states.
  • Behaviour: combines entropy and observables from the state per the functional (e.g., F = E - T·S in numeric semantics).

Invariants

  • Monotonicity: along admissible flows, free energy decreases toward equilibrium when temperature/constraints are fixed.
  • Consistency: agrees with entropy monotonicity and observable algebra laws.

Proof

  • Uses: Free Energy Functional and Thermodynamic State.
  • Shows that: free-energy evaluation is well-defined over earned thermodynamic carriers.

title: Get Context Nucleus id: get-context-nucleus role: operator status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/context-nucleus inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Get Context Nucleus

The Get Context Nucleus operator maps a Context to its nucleus-equivalence class, identifying variations that are nucleus-stable.

Definition

  • Input: a context recognition.
  • Output: a context recognition representing its nucleus class.
  • Behaviour: applies the nucleus induced by acts/context-nucleus to identify contexts equivalent under nucleus-stable trace shuffles.

Proof

By construction from acts/context-nucleus, this operator computes the nucleus on contexts. It maps each context to its nucleus class, eliminating variations that do not change the earned nucleus invariant while remaining in the context carrier.

title: Get Graph Entry Steps id: get-graph-entry-steps role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: connection-graph alias: graph outputs:
  • id: runtime-value alias: entries transformation:
  • call
    • primitive-get-graph-entry-steps
    • graph proof_refs:
  • connection-graph
  • runtime-value
  • primitive-get-graph-entry-steps

Get Graph Entry Steps

Returns the list of entry step ids in a Connection Graph (steps with no incoming edges).

Proof

  • Uses: graph edge relation to find sources.
  • Shows that: entry points are computable as a primitive query.

title: Get Graph Exit Steps id: get-graph-exit-steps role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: connection-graph alias: graph outputs:
  • id: runtime-value alias: exits transformation:
  • call
    • primitive-get-graph-exit-steps
    • graph proof_refs:
  • connection-graph
  • runtime-value
  • primitive-get-graph-exit-steps

Get Graph Exit Steps

Returns the list of exit step ids in a Connection Graph (steps with no outgoing edges).

Proof

  • Uses: graph edge relation to find sinks.
  • Shows that: exit points are computable as a primitive query.

title: Global Convergence Operator id: global-convergence-operator role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Global Convergence Operator

Combines local snapshots into a consistent global recognition of state.

Definition

  • Inputs: local snapshots from Local Snapshot Operator and coordination via Snapshot Protocol.
  • Output: global Memory Carrier representing a consistent system snapshot.
  • Behaviour: reconcile local snapshots, align channel states, and assemble into a coherent global memory.

Invariants

  • Consistency: respects causality; no partial messages missing.
  • Idempotent: recombining already-consistent snapshots yields the same global state.

Proof

  • Uses: local snapshots plus snapshot protocol coordination.
  • Produces: global memory carrier.
  • Shows that: distributed state can be converged into a consistent global recognition.

title: Glue Parameterized Sheaf id: glue-parameterized-sheaf role: operator status: plan derived_from: math/operator/sheaf-glue inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Glue Parameterized Sheaf

Executable gluing operator for parameterized sheaves: amalgamates a validated matching family of parameterized sections into a global Parameterized Section.

Definition

  • Inputs:
    • A cover {Ui → U} in the Parameterized Coverage Site.
    • A Parameterized Matching Family of section payloads agreeing on overlaps.
  • Output: a Parameterized Section over U produced by calling the sheaf’s executable gluing routine.
  • Behaviour: reuses cached coherence witnesses, invokes the parameterized sheaf’s amalgamation API, and emits the resulting section with provenance for replay or distribution.

Preconditions

  • Matching family has been validated (or constructed) by Check Parameterized Matching Family.
  • Restriction and overlap functions for the cover are total and consistent.

Postconditions

  • The glued section restricts to each Ui as the corresponding input section.
  • Gluing is deterministic and unique for the supplied matching family; failure to glue must raise an explicit error (no silent drops).

Proof

  • Derived from: Glue Sheaf by instantiating cover, matching, and restriction data with parameterized executable forms.
  • Shows that: the earned sheaf condition can be executed in calculus, producing concrete sections from compatible local data.

title: Glue Sections id: glue-sections role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: hypertensor-section alias: left
  • id: hypertensor-section alias: right outputs:
  • id: hypertensor-section alias: glued transformation:
  • call
    • sheaf-glue
    • call
      • coverage-site
    • call
      • matching-family
      • left
      • right proof_refs:
  • hypertensor-section
  • sheaf-glue
  • matching-family

Glue Sections

Glues two compatible sections over overlapping windows into a single section, using the earned sheaf gluing operation.

Proof

  • Uses: Sheaf Glue over the Coverage Site to combine compatible sections.
  • Shows that: compatible local sections can be glued into a global section, preserving sheaf invariants.

title: Graph Cycle Search id: graph-cycle-search role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: edge-set alias: edges outputs:
  • id: recognition alias: cycle-witness transformation:
  • call
    • primitive-graph-cycle-search
    • edges proof_refs:
  • edge-set
  • recognition
  • primitive-graph-cycle-search

Graph Cycle Search

Primitive cycle finder over an Edge Set that produces a recognition witness when a directed cycle exists, or bottom when the graph is acyclic.

Proof

  • Uses: depth-first/back-edge detection over the directed edges.
  • Shows that: cycle presence can be witnessed as a recognition for higher-level acyclicity predicates.

title: Primitive Graph Cycle Search id: primitive-graph-cycle-search role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: edge-set alias: edges outputs:
  • id: recognition alias: cycle-witness transformation:
  • return
  • cycle-witness proof_refs:
  • edge-set
  • recognition

Primitive Graph Cycle Search

Performs cycle detection over an Edge Set, emitting a recognition witness of a directed cycle or bottom if none.

Proof

  • Uses: depth-first/back-edge search.
  • Shows that: cycle detection is available as a primitive for acyclicity checks.

title: Primitive Graph Edge Type Check id: primitive-graph-edge-type-check role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: edge-set alias: edges
  • id: port-set alias: ports outputs:
  • id: recognition alias: predicate transformation:
  • return
  • predicate proof_refs:
  • edge-set
  • port-set
  • recognition

Primitive Graph Edge Type Check

Checks that every edge connects ports with identical carrier types.

Proof

  • Uses: port type assignments to compare each edge’s endpoints.
  • Shows that: edge typing can be decided as a primitive predicate.

title: Harmonic Flow id: harmonic-flow role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Harmonic Flow

Flow governed by harmonic modes instead of local context.

Definition

  • Inputs: Harmonic Mode decomposition and a Hypertensor Section.
  • Output: evolved section according to spectral (harmonic) dynamics.
  • Behaviour: evolve each mode separately per its eigenvalue/phase, then recombine.

Invariants

  • Mode-wise evolution: modes evolve independently.
  • Consistency: recombined section respects sheaf/coverage constraints.

Proof

  • Uses: harmonic modes to drive flow.
  • Produces: evolved section.
  • Shows that: spectral/harmonic dynamics can drive section evolution.

title: Holonomy Operator id: holonomy-operator role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Holonomy Operator

Recognises the phase accumulated by transporting a section along a closed shuffle-equivalence loop (relational geometric phase).

Definition

  • Inputs: a closed Trace Shuffle Class loop and a Hypertensor Section.
  • Output: recognition representing the accumulated phase/holonomy.
  • Behaviour: transport the section along the loop via restrictions/gluings and compare to the original; record the deviation as a recognition.

Invariants

  • Loop invariance: trivial holonomy on contractible loops; nontrivial otherwise.
  • Sheaf compatibility: uses earned sheaf transports; respects gluing/locality.

Proof

  • Uses: shuffle classes and hypertensor sections to define transport; compares start/end to extract phase as a recognition.
  • Shows that: path dependence is measurable as holonomy in the relational setting.

title: Identity Path Recognition id: identity-path-recognition role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Identity Path Recognition

Recognises paths where flow followed by nucleus leaves recognitions invariant.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Directed Path and its identity path from Identify Path.
  • Output: recognition indicating whether the path is homotopic to identity (invariant under flow/nucleus).
  • Behaviour: compare path composition with identity, collapse under nucleus, report invariance.

Invariants

  • True on loops contractible to identity; false otherwise.
  • Stable under homotopy.

Proof

  • Uses: path identity and directed paths.
  • Produces: recognition of identity equivalence.
  • Shows that: invariance along paths is recognisable in the homotopy framework.

title: Identity id: identity role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: runtime-value alias: value outputs:
  • id: runtime-value alias: value transformation:
  • value proof_refs:
  • runtime-value

Identity

Passes its input value through unchanged. Used to model returns and trivial flows in nested-array programs.

Proof

  • Uses: no additional structure; identity is the neutral morphism on any carrier.
  • Shows that: composing with identity yields the same value, enabling return to be expressed without primitive opcodes.

  • id: expression alias: then-branch
  • id: expression alias: else-branch

      • call

        • evaluate-expression

        • state
