        • call
        • evaluate-expression
        • else-branch
        • state proof_refs:
  • evaluate-expression
  • is-true
  • expression
  • runtime-value
  • evaluation-state

If

Branching operator that evaluates a predicate expression to a recognition value and selects one of two branches accordingly inside the nested-array evaluator.

Proof

  • Uses: evaluate-expression to compute predicate/branches and is-true to interpret the predicate recognition as a runtime truth value.
  • Shows that: branching is defined in terms of earned operators over expressions and recognitions, without relying on any external logic algebra.

title: Imply Recognition id: imply-recognition role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: recognition alias: antecedent
  • id: recognition alias: consequent outputs:
  • id: recognition alias: implication transformation:
  • let
    • merged
      • call
        • recognition-merge
        • antecedent
        • consequent
    • impl_predicate
      • call
        • imply-predicate
        • antecedent
          • predicate
        • consequent
          • predicate
    • implication
      • call
        • update-predicate
        • merged
        • impl_predicate
    • return
      • implication proof_refs:
  • recognition
  • recognition-merge
  • imply-recognition

Imply Recognition

Implication in the recognition lattice: returns the greatest recognition x such that antecedent ∧ x ≤ consequent, gluing supports/windows/scopes and updating the predicate.

Proof

  • Derived from: implication act on predicates plus sheaf gluing for supports/windows.
  • Shows that: implication is earned as the right adjoint to meet over recognitions while preserving locality and flow/nucleus invariants.

title: Import Linked Data id: import-linked-data role: operator status: someday derived_from: computer/function/recognition inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Import Linked Data

Ingests external RDF/linked-data (e.g., Wikidata/Wiktionary dumps or HTTP fetch) into the internal Linked Data Graph.

Definition

  • Inputs: source descriptor (URI, format, retrieval parameters).
  • Outputs: Linked Data Graph or Computer Failure.
  • Behaviour: fetch/parse RDF, populate triples/quads with provenance, optional mappings; emit failure on parse/integrity/policy errors.

Invariants

  • Integrity: payload hashes must verify; provenance recorded.
  • Policy: import complies with source licensing/usage constraints.
  • No state mutation on failure.

Proof

  • Derived from: Computer Recognition, treating RDF payload as recognised data with provenance.

title: Inject Noise id: inject-noise role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Inject Noise

Injects perturbations into flow/context according to a relational noise model.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Relational Noise Model and the earned Flow Context.
  • Output: noisy recognitions in the Recognition Carrier, ready for collapse via nucleus.
  • Behaviour: branch flow along noise channels, apply distortions, then collapse or propagate as appropriate.

Invariants

  • Respect bounds: noise never exceeds modelled distortion limits.
  • Collapse: noisy branches eventually collapse or signal failure.

Proof

  • Uses: noise model to generate perturbations; flow context to propagate them.
  • Produces: noisy recognitions for further processing/collapse.
  • Shows that: noise injection can be handled structurally.

title: Intake Normalisation tags:

  • intake
  • normalization layer: internal-theorem status: plan depends_on:
  • math/structure/intake-context
  • acts/equate-contexts claims:
  • type: operator judgement: Normalise : IntakeContexts × Observations ⇀ IntakeContexts × Observations enforces contextual equivalence

Intake Normalisation

Statement

There exists an operator/relation

Normalise : IntakeContexts × Observations ⇀ IntakeContexts × Observations

that enforces earned contextual equivalences on intake data.

Definition by acts

  • equate-contexts induces normalization over contexts and linked observations, ensuring no intake path produces conflicting recognitions.

Uniqueness

Given IntakeContexts, Observations, and the equate act, Normalise is fixed up to nucleus equivalence.

title: Interface Output Presentation id: interface-present role: Derive observables for output ports from internal recognitions/sections. status: plan inputs:

  • id: interface-port name: out-port
  • id: recognition name: recognitions
  • id: quasicrystalline-hypertensor-sheaf-section name: section outputs:
  • id: observable-carrier name: observables transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Interface Output Presentation

Determines which observables/metrics may be presented at an output port and how they are derived from internal recognitions and sheaf sections.

Proof

  • Uses: port exposure rules, derivation of observables from recognitions/section, and filtering to enforce port constraints.
  • Shows that: presenting outputs at a port yields only allowed observables derived from earned internal state.

title: Interface Simulation id: interface-simulation role: Execute the interface stack for external requests end-to-end. status: plan inputs:

  • id: interface-port name: port
  • id: recognition name: request
  • id: policy-regime name: policy
  • id: trace-carrier name: trace outputs:
  • id: recognition name: response
  • id: failure-carrier name: failure transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Interface Simulation

Executes authentication/policy, intake normalization, dispatch, and output presentation for an external interface request.

Proof

  • Uses: interface-validate, distribution-route, and interface-present to sequence validation → dispatch → presentation.
  • Shows that: every request is validated, dispatched, and presented (or failed) according to earned intake/policy/measurement semantics.

title: Interface Input Validation id: interface-validate role: Validate incoming payloads at an interface port under intake and policy. status: plan inputs:

  • id: interface-port name: port
  • id: recognition name: payload
  • id: policy-regime name: policy
  • id: trace-carrier name: trace outputs:
  • id: recognition name: recognition
  • id: failure-carrier name: failure transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Interface Input Validation

Validates whether an incoming payload at an input port can be turned into recognised observations or views under earned intake/policy rules.

Behaviour

  • Normalize payload via intake stack.
  • Check policy acceptance given port/trace context.
  • On success, emit a recognition carrier with trace provenance; on failure, emit a failure carrier.

Proof

  • Uses: intake normalization, policy acceptance, and recognition/failure emission operators.
  • Shows that: only payloads admitted by intake and policy produce recognitions; otherwise a failure is reported without mutating state.

title: Interleaving Flow id: interleaving-flow role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Interleaving Flow

Merges independent flows using shuffle equivalence to model concurrency.

Definition

  • Inputs: multiple flows/trace segments with independent contexts.
  • Output: Trace Shuffle Class representing all interleavings.
  • Behaviour: compute shuffle equivalence of interleavings; collapse under nucleus where applicable.

Invariants

  • Commutativity: independent steps commute up to shuffle.
  • Coverage: all valid interleavings are represented.

Proof

  • Uses: flow context and shuffle structure to merge traces.
  • Produces: shuffle class of interleavings.
  • Shows that: concurrency can be expressed as shuffle-based flow composition.

title: Introspection Monitor Task id: introspection-monitor-task role: Schedule and execute monitoring tasks over introspection channels. status: plan inputs:

  • id: introspection-probe name: probes
  • id: introspection-channel name: channel
  • id: task-carrier name: tasks outputs:
  • id: task-carrier name: tasks' transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Introspection Monitor Task

Monitoring tasks scheduled alongside normal work:

  • read from Introspection Channel,
  • compute derived metrics or checks,
  • optionally inject monitoring results back into intake and scheduling.

Proof

  • Uses: channel reads, metric computation, intake injection, and task scheduling to integrate monitoring into the task carrier.
  • Shows that: monitoring tasks are scheduled with computed metrics while preserving task-carrier invariants.

title: Irreversible Check id: irreversible-check role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Irreversible Check

Checks whether a given flow step is reversible or irreversible per the flow law.

Definition

  • Inputs: a flow step in Flow Context and Reversible Flow Law.
  • Output: updated law witness indicating reversibility/irreversibility.
  • Behaviour: evaluate entropy change and existence of inverse; classify.

Invariants

  • Correct classification: reversible iff entropy preserved and inverse exists.
  • Stability: classification stable under equivalent flow presentations.

Proof

  • Uses: flow/entropy criteria.
  • Produces: reversibility witness.
  • Shows that: reversibility can be decided per step in alignment with entropy.

title: Irreversible Flow id: irreversible-flow role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Irreversible Flow

Flow with entropy monotonicity constraints enforcing time’s direction.

Definition

  • Inputs: earned Flow Context and the Entropy Functional.
  • Output: constrained flow that forbids entropy decrease along admissible evolutions.
  • Behaviour: restrict flow steps to those with non-increasing entropy; attach witnesses when irreversibility holds.

Invariants

  • Entropy monotonicity: entropy never increases along the flow.
  • Causality: flow respects temporal ordering implied by entropy direction.

Proof

  • Uses: Flow Context and Entropy Functional.
  • Produces: constrained Flow Context.
  • Shows that: irreversibility can be enforced by restricting flow to entropy monotone steps.

title: Is Graph Acyclic id: is-graph-acyclic role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: edge-set alias: edges outputs:
  • id: recognition alias: predicate transformation:
  • let
    • cycles
      • call
      • detect-graph-cycles
      • edges
    • return
      • call
      • negate-recognition
      • cycles proof_refs:
  • edge-set
  • detect-graph-cycles
  • negate-recognition
  • recognition

Graph Acyclic?

Predicate over the Recognition Carrier asserting that the Edge Set has no directed cycles. It detects cycles, then negates the cycle witness.

Proof

  • Uses: Edge Set adjacency to detect cycle witnesses via detect-graph-cycles, then applies negate-recognition to assert acyclicity.
  • Shows that: if detection yields no cycles, negation yields the top predicate; if cycles are present, the predicate captures the failure, enabling topological ordering preconditions in wiring plans.

title: Is True id: is-true role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: recognition alias: predicate outputs:
  • id: runtime-value alias: truth transformation:
  • call
    • recognition-truth
    • predicate proof_refs:
  • recognition
  • runtime-value
  • recognition-truth

Is True

Converts a predicate in the Recognition Carrier to a truthy/falsy runtime value for branching in the interpreter.

Proof

  • Uses: check for top/bottom (or equivalent truth ordering) on recognitions.
  • Shows that: branching relies only on the earned recognition lattice without introducing an unearned logic algebra.

title: Join Recognition id: join-recognition role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: recognition alias: left
  • id: recognition alias: right outputs:
  • id: recognition alias: join transformation:
  • let
    • merged
      • call
        • recognition-merge
        • left
        • right
    • predicate
      • call
        • join-predicate
        • left
          • predicate
        • right
          • predicate
    • join
      • call
        • update-predicate
        • merged
        • predicate
    • return
      • join proof_refs:
  • recognition
  • recognition-merge
  • join-recognition

Join Recognitions

Binary join (disjunction) on recognitions: glue supports/windows/scopes, then take the lattice join of predicates.

Invariants

  • Support/window/scope remain glued; predicate is the least upper bound.
  • Flow/nucleus compatibility maintained via recognition-merge.

Proof

  • Derived from: recognition join act on predicates plus sheaf gluing for supports/windows.
  • Shows that: recognitions can be disjoined without breaking locality or flow/nucleus invariants.

title: Judgement Equivalence id: judgement-equivalence role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Judgement Equivalence

The judgemental lift of observation equivalence: typing contexts respect CIU behaviour via judgement equivalence.

Definition

  • Inputs/Outputs: typing judgements within the earned Judgment Triad.
  • Behaviour: two judgements are equivalent when their observations are contextually indistinguishable via Observation Equivalence and typing contexts respect that equivalence.

Invariants

  • Contextual indistinguishability extended to typing judgements.
  • Witnessed by normalized/witness acts.

Proof

  • Uses: Observation Equivalence as the base CIU relation on observations.
  • Shows that: lifting observation equivalence to judgements yields typing equivalence respecting contextual indistinguishability.

title: Judgment Dualize id: judgment-dualize role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Judgment Dualize

Produces the dual of a judgment by reversing flow and swapping premises/conclusions.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Judgment Triad.
  • Output: its Judgment Dual.
  • Behaviour: reverse admissible flow, interchange premise/conclusion roles, adjust inference direction.

Invariants

  • Duality: applying twice returns the original (up to equivalence).
  • Soundness: dual respects earned flow/nucleus constraints.

Proof

  • Uses: judgment triad data and flow reversal rules.
  • Produces: dual judgment.
  • Shows that: judgments have coherent duals in the relational setting.

title: Judgment Evaluate Section id: judgment-evaluate-section role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Judgment Evaluate Section

Typed big-step/small-step evaluation of judgments over hypertensor sections, integrating substitution and evaluation semantics.

Definition

  • Inputs: typing derivation from Typed Judgment System, a section to evaluate, and substitution environment (if needed).
  • Output: Proof Trace showing evaluation steps and resulting typed value/section.
  • Behaviour: apply Substitute Section as needed, then iterate Evaluate Section Step under typing constraints, recording derivation.

Invariants

  • Type preservation: evaluated result has the derived type.
  • Progress: well-typed terms evaluate or are values; failure only via failure algebra.

Proof

  • Uses: typed judgment rules, substitution, small-step evaluation.
  • Produces: proof trace of evaluation.
  • Shows that: evaluation is type-safe and derivations can be recorded formally.

title: Judgment Evaluate Step id: judgment-evaluate-step role: operator status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/recognize-value inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Judgment Evaluate Step

Operator describing call-by-value evaluation in the judgment calculus: stepping inside an evaluation context when the hole contains a value.

Proof

Sketch: evaluation frames from Judgment Evaluation Context locate the hole; Judgment Value recognises values. Define the step relation accordingly and show it respects the earned evaluation structure.

title: Judgment Substitution id: judgment-substitution role: operator status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/substitution-term inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Judgment Substitution

Capture-avoiding substitution for judgment-calculus terms, earned by SubstitutionTerm.

Proof

Sketch: SubstitutionTerm re-enters evaluation contexts without variable capture, using the binder structure. Show it respects the earned term/binder constructors and satisfies the beta rule when combined with evaluation.

title: Kernel Crawl id: kernel-crawl role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Kernel Crawl

Traverses the wiring/ports to enumerate reachable operators, carriers, and bindings in a wiring plan, producing annotations or failures.

Definition

  • Inputs: Wiring Plan and its underlying Connection Graph.
  • Outputs: annotated wiring plan or Failure Algebra element on invalid wiring.
  • Behaviour:
    1. Perform a graph traversal from entry steps, recording reachable operators and carrier-labelled ports.
    2. Check each edge for type compatibility; log a failure on mismatch.
    3. Detect cycles; if present, emit failure and stop traversal.
    4. Return the wiring plan annotated with reachability and discovered gaps.

Invariants

  • Read-only: does not mutate carrier state beyond annotating the plan.
  • Typing: only well-typed ports/operators are recorded; mismatches yield failures.
  • Acyclicity: cycles are reported as failures; traversal is otherwise exhaustive.

Proof

  • Uses: Connection Graph for traversal rules and Wiring Plan typing/acyclicity constraints.
  • Shows that: traversing a well-typed, acyclic wiring plan yields reachability annotations; ill-typed edges or cycles are captured as failure algebra elements.

title: Know Modality id: know-modality role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Know Modality

Epistemic necessity operator K_a that collapses an epistemic profile to the states that survive all earned observations of an agent.

Definition

  • Inputs: an Epistemic Profile for agent a and its inherited Modal Structure.
  • Output: the same epistemic profile restricted to states known by a.
  • Behaviour: take the meet of all observations available to a in the profile, then apply the nucleus (necessity) to close under earned inference; produce the resulting epistemic subprofile.

Invariants

  • Monotone: if P ≤ Q then K_a(P) ≤ K_a(Q).
  • Idempotent: K_a(K_a(P)) = K_a(P); inflationary/deflationary as earned by the modal structure.
  • Compatibility: commutes with profile restriction and observation witnesses.

Proof

  • Uses: Epistemic Profile and modal operators from Modal Structure to form the necessity operator.
  • Produces: an Epistemic Profile closed under knowledge for agent a.
  • Shows that: epistemic necessity is an interior/closure operator on epistemic recognitions satisfying modal laws.

title: Knowledge Graph Context id: knowledge-graph-context role: Attach trace/topology/policy/audit context to knowledge graph nodes/edges. status: plan inputs:

  • id: knowledge-node name: nodes
  • id: knowledge-edge name: edges
  • id: trace-carrier name: trace
  • id: policy-regime name: policy outputs:
  • id: knowledge-node name: nodes'
  • id: knowledge-edge name: edges' transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Knowledge Graph Context

Attaches trace IDs, topology constraints, policy regimes, and audit trails to knowledge graph nodes/edges to form a typed, constrained directed graph whose elements trace back to earned acts and contexts.

Proof

  • Uses: node/edge annotation operators to attach trace/policy metadata.
  • Shows that: contextualising nodes/edges yields a knowledge graph with provenance and policy constraints recorded.

title: Last id: last role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: runtime-value alias: items outputs:
  • id: runtime-value alias: value transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs:
  • runtime-value

Last

Returns the last element of a sequence of runtime values. Placeholder until sequence semantics are fully earned.

Proof

  • Uses: sequence order on runtime values.
  • Shows that: a selector for the tail exists; refine when sequence semantics are specified.

title: Let id: let role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: evaluation-state alias: state
  • id: runtime-value alias: bindings
  • id: expression alias: body outputs:
  • id: runtime-value alias: result transformation:
  • call
    • evaluate-with-bindings
    • state
    • bindings
    • body proof_refs:
  • evaluate-with-bindings
  • runtime-value
  • expression
  • evaluation-state

Let Binding

Introduces local bindings for subsequent expressions in the nested-array interpreter.

Proof

  • Uses: evaluate-with-bindings to extend the environment with provided bindings and evaluate the body expression.
  • Shows that: let-binding semantics are captured via an explicit evaluator step without hidden primitives.

title: Literal Equals id: literal-equals role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: runtime-value alias: left
  • id: runtime-value alias: right outputs:
  • id: runtime-value alias: truth transformation:
  • if
      • eq
      • left
      • right
      • return
      • true
      • return
      • false proof_refs:
  • runtime-value

Literal Equals

Compares two runtime literal payloads for structural equality, returning a boolean runtime value.

Invariants

  • Reflexive: literal equals itself.
  • Symmetric: order of arguments does not matter.
  • Deterministic: no side effects; returns true or false only.

title: Load Persistent Context tags:

  • storage
  • context layer: internal-theorem status: plan depends_on:
  • math/structure/persistent-context-carrier
  • math/structure/persistent-recognition
  • acts/retrace-context
  • acts/renew-context claims:
  • type: operator judgement: load_context : (PersistentContext, BaselineRemainder, PersistentRecognitionCarrier) ⇀ PersistentContextCarrier

Load Persistent Context

Statement

There exists an operator

load_context : (PersistentContext, BaselineRemainder, PersistentRecognitionCarrier) ⇀ PersistentContextCarrier

that replays a stored persistent context (and its fixed recognitions) to yield an earned persistent context up to nucleus equivalence.

Definition by acts

  • Uses retrace-context to replay the stored context on a trace.
  • Uses renew-context to reintroduce the baseline remainder.
  • Reattaches fixed recognitions from PersistentRecognitionCarrier.

Uniqueness

Because the inputs are earned carriers and the replay/renew acts fix the behaviour, any operator respecting these produces a nucleus-equivalent persistent context; thus load_context is unique in the earned sense.

title: Load Section id: load-section role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Load Section

Math-level operator mirroring machine load: materialises a section from encoded data.

Definition

  • Inputs: encoded section per Section Encoding Schema.
  • Output: Hypertensor Section in memory.
  • Behaviour: decode the payload, validate against schema, and instantiate section.

Invariants

  • Fidelity: decoded section matches encoding.
  • Safety: invalid encodings yield failure (via failure algebra).

Proof

  • Uses: encoding schema to decode safely.
  • Produces: section or failure.
  • Shows that: loading a section is well-defined at the math level.

title: Load Snapshot id: load-snapshot role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: snapshot alias: snapshot outputs:
  • id: recognition alias: recognitions
  • id: evaluation-state alias: eval-state
  • id: failure-carrier alias: failure transformation:
  • call
    • primitive-load-snapshot
    • snapshot proof_refs:
  • snapshot
  • recognition
  • evaluation-state
  • failure-carrier
  • primitive-load-snapshot

Load Snapshot

Reconstructs recognitions and evaluation state from a stored Snapshot.

Proof

  • Uses: decode/reconstruction primitive.
  • Shows that: loading is explicit; failures are surfaced when reconstruction cannot satisfy invariants.

title: Local Snapshot Operator id: local-snapshot-operator role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Local Snapshot Operator

Captures local state with baseline remainder; no global context required.

Definition

  • Inputs: local Persistent Context Carrier and Persistent Recognition Carrier.
  • Output: local Memory Carrier snapshot.
  • Behaviour: record local contexts/recognitions, compute baseline remainder, and package for later global composition.

Invariants

  • Consistency: snapshot reflects a self-consistent local state.
  • Non-intrusive: does not alter local state; pure capture.

Proof

  • Uses: persistent carriers to build local snapshot.
  • Produces: Memory Carrier.
  • Shows that: local snapshots are well-defined building blocks for distributed consistency.

title: Lookup Binding id: lookup-binding role: operator status: plan inputs:

    • return

  • evaluation-state
  • binding-environment
  • runtime-value

Lookup Binding

Retrieves a bound runtime value from the Evaluation State by name (port id or variable).

Proof

  • Uses: the state’s Binding Environment.
  • Shows that: name resolution is available as a documented lookup over the binding environment.

title: Lookup Signature id: lookup-signature role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: operator-signature alias: signature transformation:

    • read-operator-signature
    • target proof_refs:

  • operator-signature
  • read-operator-signature

Lookup Signature

Kernel lookup that retrieves the Operator Signature associated with an operator id from the interpreter’s registry.

Proof

  • Uses: the registry mapping operator ids to their signatures.
  • Shows that: signatures are available to enforce typing before execution.

title: Map Evaluate id: map-evaluate role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: argument-list alias: expr-list
  • id: evaluation-state alias: state outputs:
  • id: runtime-value alias: values transformation:
  • call
    • evaluate-arguments
    • expr-list
    • state proof_refs:
  • evaluate-expression
  • evaluation-state
  • argument-list
  • runtime-value
  • evaluate-arguments

Map Evaluate

Evaluates an Argument List of expressions in an Evaluation State, returning the list of values.

Proof

  • Uses: evaluate-expression over each element of the list within the same evaluation state.
  • Shows that: list arguments can be evaluated componentwise using the core interpreter.

title: Map InterTower id: map-intertower role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Map InterTower

Applies an intertower map to transport recognitions between tower geometries.

Definition

  • Inputs: an InterTower Map and recognitions in one tower.
  • Output: mapped recognitions in the target tower, updating the intertower map if needed.
  • Behaviour: apply the functorial correspondences respecting flow/nucleus and tower transitions.

Invariants

  • Coherence: mapping commutes with tower transitions.
  • Soundness: mapped recognitions preserve earned invariants.

Proof

  • Uses: intertower map structure and tower data.
  • Produces: mapped recognitions/intertower map.
  • Shows that: cross-tower transport is executable and coherent.

title: “Markov\u2013Modal Fuse” id: markov-modal-fuse role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Markov–Modal Fuse

Combines stochastic evolution with modal tower traversal.

Definition

  • Inputs: stochastic evolution kernel and modal operators.
  • Output: updated Markov–Modal Fusion state/dynamics.
  • Behaviour: apply stochastic transition and modal shifts in a coherent, fused manner.

Invariants

  • Probability preservation and modal coherence.
  • Commutation rules between modal shifts and stochastic updates respected.

Proof

  • Uses: fused structure to orchestrate combined updates.
  • Produces: fused dynamic state.
  • Shows that: stochastic and modal behaviours can be applied jointly.

title: Max Collapse Depth id: max-collapse-depth role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: recognition-collapse-depth alias: left
  • id: recognition-collapse-depth alias: right outputs:
  • id: recognition-collapse-depth alias: depth transformation:
  • return
    • depth proof_refs:
  • recognition-collapse-depth

Max Collapse Depth

Returns the greater of two collapse-depth measures.

Proof

  • Uses: ordering on collapse depth.
  • Shows that: collapse depth can be combined conservatively.

title: MCP Handle Request id: mcp-handle-request role: operator status: someday derived_from: computer/function/evaluate-step inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

MCP Handle Request

Dispatches a single MCP request against an endpoint wiring plan, returning a typed response or a computer failure.

Definition

  • Inputs:
    • MCP Endpoint describing available commands and handlers.
    • MCP Request carrying the command and args.
    • OAuth Token for authorisation (may be nil for open commands).
    • Computer Evaluation State to execute the bound handler.
  • Outputs: MCP Response or Computer Failure.
  • Behaviour:
    1. Validate the request against the endpoint (command exists, schema matches).
    2. Validate the token (scopes, expiry) if the command requires auth.
    3. Bind command arguments into the evaluation state ports declared by the endpoint.
    4. Step the handler via Evaluate Step.
    5. Marshal outputs into MCP response; on any error, emit computer failure with origin/diagnostics.
  • Serialization: request/response use the fields declared in the respective structures; failures follow Computer Failure.

Invariants

  • Command dispatch follows endpoint wiring; no unregistered command executes.
  • Auth/policy enforced before dispatch; failures do not mutate state.
  • Responses are typed per endpoint schema and trace back to the request/endpoint version.

Proof

  • Derived from: Computer Evaluate Step.
  • Shows that: MCP dispatch/refusal can be expressed as a single computer evaluation step respecting wiring, typing, and failure semantics.

title: Measure Collapse Depth id: measure-collapse-depth role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Measure Collapse Depth

Measures how many nucleus applications are needed to reach section normal form.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Hypertensor Section.
  • Output: Section Collapse Depth.
  • Behaviour: run normalization, count nucleus steps, report depth.

Invariants

  • Zero for normal forms; increases with complexity.
  • Deterministic given the section and normalization strategy.

Proof

  • Uses: normalization algorithm to count steps.
  • Produces: collapse depth metric.
  • Shows that: collapse depth is consistent with normalization.

title: Measure Fixed Invariant id: measure-fixed-invariant role: operator status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/fixed-measure inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Measure Fixed Invariant

The Measure Fixed Invariant operator collapses a fixed invariant state along the earned nucleus for the fixed profile.

Definition

  • Input: a Fixed Invariant State.
  • Output: a Fixed Invariant State representing the nucleus-equivalence class of the input.
  • Behaviour: applies the nucleus induced by acts/fixed-measure to restrict the state to its fixed-profile measurement.

Invariants

  • Measurement retains only trace-shuffle- and fixed-shape-compatible content.
  • Outputs remain in the fixed invariant carrier.

Proof

By construction from acts/fixed-measure, this operator computes the nucleus of fixed invariant states. It collapses variations that the fixed profile identifies while keeping results within the same carrier.

title: Measure Flow Gradient id: measure-flow-gradient role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Measure Flow Gradient

Quantifies rate of change of recognitions along flow.

Definition

  • Inputs: flow dynamics and recognitions.
  • Output: Flow Gradient measurements.
  • Behaviour: compute derivative-like change per flow step; record as gradient.

Invariants

  • Monotone with respect to flow strength.
  • Zero when flow leaves recognition unchanged.

Proof

  • Uses: flow context to measure change.
  • Produces: flow gradient data.
  • Shows that: flow steepness can be measured recognitionally.

title: Measure Modal Drift id: measure-modal-drift role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Measure Modal Drift

Measures drift of recognitions across modal layers under flow.

Definition

  • Inputs: recognitions, Modal Structure, and Flow Context.
  • Output: Modal Drift measurement.
  • Behaviour: track modal layer changes per flow step; quantify drift rate.

Invariants

  • Monotonic tracking: drift accumulates; stabilises if modal status fixed.
  • Zero drift when modal status unchanged.

Proof

  • Uses: modal structure and flow to observe changes.
  • Produces: modal drift data.
  • Shows that: modal drift is measurable.

title: Measure Recognition Collapse Depth id: measure-recognition-collapse-depth role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Measure Recognition Collapse Depth

Measures how many nucleus/simplification steps a recognition needs to reach normal form.

Definition

  • Inputs: a recognition.
  • Output: Recognition Collapse Depth.
  • Behaviour: run normalisation, count steps, return the count.

Invariants

  • Zero for normal forms.
  • Deterministic given the recognition and normalisation strategy.

Proof

  • Uses: normal form computation.
  • Produces: collapse depth metric.
  • Shows that: collapse depth aligns with the chosen normalisation procedure.

title: Measurement Dynamics id: measurement-dynamics role: operator status: plan derived_from: acts/mixing inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Measurement Dynamics

Statement

The entropy and mixing acts applied to MeasurementKernels describe how repeated measurement and kernel application converge toward stationary behaviour on the earned class of systems.

Proof sketch

  • entropy and mixing record convergence/mixing properties of kernels.
  • Applying these to MeasurementKernels yields convergence toward stationary behaviour.

Uniqueness

Given the kernels and entropy/mixing acts, the measurement dynamics are fixed up to earned equivalence.

Proof

  • Derived from: Mixing.
  • Inputs: Measurement Kernel.
  • Shows that: applying mixing/entropy properties to measurement kernels yields convergence toward stationary behaviour.

title: Meet Recognition id: meet-recognition role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: recognition alias: left
  • id: recognition alias: right outputs:
  • id: recognition alias: meet transformation:
  • let
    • merged
      • call
        • recognition-merge
        • left
        • right
    • predicate
      • call
        • meet-predicate
        • left
          • predicate
        • right
          • predicate
    • meet
      • call
        • update-predicate
        • merged
        • predicate
    • return
      • meet proof_refs:
  • recognition
  • recognition-merge
  • meet-recognition

Meet Recognitions

Binary meet (conjunction) on recognitions: glue supports/windows/scopes, then take the lattice meet of predicates.

Invariants

  • Support/window/scope remain glued; predicate is the greatest lower bound.
  • Flow/nucleus compatibility maintained via recognition-merge.

Proof

  • Derived from: recognition meet act on predicates plus sheaf gluing for supports/windows.
  • Shows that: recognitions can be conjoined without breaking locality or flow/nucleus invariants.

title: Merge Bindings id: merge-bindings role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: runtime-value alias: bindings outputs:

    • extended proof_refs:

  • runtime-value

Merge Bindings

Extends an Evaluation State with additional binding mappings.

Proof

  • Uses: state binding map to merge new entries.
  • Shows that: binding extension is available as an explicit operator update.

title: Merge Predicate id: merge-predicate role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: recognition-ring alias: left
  • id: recognition-ring alias: right outputs:
  • id: recognition-ring alias: merged transformation:
  • return
    • merged proof_refs:
  • recognition-ring

Merge Predicate

Combines two recognition predicates (e.g., via lattice join/meet as appropriate to the merge context).

Proof

  • Uses: recognition lattice/ring operations.
  • Shows that: predicates can be combined while staying in the earned lattice.

title: Merge Provenance id: merge-provenance role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: trace-carrier alias: left
  • id: trace-carrier alias: right outputs:
  • id: trace-carrier alias: merged transformation:
  • return
    • merged proof_refs:
  • trace-carrier

Merge Provenance

Combines provenance records from two recognitions into a merged trace history.

Proof

  • Uses: trace composition rules.
  • Shows that: provenance can be merged to preserve reproducibility.

title: Modal Fold id: modal-fold role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Modal Fold

Collapses two modal layers (e.g., □ and ◇) into a mixed operator when admissible.

Definition

  • Inputs: modal operators from Modal Structure.
  • Output: folded modal structure where selected layers are identified.
  • Behaviour: define a mixed modality combining necessity/possibility; collapse recognitions accordingly.

Invariants

  • Soundness: folding respects modal laws and does not violate earned adjunctions.
  • Idempotent: folding twice yields the same structure.

Proof

  • Uses: modal structure to define folding map.
  • Produces: folded modal structure.
  • Shows that: modal layers can be identified when admissible without breaking modal coherence.

title: Modal Unfold id: modal-unfold role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Modal Unfold

Expands a recognition into its pre-modal decomposition across layers.

Definition

  • Inputs: a modal structure with folded/combined modalities.
  • Output: modal structure with separated layers showing pre-modal components.
  • Behaviour: decompose mixed modality into base necessity/possibility (or other) layers; redistribute recognitions accordingly.

Invariants

  • Soundness: unfolding preserves semantics; only exposes structure.
  • Reversibility: folding then unfolding recovers the original (up to equivalence).

Proof

  • Uses: modal structure to split combined modalities.
  • Produces: unfolded modal structure.
  • Shows that: modal layers can be separated for analysis while maintaining earned modal laws.

title: Modus Ponens Recognition id: modus-ponens-recognition role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: recognition alias: antecedent
  • id: recognition alias: implication outputs:
  • id: recognition alias: consequence transformation:
  • let
    • combined
      • call
        • recognition-merge
        • antecedent
        • implication
    • result_predicate
      • call
        • modus-ponens-predicate
        • antecedent
          • predicate
        • implication
          • predicate
    • consequence
      • call
        • update-predicate
        • combined
        • result_predicate
    • return
      • consequence proof_refs:
  • recognition
  • recognition-merge
  • imply-recognition
  • meet-recognition
  • modus-ponens-act

Recognition Modus Ponens

Inference: from A and A ⇒ B, derive B in the recognition lattice while preserving support/window/scope/provenance and flow/nucleus compatibility.

Proof

  • Uses: implication/meet adjunction on predicates plus sheaf gluing for supports/windows.
  • Shows that: modus ponens is earned in the recognition lattice; given antecedent and implication, the consequence follows without violating locality or flow/nucleus invariants.

title: Monad Reindexing id: monad-reindex role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Monad Reindexing

Definition

  • Inputs: a morphism in the Index Base and a monadic object in the source Monad Fiber.
  • Outputs: the pullback of that monadic object into the target fibre, with the monad action preserved.
  • Behaviour: lifts the math-layer Reindexing operator so that pulling back along a base morphism commutes with the fibrewise monad structure.

Invariants

  • Functorial: respects identity and composition of base morphisms.
  • Monad-compatible: preserves unit and multiplication of the fibrewise monad.

Proof

  • Uses: Reindexing to transport monadic recognitions along base morphisms while preserving unit/multiplication.
  • Shows that: when fibres carry a monad, the reindexing functor lifts to monadic objects without breaking the monad laws, yielding a fibrewise monad in the target fibre.

title: Multi-Scale Reconvergence id: multi-scale-reconvergence role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Multi-Scale Reconvergence

Collapses recognitions across multiple scales to a unified shape when consistent.

Definition

  • Inputs: recognitions/sections at multiple levels of the Scale Tower.
  • Output: unified Hypertensor Section if consistent; otherwise refusal/failure.
  • Behaviour: check compatibility across scales via rescaling; if consistent, apply multi-level nucleus to converge to a common section.

Invariants

  • Consistency: only converges when cross-scale data agree under rescaling.
  • Idempotent: reconverging an already converged set yields the same result.

Proof

  • Uses: scale rescaling and nucleus to merge multi-scale data.
  • Produces: unified section or signals inconsistency.
  • Shows that: multi-scale data can be reconciled when compatible.

title: Natural Transformation id: natural-transformation role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Natural Transformation

Definition

  • Inputs: two fibrewise functors built from the Effect Monad and Coeffect Comonad acting over the indexed base.
  • Outputs: a family of morphisms, one per context, commuting with both effect and coeffect reindexing actions.
  • Behaviour: assigns to each context a morphism between the corresponding fibres such that pulling back along any base morphism preserves the transformation (naturality square commutes).

Invariants

  • Reindexing-natural: for any base morphism, reindexing then transforming equals transforming then reindexing.
  • Compatible with monad/comonad structure: respects unit/multiplication and counit/comultiplication where applicable.

Proof

  • Uses: the indexed monad/comonad structures (Effect Monad, Coeffect Comonad) to define source and target functors.
  • Produces: Module witnesses built from families of fibrewise arrows that commute with reindexing.
  • Shows that: specifying fibrewise arrows that commute with reindexing yields a natural transformation across the indexed base, refining the context-level notion at calculus.

title: Negate Recognition id: negate-recognition role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: recognition alias: predicate outputs:
  • id: recognition alias: negation transformation:
  • let
    • neg_predicate
      • call
        • negate-predicate
        • predicate
          • predicate
    • negation
      • call
        • update-predicate
        • predicate
        • neg_predicate
    • return
      • negation proof_refs:
  • recognition
  • negate

Negate Recognition

Pseudocomplement in the recognition lattice: returns the largest recognition whose meet with the input yields bottom, preserving support/window/scope via structural sharing.

Proof

  • Derived from: negation act on predicates.
  • Shows that: recognition negation can be expressed by negating the predicate while preserving support/window/scope and flow/nucleus data.

title: Network Schedule id: network-schedule role: Schedule tasks over a network topology respecting trace and wiring constraints. status: plan inputs:

  • id: network-topology name: topology
  • id: network-channel name: channels
  • id: task-carrier name: tasks
  • id: trace-carrier name: trace outputs:
  • id: task-carrier name: scheduled transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Network Schedule

Orders tasks attached to ports/channels in a way that respects the trace spine, port compatibility, and scheduling dependencies.

Proof

  • Uses: task precedence/parallelization plus topology-aware ordering to produce a schedule consistent with trace and wiring constraints.
  • Shows that: the resulting schedule can be executed without violating dependencies or port/channel compatibility.

title: Norm Violation Recognition id: norm-violation-recognition role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Norm Violation Recognition

Detects when a recognition violates a norm regime and emits a violation witness or neutral element if compliant.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Norm Regime and a recognition in the Recognition Carrier.
  • Outputs: recognition of violation (or neutral if compliant).
  • Behaviour: evaluate the recognition against the regime’s admissibility predicate; if outside, return a violation recognition (e.g., bottom or tagged violation); otherwise return neutral/top.

Invariants

  • Soundness: compliant recognitions do not yield violation; violators always yield violation.
  • Monotonicity: tightening the regime cannot turn a violation into compliance.
  • Compatibility: interacts with flow/nucleus so violations remain violations under evolution.

Proof

  • Uses: Norm Regime as policy, and Recognition Carrier as domain/codomain.
  • Produces: Recognition Carrier marking violation.
  • Shows that: normative predicates can be expressed as recognition operations consistent with earned lattice/logic structure.

title: Normalise Admissible Morphism id: normalise-admissible role: operator status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/normalize-strongly inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Normalise Admissible Morphism

Operator N∞ : Adm → Adm mapping admissible morphisms to canonical normal forms as defined in Admissible Normal Form.

Statement

  • Termination: every f ∈ Adm rewrites to N∞(f) under the earned rewrite relation.
  • Confluence: normal form is unique up to coherent cells.
  • Factorisation: f_nf = m* ∘ e* with maximal generative part e* and minimal observational part m*, no further rewrites apply.

Proof

Sketch: follows from the earned strong normalization/reducibility acts; define N∞ by normalising and collapsing via the admissible normal-form structure.

title: Normalize Time id: normalize-time role: operator status: someday derived_from: computer/function/evaluate-step inputs:

  • computer/library/type/calendar-event
  • computer/library/type/timezone-profile outputs:
  • computer/library/type/calendar-event
  • computer/type/computer-failure transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Normalize Time

Applies timezone/offset rules to calendar events, yielding normalized instants.

Definition

  • Inputs: Calendar Event, Timezone Profile.
  • Outputs: normalised Calendar Event or Computer Failure.
  • Behaviour: rewrites event instants/durations using timezone rules; fails on invalid/ambiguous transitions.

Invariants

  • Deterministic normalization for given rules; no ambiguous offsets accepted.
  • No state mutation on failure.

Proof

  • Derived from: Computer Evaluate Step.
  • Shows that: applying timezone rules via computer evaluation yields normalized events or failures without bypassing kernel semantics.

title: OAuth Validate id: oauth-validate role: operator status: someday derived_from: computer/function/recognition inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

OAuth Validate

Validates OAuth credentials and issues tokens as computer-layer recognitions.

Definition

  • Inputs: OAuth Credential.
  • Outputs: OAuth Token or Computer Failure.
  • Behaviour:
    1. Verify client registration, redirect URI, signature, and expiry.
    2. Check requested scopes against policy.
    3. Issue an OAuth token with bounded scopes/expiry, signed for integrity; or emit a computer failure detailing the rejection.
  • Serialization: inputs/outputs follow the JSON/YAML fields declared in the credential/token structures.

Invariants

  • Tokens are only issued for valid credentials with authorised scopes.
  • Failure purity: on failure, no token is issued and no mutable state changes.
  • Tokens carry provenance/issuer data for audit.

Proof

  • Derived from: Computer Recognition.
  • Shows that: credential recognition/validation/refusal can be expressed with the computer recognition operator while emitting explicit computer failures.

title: Obligation Operator id: obligation-operator role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Obligation Operator

Given a norm regime and a recognition, produce the obligation it imposes.

Definition

  • Inputs: Norm Regime and a recognition in the Recognition Carrier.
  • Output: recognition representing the minimal action/condition required to avoid violation.
  • Behaviour: map each recognition to the closest admissible recognition under the regime (interior/closure depending on discipline/filter).

Invariants

  • Soundness: satisfying the obligation implies compliance with the regime.
  • Minimality: obligation is least among admissible recognitions above the input.
  • Monotonicity: strengthening the regime tightens obligations.

Proof

  • Uses: Norm Regime as policy and Recognition Carrier as domain/codomain.
  • Shows that: obligations can be expressed as recognition-level operators consistent with the lattice/logic structure.

title: Observable Bayes Update id: observable-bayes-update role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Observable Bayes Update

Typed probabilistic/Bayesian update operator as a combinator in the observable type system.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Bayesian Profile, an Observation, and observable operators from Observable Typed Operator.
  • Output: updated Bayesian Profile.
  • Behaviour: compute likelihood via observable operators, reweight the profile, renormalise, and recompute entropy; enforce typing.

Invariants

  • Normalisation: probabilities sum to 1.
  • Type safety: only well-typed updates allowed.
  • Entropy monotonicity under informative updates.

Proof

  • Uses: observable algebra and typed rules to perform Bayesian update.
  • Produces: posterior profile.
  • Shows that: Bayesian updates can be expressed as typed observables.

title: Observation Equivalence (CIU) id: observation-equivalence role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Observation Equivalence (CIU)

The observation-equivalence acts define contextual indistinguishability of observations: two observations are equivalent iff no earned observation context can distinguish them (CIU).

Definition

  • Inputs/Outputs: observation values within the earned observation carrier.
  • Behaviour: two observations are equivalent when every observation context yields the same result (contextual CIU criterion), incorporating normalisation and trace-based reentry.

Invariants

  • Contextual indistinguishability: preserved under earned observation contexts.
  • Trace compatibility: equivalence respects trace/reentry acts.

Proof

  • Uses: Observation and its earned contexts to witness indistinguishability.
  • Shows that: contextual equivalence of observations holds when every earned context yields nucleus-equivalent results, providing the CIU relation for observations.

title: Observe Judgment id: observe-judgment role: operator status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/observe-judgement inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Observe Judgment

The Observe Judgment operator witnesses judgment classifications through the observation layer.

Definition

  • Input: a judgment triad with witnesses.
  • Output: a judgment triad whose witnesses are observable.
  • Behaviour: applies the observe-judgment act to ensure witnesses are visible through the fixed observation layer.

Invariants

  • Witnesses are consistent with stabilized observation.
  • Judgments remain within the triad carrier.

Proof

Derived from acts/observe-judgement, which ensures observed witnesses match the stabilized judgment classification.

title: Parameterized Restrict id: parameterized-restrict role: operator status: plan derived_from: math/operator/hypertensor-restrict inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Parameterized Restrict

Executable restriction of a parameterized section along a cover arrow in the parameterized coverage site.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Parameterized Section over U and an inclusion V → U from the Parameterized Coverage Site.
  • Output: the restricted section over V, encoded in the same payload format.
  • Behaviour: calls the section’s restriction hook with the cover metadata, ensuring compatibility with overlap constructors and caching if available.

Invariants

  • Functoriality: restriction along identities is identity; along composites it matches iterated restriction.
  • Coverage compatibility: restriction respects cover refinements and produces inputs suitable for matching-family and gluing operators.
  • Determinism: given the same section and cover arrow, the restriction output is byte-identical or errors explicitly.

Proof

  • Derived from: Hypertensor Restrict by fixing executable restriction hooks on parameterized sections.
  • Shows that: the abstract restriction maps are realizable in calculus, feeding matching-family checks, gluing, and path composition lifts.

title: Parameterized Sheaf Locality id: parameterized-sheaf-locality role: operator status: plan derived_from: math/operator/sheaf-locality inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Parameterized Sheaf Locality

Executable locality witness: if two parameterized sections agree on a cover, they are equal in the parameterized sheaf.

Definition

  • Inputs: two Parameterized Sections over a window U and a cover {Ui → U} in the parameterized coverage site, with restrictions along each cover arrow.
  • Output: equality witness (or refusal) showing the sections are equal when all restrictions coincide.
  • Behaviour: applies the sheaf’s restriction functions to both sections on each cover element and overlap, checks equality of encoded payloads, and produces an equality certificate when all checks pass.

Preconditions

  • Restriction functions for the cover are total and deterministic.
  • Section encodings support equality checks used by the sheaf.

Postconditions

  • Successful equality witness can be reused under cover refinements.
  • Failure produces no equality witness; caller may attempt refinement or recomputation.

Proof

  • Derived from: Sheaf Locality by executing restriction and equality checks with parameterized sheaf routines.
  • Shows that: the earned locality law is realizable at the calculus level with executable evidence.

title: Parametricity id: parametricity role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Parametricity

Definition

  • Inputs: the indexed effect monad (Effect Monad), coeffect comonad (Coeffect Comonad), and the naturality data that ties them together (Natural Transformation).
  • Outputs: a Module of relations stating that terms respect all reindexings and both modal actions uniformly.
  • Behaviour: enforces that applying effect and coeffect along any base morphism yields indistinguishable behaviour in the relational model (uniformity across contexts); the resulting relation is carried as a module stable under reindexing.

Invariants

  • Reindexing-uniform: terms commute with all base morphisms via the effect/coeffect reindexing functors.
  • Modal coherence: effect and coeffect actions commute where defined, maintaining relational equality under both modalities.

Proof

  • Uses: Effect Monad, Coeffect Comonad, and Natural Transformation to define the modal actions and how terms move between them.
  • Produces: Module relations that remain fixed under every reindexing and modal action.
  • Shows that: requiring terms to be invariant under these indexed monad/comonad actions yields the parametricity property as a calculus-level operator.

title: Pareto Dominance id: pareto-dominance role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Pareto Dominance

Computes recognitions that are Pareto-superior across agents’ preference profiles.

Definition

  • Inputs: a collection of Preference Profiles.
  • Output: recognition of states that no agent strictly prefers less (Pareto front).
  • Behaviour: intersect preference orders to identify non-dominated recognitions.

Invariants

  • Soundness: outputs are non-dominated across given profiles.
  • Monotonicity: adding stricter preferences shrinks the Pareto set.

Proof

  • Uses: preference orders to compute non-domination.
  • Produces: Recognition Carrier marking Pareto-superior recognitions.
  • Shows that: multi-agent rankings can be combined via Pareto criteria.

title: Parse Command id: parse-command role: operator status: someday derived_from: computer/function/recognition inputs:

  • computer/library/type/mud-session outputs:
  • computer/library/type/mud-command
  • computer/type/computer-failure transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Parse Command

Parses line-oriented client input into a validated MUD Command.

Definition

  • Inputs: raw text from a MUD Session connection and session context (player/entity bindings).
  • Outputs: a MUD Command or Computer Failure.
  • Behaviour: tokenises input, checks grammar (verb/arguments), resolves targets against session/world recognitions, and emits a validated command; on syntax or resolution errors, emits a failure without mutating session state.

Invariants

  • Grammar correctness: only well-formed commands are emitted.
  • Referential integrity: command targets must be reachable from session context; else fail.
  • Purity: parsing failures do not mutate session/world state.

Proof

  • Derived from: Computer Recognition.
  • Uses: MUD Session as context.
  • Shows that: refining recognition to the MUD grammar yields executable commands while preserving kernel semantics and failure handling.

title: Path Compose Addressed id: path-compose-addressed role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Path Compose Addressed

Path composition with explicit addressing of subwindows/subsections (region selection).

Definition

  • Inputs: two composable paths in Directed Path with explicit source/target Hypertensor Windows (subwindows/subsections).
  • Output: composed path with region/address metadata preserved.
  • Behaviour: ensure endpoints match at the addressed regions; compose via Path Compose.

Invariants

  • Region correctness: composition valid only when regions align.
  • Address preservation: resulting path retains addressing info for further ops.

Proof

  • Uses: addressed window data and path composition.
  • Produces: composed directed path.
  • Shows that: path composition can be region-aware to mirror machine addressing.

title: Path Compose Runtime id: path-compose-runtime role: Compose parameterized paths in runtime memory using coverage runtime carrier. status: plan inputs:

  • id: coverage-runtime name: runtime outputs:
  • id: coverage-runtime name: runtime'
  • id: failure-carrier name: failure transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Path Compose Runtime

Kernel operator that executes distributed path composition using the Coverage Runtime Carrier and emits either a composite path payload or a FailureCarrier.

Definition

  • Inputs: two parameterized paths stored in runtime memory with aligned endpoints and cover identifiers, plus access to overlap caches and restriction engines.
  • Outputs: composed path written back into runtime memory with provenance tags, or a failure payload indicating mismatch/refinement issues.
  • Behaviour: reuses cached overlaps, invokes the discretized concatenation from the system layer, and logs matching-family provenance into the runtime ledger.

Preconditions

  • Coverage runtime is consistent; caches not invalidated.
  • Endpoint alignment and cover refinement have been confirmed or are checkable.

Postconditions

  • Successful compositions update overlap caches and matching logs atomically.
  • Failures leave runtime state unchanged aside from a recorded failure event.

Proof

  • Uses: cached overlaps and distributed path composition to perform runtime concatenation.
  • Shows that: composing paths in runtime yields updated coverage runtime or an explicit failure while maintaining runtime consistency.

title: Path Compose id: path-compose role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Path Compose

Concatenates directed paths when endpoints match.

Definition

  • Inputs: paths p: x → y, q: y → z in Directed Path.
  • Output: concatenated path q ∘ p: x → z modulo homotopy.

Invariants

  • Unital: composing with identity path yields the same class up to homotopy.
  • Associative up to homotopy (associator earned in the directed path/groupoid structure).
  • Directed: only composable when endpoints match in order.

Proof

Uses the directed-path structure to define concatenation and the groupoid data to supply associator/unit homotopies. Shows that: concatenation yields a well-defined morphism in the directed path/fundamental groupoid.

title: Path Composition Lift id: path-composition-lift role: operator status: plan derived_from: math/operator/path-compose inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Path Composition Lift

Parameterized lift of Path Compose that fixes interval discretizations, metrics, or step bounds so concatenation is programmable inside calculus flows.

Definition

  • Parameters: subdivision of the unit interval, tolerance for reparameterization, and a chosen metric on contexts so path concatenation remains well-formed.
  • Inputs: two parameterized paths (as parameterized sections/curves) with aligned endpoints and compatible step counts.
  • Outputs: a parameterized composite path following the concatenation then resampling to the fixed subdivision.
  • Behaviour: reindexes both inputs to the shared subdivision, concatenates, then contracts via homotopies encoded in the upstream math operator.

Invariants

  • Endpoint preservation: start/endpoints match those of the operands.
  • Subdivision stability: resampling respects monotonicity and does not create spurious backtracking.
  • Compatibility: composition commutes with restriction to subcovers and with path reversal when available.

Proof

  • Derived from: Path Compose by fixing computational parameters for intervals and metrics.
  • Shows that: the abstract homotopical composition can be executed in calculus settings where discretization and reparameterization are explicit.

title: Identify Path id: path-identity role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Identify Path

Provides the identity (constant) path at a recognition/context.

Definition

  • Inputs: a point/recognition x in the Recognition Carrier.
  • Outputs: identity path id_x: x → x in Directed Path.

Invariants

  • Unit: for any path p: x → y, p ∘ id_x = p and id_y ∘ p = p up to homotopy; unitors established in the directed path/groupoid structure.

Proof

Uses the directed-path structure to form constant paths and the groupoid data to supply unit homotopies. Shows that identity paths act as units for path composition in the directed path/fundamental groupoid.

title: Path Lift id: path-lift role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Path Lift

Lifts a path in a modality/profile tower to a path in a hypertensor section, completing the fibre-bundle analogy.

Definition

  • Inputs: a path in the Modality Tower or profile tower, and a Hypertensor Section over the base.
  • Output: a Directed Path in the section that projects to the given base path.
  • Behaviour: use sheaf restriction/gluing to construct section-level lifts for each step; compose via Path Compose.

Invariants

  • Lifting: projection of the lifted path equals the base path.
  • Compatibility: lifts respect sheaf/coverage structure and nucleus equivalence.
  • Holonomy readiness: lifted paths support holonomy/curvature measurements.

Proof

  • Uses: Modality Tower as base path carrier, Hypertensor Section as fibre, and Path Compose for composition.
  • Produces: Directed Path in the section.
  • Shows that: paths in base towers can be lifted to section-level paths, enabling holonomy analysis.

title: Permission Operator id: permission-operator role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Permission Operator

Recognises the actions/recognitions allowed under a norm regime.

Definition

  • Inputs: Norm Regime and a recognition.
  • Output: recognition collecting admissible refinements compatible with the regime’s discipline/filter.
  • Behaviour: project the input onto the admissible region of the norm regime.

Invariants

  • Soundness: outputs are always compliant.
  • Maximality: yields the largest admissible recognition below or compatible with the input.
  • Monotonicity: stronger regimes yield smaller permissions.

Proof

  • Uses: Norm Regime and Recognition Carrier.
  • Produces: Recognition Carrier element denoting permission.
  • Shows that: permissions are expressible as projections in the recognition lattice under the norm regime.

title: Persist Snapshot id: persist-snapshot role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: snapshot alias: snapshot outputs:
  • id: snapshot alias: persisted
  • id: failure-carrier alias: failure transformation:
  • call
    • write-snapshot
    • snapshot proof_refs:
  • snapshot
  • failure-carrier
  • write-snapshot

Persist Snapshot

Writes an encoded snapshot atomically to storage, returning the persisted snapshot or failure.

Proof

  • Uses: storage write primitive.
  • Shows that: persistence is explicit and failure surfaced.

title: Persist World id: persist-world role: operator status: someday derived_from: computer/function/storage inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Persist World

Saves or restores the MUD World State and associated MUD Transcript using the computer storage operator.

Definition

  • Inputs: current world state and transcript descriptor (id/hash).
  • Outputs: updated MUD Transcript after save, or restored world/transcript on load; otherwise a Computer Failure.
  • Behaviour:
    • Save: validate world/transcript integrity, invoke computer storage to persist snapshot + event log, return updated transcript id/hash.
    • Load: retrieve snapshot + log, verify integrity, reconstruct world state and transcript carriers; on failure, emit failure and leave state unchanged.

Invariants

  • Integrity: hashes/digests must match on save/load.
  • Fidelity: round-tripping world + transcript yields observationally equivalent state.
  • Failure purity: failures do not mutate world or transcript.

Proof

  • Derived from: Computer Storage.
  • Uses: MUD World State and MUD Transcript.
  • Shows that: refining computer storage with MUD payloads yields persistence suitable for the MUD server while preserving kernel semantics.

title: Plan Runbook id: plan-runbook role: operator status: someday derived_from: computer/function/wiring inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Plan Runbook

Builds or refreshes a program wiring plan from validated commands and session state.

Definition

  • Inputs: Program Command stream and Program Session.
  • Outputs: Program Wiring Plan or Computer Failure.
  • Behaviour: composes commands into an acyclic wiring plan over computer operators/carriers; emits failure when wiring/typing/ordering constraints are violated.

Invariants

  • Acyclic, well-typed plan respecting command constraints.
  • Failure purity: on failure, prior session/plan unchanged.
  • Deterministic: given the same command stream/session, yields the same plan or the same failure.
  • Failure modes: type errors (port/carrier mismatch), cycles, missing targets, policy violations.

Proof

  • Derived from: Computer Wiring.
  • Shows that: building runbooks from commands via computer wiring yields a reusable planning step for programs, with explicit failure handling.

title: Plan Schedule id: plan-schedule role: operator status: someday derived_from: computer/function/wiring inputs:

  • computer/library/type/calendar-event
  • computer/library/type/recurrence-rule
  • computer/library/type/timezone-profile outputs:
  • computer/library/type/calendar-schedule
  • computer/library/type/conflict-report
  • computer/type/computer-failure transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Plan Schedule

Builds/refreshes a calendar wiring plan respecting dependencies, recurrence, and constraints.

Definition

  • Inputs: Calendar Event set, Recurrence Rule, and Timezone Profile.
  • Outputs: Calendar Schedule, Conflict Report when conflicts detected, or Computer Failure on wiring errors.
  • Behaviour: expands recurrence, normalises times, computes dependencies, and assembles an acyclic wiring plan; emits conflicts/failures as appropriate.

Invariants

  • Acyclic, well-typed plan; all dependencies/recurrence expansions resolved.
  • Conflicts reported without committing invalid plans.
  • No partial plan mutation on failure.

Proof

  • Derived from: Computer Wiring.
  • Shows that: assembling calendar plans via computer wiring yields executable schedules; conflicts/failures are surfaced as explicit outputs.

title: Plug Frame id: plug-frame role: operator status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/build-evaluation-context inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Plug Frame

Operator plug : Frame × Term → Term inserting a term into the hole of an evaluation frame from EvaluationContext.

Statement

For any frame F and term t, plug(F, t) yields the program obtained by inserting t into the unique hole of F. For the neutral frame empty, plug(empty, t) = t.

Proof

Sketch: by induction on frames generated by BuildEvaluationContext, define plug structurally and show neutrality for empty from SeedEvaluationContext.

title: Policy Enforcement id: policy-enforce role: Check and enforce a policy regime over traces/ports/tasks. status: plan inputs:

  • id: policy-regime name: policy
  • id: trace-carrier name: trace outputs:
  • id: policy-regime name: policy'
  • id: failure-carrier name: failure transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Policy Enforcement

Checks and enforces policy regimes over traces/ports/tasks.

Behaviour

  • Detect violations of the policy against the trace.
  • If none, return the policy (possibly annotated); otherwise emit a failure.

Proof

  • Uses: violation detection and failure emission operators.
  • Shows that: enforcement reports violations via failure carriers without mutating state when violations occur.

title: Primitive Pop Evaluation Context id: primitive-pop-evaluation-context role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: evaluation-state alias: state outputs:
  • id: evaluation-state alias: updated transformation:
  • return
  • updated proof_refs:
  • evaluation-state
  • evaluation-context

Primitive Pop Evaluation Context

Pops the top frame from the evaluation context stack within an evaluation state.

Proof

  • Uses: stack operations on the state’s context_stack.
  • Shows that: popping frames is available as a primitive state update.

title: Pop Evaluation Context id: pop-evaluation-context role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: evaluation-state alias: state outputs:

    • stack
      • state
        • context_stack
    • updated_stack
      • pop
        • stack
    • return
      • state
        • context_stack
          • updated_stack proof_refs:
  • evaluation-state
  • evaluation-context
  • evaluation-context-stack

Pop Evaluation Context

Removes the top evaluation frame from the context stack inside an Evaluation State, returning the updated state.

Invariants

  • Removes exactly one frame from the head of the context stack.
  • Stack remains well-formed; no mutation beyond dropping the head.

title: Primitive Port Type Check id: primitive-port-type-check role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: port-set alias: ports outputs:
  • id: recognition alias: predicate transformation:
  • return
  • predicate proof_refs:
  • port-set
  • recognition

Primitive Port Type Check

Validates that each port’s carrier/direction matches its inducing operator signature.

Proof

  • Uses: port metadata and signatures.
  • Shows that: port typing can be decided as a primitive predicate.
