    • return
      • merged proof_refs:
  • recognition
  • glue-sections
  • union-window
  • merge-predicate
  • union-scope
  • ensure-flow-nucleus-compatibility

Recognition Merge

Merges two recognitions over possibly overlapping support into a single recognition with glued support/window, combined predicate/evidence/scope, and compatible flow/nucleus data.

Proof

  • Uses: section gluing, window/scope union, and predicate merge to combine recognitions while preserving locality and flow/nucleus compatibility.
  • Shows that: recognitions can be merged into a single carrier without violating sheaf or flow invariants.

title: Recognition Pullback id: recognition-pullback role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Recognition Pullback

Pulls back a recognition/subobject along a morphism in the relational sheaf topos.

Definition

  • Inputs: a morphism f: X → Y and a subobject/recognition on Y expressed via the Recognition Classifier.
  • Outputs: the pullback recognition/subobject on X.
  • Behaviour: composes the characteristic map with f to obtain the pulled-back recognition.

Invariants

  • Functorial: pullback respects composition/identity.
  • Commutative with pushforward on base-change squares (via Base Change Square).

Proof

  • Uses: pullback structure in the Relational Sheaf Topos and the recognition classifier.
  • Shows that: recognitions/subobjects can be transported along morphisms while preserving classifier semantics.

title: Recognition Pushforward id: recognition-pushforward role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Recognition Pushforward

Pushes forward a recognition/subobject along a morphism in the relational sheaf topos.

Definition

  • Inputs: a morphism f: X → Y and a subobject/recognition on X expressed via the Recognition Classifier.
  • Outputs: the direct image recognition/subobject on Y.
  • Behaviour: constructs the smallest recognition on Y whose pullback along f contains the given recognition.

Invariants

  • Functorial: pushforward respects composition/identity.
  • Commutative with pullback on base-change squares (via Base Change Square).

Proof

  • Uses: pushforward/direct-image structure in the Relational Sheaf Topos and the recognition classifier.
  • Shows that: recognitions/subobjects can be transported forward along morphisms while preserving classifier semantics.

title: Recognition Truth id: recognition-truth role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: recognition alias: predicate outputs:
  • id: runtime-value alias: truth transformation:
  • return
    • truth proof_refs:
  • recognition
  • runtime-value

Recognition Truth

Determines whether a recognition predicate is top (true) or bottom (false) and returns a runtime truth value suitable for branching.

Proof

  • Uses: ordering on the recognition lattice.
  • Shows that: truth detection is available as a primitive observation of recognitions.

title: Recognize Context id: recognize-context role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Recognize Context (Calculus)

Definition

  • Inputs: Recognition Carrier context, Policy Regime, Trace Carrier.
  • Outputs: Recognition Carrier with recognized context/state/policy/trace.
  • Behaviour: refines Recognize Context with explicit policy and trace parameters so context recognition is reproducible and traceable.

Invariants

  • Recognition results respect the calculus policy/trace parameters.
  • Stable under reapplication with the same parameters (extend trace/refine policy, payload/state fixed).

Proof

  • Uses: Recognition Carrier, Policy Regime, and Trace Carrier to parameterize the recognition.
  • Shows that: recognition of contexts with explicit policy/trace inputs yields earned recognition carrier elements stable under reapplication with the same parameters.

title: Recognize Event id: recognize-event role: operator status: someday derived_from: computer/function/recognition inputs:

  • computer/library/type/calendar-event
  • computer/library/type/timezone-profile
  • computer/library/type/recurrence-rule outputs:
  • computer/library/type/calendar-event
  • computer/type/computer-failure transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Recognize Event

Recognises a calendar event specification into a validated calendar event carrier.

Definition

  • Inputs: Calendar Event spec with time, recurrence, timezone, constraints.
  • Outputs: validated Calendar Event or Computer Failure.
  • Behaviour: checks time bounds, applies Timezone Profile, validates Recurrence Rule; emits failure if inconsistent.

Invariants

  • Time/recurrence references must resolve; invalid specs emit failure.
  • No state mutation on failure.

Proof

  • Derived from: Computer Recognition.
  • Shows that: calendar event specs can be validated as recognitions using computer operators, producing event carriers or failures.

title: Recognize Judgement Triad id: recognize-judgement-triad role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Recognize Judgement Triad (Calculus)

Definition

  • Inputs: Recognition Carrier context, Policy Regime, Trace Carrier.
  • Outputs: Recognition Carrier with recognized judgement triad.
  • Behaviour: refines Recognize Judgement Triad with explicit policy/trace parameters to make judgement recognition reproducible and traceable.

Invariants

  • Soundness, idempotence, and policy monotonicity inherited from the math operator, respecting calculus parameters.

Proof

  • Uses: Recognition Carrier plus Policy Regime and Trace Carrier to parameterize judgement recognition.
  • Shows that: judgement recognition with explicit policy/trace parameters produces earned recognition carrier elements and preserves soundness, idempotence, and policy monotonicity.

title: Recognize Observation id: recognize-observation role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Recognize Observation (Calculus)

Definition

  • Inputs: Recognition Carrier context, Policy Regime, Trace Carrier.
  • Outputs: Recognition Carrier with recognized observation.
  • Behaviour: refines Recognize Observation with explicit policy/trace parameters so observation recognition is reproducible and traceable.

Invariants

  • Soundness, idempotence, and policy monotonicity inherited from the math operator, respecting calculus parameters.

Proof

  • Uses: Recognition Carrier, Policy Regime, and Trace Carrier.
  • Shows that: observation recognition with explicit policy/trace parameters produces earned recognition carrier elements while preserving soundness, idempotence, and policy monotonicity.

title: Recognize Section id: recognize-section role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Recognize Section

Math-level recognition of a hypertensor section, mirroring machine recognition.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Hypertensor Section.
  • Output: recognition result in the Recognition Carrier.
  • Behaviour: apply recognition operator to the section, producing semantic recognitions.

Invariants

  • Soundness: recognition reflects section content.
  • Stability: recognition respects sheaf/flow constraints.

Proof

  • Uses: recognition semantics on sections.
  • Produces: recognition output.
  • Shows that: recognition is definable at the math level for sections.

title: Recognize Spec id: recognize-spec role: operator status: someday derived_from: computer/function/recognition inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Recognize Spec

Normalises a program-level specification into a validated Program Command.

Definition

  • Inputs: program command spec (verb, targets, parameters).
  • Outputs: validated Program Command or Computer Failure.
  • Behaviour: checks syntax (verb/parameters present), resolves target references, applies policy/trace constraints; emits failure on invalid/forbidden specs.

Invariants

  • Layer-safe: commands respect layer/policy constraints.
  • No state mutation on failure.
  • Deterministic: same spec yields the same normalised command.

Proof

  • Derived from: Computer Recognition.
  • Shows that: program specs can be validated via computer recognition into reusable program-command carriers.

title: Reduce Interleaving id: reduce-interleaving role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Reduce Interleaving

Applies a reduction step to an interleaved flow represented by a shuffle class.

Definition

  • Inputs: an Interleaving Reduction state.
  • Output: updated reduction state with one small-step applied.
  • Behaviour: choose a permissible interleaving step, apply it, and update the shuffle class state.

Invariants

  • Shuffle-invariance: reductions respect shuffle equivalence.
  • Termination: reaches normal form when no steps remain.

Proof

  • Uses: reduction rules from the interleaving reduction structure.
  • Produces: next reduction state.
  • Shows that: interleaved flows can be evaluated via small-step reductions.

title: Reducible Substitution tags:

  • normalization
  • reducibility layer: internal-theorem status: plan depends_on:
  • math/structure/reducible-environment
  • acts/select-reducibility-candidate
  • acts/substitute-reducibility
  • acts/judge-typing claims:
  • type: operator judgement: substitute_reducible : ReducibleEnvironment × Term ⇀ ReducibleEnvironment preserves reducibility

Reducible Substitution

Statement

The earned acts select-reducibility-candidate and substitute-reducibility show that substituting typed reducible terms into a ReducibleEnvironment preserves reducibility.

Proof sketch

BuildReducibleEnvironment seeds the logical relation. The selection act chooses the candidate relation for each term, and SubstituteReducibility = (BuildReducibleEnvironment JudgeTyping) certifies that typed substitutions stay reducible.

Uniqueness

Given the earned environment and substitution acts, reducible substitution is fixed up to nucleus equivalence.

title: Reindexing id: reindexing role: operator status: plan derived_from: acts/substitute-reducibility inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Reindexing

Definition

  • Inputs: a morphism in the Index Base and a judgemental object in the source Fiber Category.
  • Outputs: the pullback of that judgement along the base morphism as a judgemental object in the target fibre.
  • Behaviour: restricts substitution along a context morphism, transporting recognised terms, types, and judgements while preserving the typing and entailment guarantees earned by the substitution reducibility act.

Invariants

  • Functorial: pulling back along identity morphisms is identity on fibres; pullbacks along composed morphisms compose accordingly.
  • Type- and entailment-preserving: transported judgements remain well-typed and maintain the reducibility guarantees from SubstituteReducibility.

Proof

  • Derived from: SubstituteReducibility, which shows substitution preserves typing and entailment.
  • Shows that: restricting substitution along a base morphism defines a functor between fibre categories, satisfying identity and composition laws and preserving recognised judgemental structure.

title: Relational Bayes Update id: relational-bayes-update role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Relational Bayes Update

Probabilistic/entropy-based update of a Bayesian profile.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Bayesian Profile and an Observation.
  • Output: updated Bayesian Profile with reweighted recognitions.
  • Behaviour: reweight recognitions using the observation likelihood; renormalise and recompute entropy; optionally collapse with fixed-invariant semantics.

Invariants

  • Normalisation: posterior probabilities sum to 1.
  • Entropy decrease: expected entropy does not increase under informative observations.

Proof

  • Uses: Bayesian Profile, Observation, and Entropy Functional.
  • Produces: updated Bayesian Profile.
  • Shows that: Bayes-like updates fit within the relational epistemic framework.

title: Relational Free and Quotient id: relational-free-and-quotient role: operator status: plan derived_from: [] inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Relational Free and Quotient

This operator will earn free constructions and quotients in the relational setting, mapping generators and relations to earned carriers. It is currently a stub pending explicit acts for introducing generators, imposing relations, and checking quotient laws.

Proof

  • TODO: fill proof.

title: Relational Stochastic Dynamics id: relational-stochastic-dynamics role: operator status: plan derived_from: [] inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Relational Stochastic Dynamics

Relational Stochastic Dynamics will earn transition kernels, chains, and fixed points over earned recognitions. It awaits explicit acts for constructing stochastic kernels, ensuring row-stochasticity, and relating fixed chains to underlying dynamics operators.

Proof

  • TODO: fill proof.

title: Rescale Section id: rescale-section role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Rescale Section

Rescales a section across levels of the scale tower, propagating flow at each scale.

Definition

  • Inputs: Scale Tower and a Hypertensor Section at some level.
  • Output: the section transported to another scale level.
  • Behaviour: restrict or extend along scale transitions, applying flow/nucleus appropriate to each level.

Invariants

  • Compatibility: rescaling respects coverage/sheaf structure at each scale.
  • Stability: rescaling then rescaling back yields nucleus-equivalent section.

Proof

  • Uses: scale tower rescalings and section sheaf structure.
  • Produces: rescaled section.
  • Shows that: sections can be coherently moved across scales.

title: Resolve Operator Signature id: resolve-operator-signature role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: operator-id alias: target outputs:
  • id: operator-signature alias: signature transformation:
  • call
    • lookup-signature
    • target proof_refs:
  • operator-id
  • operator-signature

Resolve Operator Signature

Returns the Operator Signature associated with a canonical operator id. This is the dispatcher’s entry point for arity and type checking.

Proof

  • Uses: the registry of operator signatures keyed by operator-id.
  • Shows that: every operator id can be mapped to its typing evidence before any invocation is attempted.

title: Restrict Compose Law id: restrict-compose-law role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Restrict Compose Law

Witnesses functorial composition of restrictions in the hypertensor sheaf.

Definition

  • Inputs: inclusions W→V→U in Hypertensor Site.
  • Output: law res_W^U = res_W^V ∘ res_V^U stored in Sheaf Restriction Algebra.
  • Behaviour: constructs the composite and records equality.

Invariants

  • Associativity of composition of restrictions.
  • Identity restrictions act as units.

Proof

  • Uses: sheaf functoriality on the hypertensor site.
  • Produces: restriction law witness.
  • Shows that: restriction composition obeys functorial equations.

title: Restrict Naturality Law id: restrict-naturality-law role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Restrict Naturality Law

Witnesses naturality of restrictions with respect to pullbacks in the hypertensor site.

Definition

  • Inputs: pullback square in Hypertensor Site with windows U,V,U',V' and a section over U.
  • Output: equality of pulled-back restrictions stored in Sheaf Restriction Algebra.
  • Behaviour: show f^* ∘ res = res ∘ f^* for the square.

Invariants

  • Commutation of pullback and restriction on the sheaf.
  • Stability under refinement of the square.

Proof

  • Uses: sheaf naturality and base-change properties on the site.
  • Produces: naturality witness.
  • Shows that: restriction behaves naturally with site morphisms.

title: Retrace Context id: retrace-context role: operator status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/retrace-context inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Retrace Context

Operator RetraceContext pulling recognised contexts back along the trace.

Proof

Sketch: RetraceContext = (RecognizeContext ContextualizeTrace) pulls recognitions back; composites with TraceContext reduce to identity inside the nucleus.

title: Return id: return role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: runtime-value alias: value outputs:
  • id: runtime-value alias: value transformation:
  • call
  • identity
  • value proof_refs:
  • identity
  • runtime-value

Return

Halts evaluation of the current branch and yields the given value to the interpreter via the identity transformation.

Proof

  • Uses: identity to pass the value through unchanged.
  • Shows that: return is expressible without primitive opcodes; it is the identity on runtime values.

title: Revision Operator id: revision-operator role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Revision Operator

AGM-style belief revision in relational form.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Belief Base and an Observation.
  • Output: updated Belief Base incorporating the observation.
  • Behaviour: add the observation, remove minimal conflicting beliefs, and close under inference/nucleus.

Invariants

  • Success: observation is in the revised base (unless inconsistent).
  • Minimal change: removes as little as possible to restore consistency (per chosen preorder).
  • Closure: revised base is closed under earned inference.

Proof

  • Uses: Belief Base and Observation.
  • Produces: revised Belief Base.
  • Shows that: belief revision can be expressed via recognitions consistent with modal/logic structure.

title: RG Flow id: rg-flow role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

RG Flow

Iterates renormalisation steps toward fixed points.

Definition

  • Inputs: initial Scale Window and section.
  • Outputs: candidate RG Fixed Point or an evolving sequence approaching one.
  • Behaviour: repeatedly apply RG Step along coarser embeddings until convergence or cycle; collect fixed-point witnesses.

Invariants

  • Monotone coarse-graining: each step reduces scale detail.
  • Convergence: if a fixed point exists, iteration stabilises up to nucleus equivalence.

Proof

  • Uses: RG Step iteratively.
  • Produces: RG Fixed Point when convergence occurs.
  • Shows that: RG iteration is a well-defined flow toward large-scale phases.

title: RG Step id: rg-step role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

RG Step

Renormalisation step coarse-graining a section from finer to coarser scale.

Definition

  • Inputs: a fine-scale Scale Window with a Hypertensor Section.
  • Outputs: a coarser Scale Window with a coarse-grained section.
  • Behaviour: restrict to fine window via Scale Embedding, apply Measure Fixed Invariant or nucleus to stabilise, then Coarse-Grain Section to the coarse window.

Invariants

  • Fixed-shape preservation: fixed shapes are preserved under RG; couplings may change.
  • Monotonicity: coarse-graining does not invent new fine-scale detail.

Proof

  • Uses: Scale Window, Hypertensor Section, and Measure Fixed Invariant.
  • Produces: coarse Scale Window section.
  • Shows that: renormalisation respects earned invariants and flows toward coarse descriptions.

title: Rollback id: rollback role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Rollback

Rollback the current relational state to a given time slice.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Time Slice and access to Load Context.
  • Output: restored Hypertensor Section consistent with the saved memory carrier.
  • Behaviour: load saved contexts/recognitions from the slice, replace current section, and discard morphisms that do not factor through the slice.

Invariants

  • Causality preservation: no illegal cross-time references survive rollback.
  • Fidelity: restored section is nucleus-equivalent to the saved slice.

Proof

  • Uses: Time Slice and Load Context.
  • Produces: Hypertensor Section.
  • Shows that: rollback reconstructs an earlier state without violating earned causality constraints.

title: Run Transformation id: run-transformation role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: operator-id alias: target
  • id: argument-list alias: args outputs:
  • id: runtime-value alias: result transformation:
  • return
    • result proof_refs:
  • operator-id
  • argument-list
  • runtime-value

Run Transformation

Runs the documented transformation for an operator id on validated arguments.

Proof

  • Uses: interpreter’s transformation table.
  • Shows that: execution is provided directly from documented transformations once dispatch and validation succeed.

title: Sanction Flow id: sanction-flow role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Sanction Flow

Flows contexts under a norm regime, suppressing or redirecting recognitions that violate the regime.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Norm Regime, a Context, and the earned Flow Context operator.
  • Output: transformed Context with norm-violating recognitions mapped to bottom or redirected per the discipline.
  • Behaviour: compose context flow with the norm predicate; filter or rewrite recognitions that fail the regime before continuing evaluation.

Invariants

  • Soundness: compliant recognitions are preserved; violators are suppressed or redirected consistently.
  • Monotonicity: strengthening the regime makes the flow at least as restrictive.
  • Compatibility: commutes with earned nucleus/flow laws on contexts.

Proof

  • Uses: Norm Regime to decide admissibility and Flow Context to transport contexts.
  • Produces: filtered Context.
  • Shows that: normative policies can be enforced as part of context flow without breaking earned flow/nucleus structure.

title: Save Persistent Context tags:

  • storage
  • context layer: internal-theorem status: plan depends_on:
  • math/structure/persistent-context-carrier
  • math/structure/persistent-recognition
  • acts/baseline-limit claims:
  • type: operator judgement: save_context : PersistentContextCarrier → (PersistentContext, BaselineRemainder, PersistentRecognitionCarrier)

Save Persistent Context

Statement

There exists an operator

save_context : PersistentContextCarrier → (PersistentContext, BaselineRemainder, PersistentRecognitionCarrier)

that packages a persistent context with its baseline remainder and fixed recognitions.

Definition by acts

  • Takes a context in PersistentContextCarrier and extracts its baseline remainder via baseline-limit.
  • Collects fixed recognitions from PersistentRecognitionCarrier.
  • Emits the triple (PersistentContext, BaselineRemainder, PersistentRecognitionCarrier).

Uniqueness

Because PersistentContextCarrier/PersistentRecognitionCarrier and the baseline are fixed by the earning acts, any operator respecting these yields the same triple up to nucleus equivalence; thus save_context is unique in the earned sense.

title: Save Section Snapshot id: save-section-snapshot role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Save Section Snapshot

Math-level operator mirroring save-snapshot: captures and stores a section state.

Definition

  • Inputs: current Hypertensor Section, State Transition metadata.
  • Output: persisted section encoding and/or marked checkpoint in the section.
  • Behaviour: apply Store Section to capture state; record transition metadata for future restoration.

Invariants

  • Fidelity: saved snapshot corresponds to current section state.
  • Restorability: can be reloaded to an equivalent section via load.

Proof

  • Uses: store/transition to capture state.
  • Produces: saved snapshot artefact.
  • Shows that: snapshotting can be formalised on sections for machine alignment.

title: Save Snapshot id: save-snapshot role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: recognition alias: recognitions
  • id: evaluation-state alias: eval-state outputs:
  • id: snapshot alias: snapshot
  • id: failure-carrier alias: failure transformation:
  • let
    • descriptor-ok
      • call
        • validate-snapshot-descriptor
        • eval-state
    • content
      • call
        • collect-snapshot-state
        • recognitions
        • eval-state
    • encoded
      • call
        • encode-snapshot
        • content
    • persisted
      • call
        • persist-snapshot
        • encoded
    • return
      • persisted proof_refs:
  • recognition
  • evaluation-state
  • snapshot
  • failure-carrier
  • validate-snapshot-descriptor
  • collect-snapshot-state
  • encode-snapshot
  • persist-snapshot

Save Snapshot

Persists recognitions and evaluation state into a durable Snapshot, or emits a Failure Carrier on error.

Proof

  • Uses: validation, collection, encoding, and persistence operators to assemble a snapshot.
  • Shows that: snapshot persistence is expressed entirely via earned operators over earned carriers; failures are explicit.

title: Schedule Next Step id: schedule-next-step role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: evaluation-state alias: state
  • id: wiring-plan alias: plan outputs:
  • id: evaluation-state alias: updated transformation:
  • call
    • primitive-schedule-next-step
    • state
    • plan proof_refs:
  • evaluation-state
  • wiring-plan
  • primitive-schedule-next-step

Schedule Next Step

Removes the head of the ready queue in an Evaluation State, checks its input bindings from the Wiring Plan, and marks it in-flight for execution.

Proof

  • Uses: ready queue and plan topology to pick a ready step.
  • Shows that: scheduling advances deterministically or produces failure if no ready steps remain.

title: Primitive Schedule Next Step id: primitive-schedule-next-step role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: evaluation-state alias: state
  • id: wiring-plan alias: plan outputs:
  • id: evaluation-state alias: updated transformation:
  • return
  • updated proof_refs:
  • evaluation-state
  • wiring-plan

Primitive Schedule Next Step

Primitive scheduler that dequeues the next ready step, marks it in-flight, and updates queues/bindings accordingly.

Proof

  • Uses: ready queue and dependency data in the plan.
  • Shows that: scheduling is available as a kernel primitive.

title: Schedule Tasks id: schedule-tasks role: Derive an execution order from a wiring/connection graph and task carrier. status: plan inputs:

  • id: task-carrier name: tasks
  • id: connection-graph name: graph outputs:
  • id: task-carrier name: scheduled transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Schedule Tasks

System-level operator that extracts tasks from a runbook/wiring plan and produces an execution order respecting dependencies.

Behaviour

  • Extract tasks: map each step in a connection graph to a Task with metadata (priority, cost).
  • Build task graph: derive edges from wiring constraints and trace semantics.
  • Produce schedule: linearise tasks respecting dependency order and trace spine coherence.

Invariants

  • Execution order respects dependency graph and kernel invariants.
  • Scheduler does not introduce cycles.

Proof

  • Uses: Task Carrier and Connection Graph to derive a precedence relation from wiring and trace constraints, then linearises it.
  • Shows that: scheduling derived from the graph preserves dependency invariants.

title: Section Flow and Nucleus id: section-flow-nucleus role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Section Flow and Nucleus

Flow and nucleus acts specialise to section operators

flow_section    : Section ⟶ Section
nucleus_section : Section ⟶ Section

with commutation up to earned equivalence by commute-flow-and-nucleus.

  • flow acts on sections to evolve them.
  • nucleate contracts sections to nuclei.

These operators keep sections within the earned sheaf and satisfy the earned commutation law.

Definition

  • Inputs/Outputs: Quasicrystalline Hypertensor Sheaf Section.
  • Behaviour: apply flow and nucleus to sections with commutation up to earned equivalence: flow ∘ nucleus ≃ nucleus ∘ flow.

Invariants

  • Flow–nucleus commutation holds on sections.
  • Sections remain within the earned sheaf; no new carriers introduced.

Proof

  • Uses: Commute Flow and Nucleus to justify the commutation law on sections.
  • Shows that: lifting flow/nucleus to sections preserves the commutation law and keeps sections within the sheaf.

title: Sheaf Restrict and Glue id: section-restrict-glue role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: coverage-site alias: site
  • id: matching-family alias: family outputs:
  • id: hypertensor-section alias: glued
  • id: failure-carrier alias: failure transformation:
  • let
    • valid
      • call
        • check-matching-family
        • site
        • family
    • glued
      • call
        • sheaf-glue
        • site
        • valid
    • return
      • glued proof_refs:
  • coverage-site
  • matching-family
  • sheaf-glue
  • check-matching-family

Sheaf Restrict and Glue

Earned sheaf acts provide the section operators:

restrict : Section × Window_sub ⟶ Section_sub
glue     : {s_i on U_i compatible} ⟶ Section
  • presheaf supplies restriction maps over admissible windows.
  • sheaf-condition and sheafification earn the gluing operation for compatible local sections.

These are the canonical section operators for the quasicrystalline hypertensor sheaf.

Definition

  • Inputs: Quasicrystalline Hypertensor Sheaf Section and admissible windows/subcovers.
  • Outputs: restricted sections on subwindows and glued sections from compatible families.
  • Behaviour: apply restriction maps and glue compatible local sections to yield global sections, respecting sheaf axioms.

Invariants

  • Sheaf condition holds: glued sections restrict to the originals; uniqueness of glued sections up to earned equivalence.
  • Restriction/glue commute with flow–nucleus dynamics.

Proof

  • Uses: Coverage Site covers/restrictions, Hypertensor Site as the underlying site, check-matching-family to validate compatibility, and sheaf-glue to assemble the section.
  • Shows that: applying restriction/glue to quasicrystalline sections preserves sheaf axioms and flow–nucleus dynamics on the section.

title: Self-Directed Operation id: self-directed-operation role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Self-Directed Operation

Executes an evaluation step initiated internally (e.g., maintenance tasks) under explicit policy/trace guardrails.

Definition

  • Inputs: Evaluation State, Wiring Plan marking eligible self-directed steps, ambient Policy Regime, and Trace Carrier.
  • Outputs: updated evaluation state or Failure Algebra.
  • Behaviour: selects permitted internal steps (maintenance, scans), executes them via evaluate-step semantics, and records failures when guardrails are violated.

Invariants

  • Only whitelisted internal steps may execute; respects policy/trace.
  • Failure purity: failures leave state unchanged.
  • Auditable: actions recorded in evaluation state/plan bindings.

Proof

  • Uses: Evaluate Computer Step semantics restricted to whitelisted internal tasks and guardrails from the policy/trace.
  • Shows that: executing only permitted internal steps preserves evaluation invariants; violations are captured in the failure algebra without mutating state.

title: Self-Witnessing Operator id: self-witnessing-operator role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Self-Witnessing Operator

Generates recognitions that witness the profile witnessing itself; fixed points approximate reflexive awareness.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Reflexive Profile and the Nucleus operator.
  • Output: updated Reflexive Profile containing self-witnessing recognitions.
  • Behaviour: apply nucleus to self-referential recognitions to stabilise self-witnessing; iterate to approach fixed points.

Invariants

  • Reflexivity: results remain self-referential.
  • Stability: fixed points represent stable self-awareness states.

Proof

  • Uses: Reflexive Profile and Nucleus.
  • Produces: refined Reflexive Profile.
  • Shows that: self-witnessing is expressible within the earned logic/nucleus framework.

title: Semantic Collapse id: semantic-collapse role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Semantic Collapse

Collapses a logical formula/recognition expression to its recognition semantics, eliminating syntactic redundancy.

Definition

  • Inputs: recognition expression in the Recognition Carrier.
  • Output: semantic recognition after applying lattice/logic laws to remove redundancy.
  • Behaviour: normalise using meets/joins/implication/negation and nucleus to produce a canonical semantic form.

Invariants

  • Idempotent: repeated collapse yields the same semantic recognition.
  • Soundness: semantics preserved; syntax removed.

Proof

  • Uses: Recognition Carrier and Logic Algebra.
  • Produces: canonical semantic recognition.
  • Shows that: syntactic forms can be reduced to earned semantics.

title: Serialize Port id: serialize-port role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Serialize Port

Serializes/deserializes recognitions for transmission through ports.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Ports Carrier and recognitions to send/receive.
  • Output: same carrier with serialized payloads conforming to Section Encoding Schema.
  • Behaviour: encode recognitions for outbound ports; decode inbound payloads.

Invariants

  • Round-trip fidelity for encodable recognitions.
  • Schema compliance enforced.

Proof

  • Uses: encoding schema for deterministic serialization.
  • Produces: port-ready payloads.
  • Shows that: ports can safely carry encoded recognitions.

title: Set Binding id: set-binding role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: evaluation-state alias: state
  • id: runtime-value alias: name
  • id: runtime-value alias: value outputs:
  • id: evaluation-state alias: updated transformation:
  • let
    • env
      • state
        • bindings
    • new_env
      • extend
        • env
        • name
        • value
    • return
      • state
        • bindings
          • new_env proof_refs:
  • evaluation-state
  • binding-environment
  • runtime-value

Set Binding

Returns a new Evaluation State whose binding environment maps name to value (shadowing any prior binding).

Invariants

  • Functional update: produces a new state; original bindings remain unchanged.
  • Shadowing: new binding for name overwrites prior mapping while preserving others.

title: Sheaf Glue id: sheaf-glue role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: coverage-site alias: site
  • id: matching-family alias: family outputs:
  • id: hypertensor-section alias: section transformation:
  • let
    • valid
      • call
      • check-matching-family
      • site
      • family
    • return
      • call
      • construct-glued-section
      • valid proof_refs:
  • hypertensor-site
  • matching-family
  • check-matching-family
  • construct-glued-section

Glue Sheaf

Glues a matching family of sections over a cover of windows into a unique global section, enforcing the sheaf condition.

Definition

  • Inputs:
    • A cover of a window by sub-windows in Hypertensor Site.
    • A Matching Family of sections in Hypertensor Section agreeing on overlaps.
  • Output: a unique global section over the covered window in Hypertensor Section.
  • Behaviour:
    1. Check matching on pairwise overlaps.
    2. Construct the amalgamated section.
    3. Verify uniqueness up to earned equivalence.

Invariants

  • Locality: if two glued sections agree on all sub-windows of a cover, they are equal.
  • Gluing: every matching family admits a unique amalgamation.
  • Compatibility: respects restriction maps of the Sheaf.

Proof

  • Uses: Hypertensor Site coverage and restriction structure plus Matching Family witnesses; validation via check-matching-family; construction via construct-glued-section.
  • Shows that: the earned sheaf condition holds by producing unique amalgams from matching families over covers.

title: Sheaf Locality id: sheaf-locality role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: hypertensor-site alias: site
  • id: hypertensor-section alias: section-a
  • id: hypertensor-section alias: section-b outputs:
  • id: recognition alias: equality-witness transformation:
  • call
  • compare-section-restrictions
  • site
  • section-a
  • section-b proof_refs:
  • hypertensor-site
  • compare-section-restrictions

Sheaf Locality

Witnesses the sheaf locality property: if two sections agree on all members of a cover, they are equal.

Definition

  • Inputs: two sections over a window U in the Hypertensor Site.
  • Output: a recognition witness that they are equal if all restrictions to a cover of U are equal.
  • Behaviour: restrict both sections along a cover and compare; if all restrictions coincide, produce an equality witness globally.

Invariants

  • Locality: agreement on a cover implies global equality.
  • Stable under refinement of covers.

Proof

  • Uses: cover restrictions in the Hypertensor Site and comparison via compare-section-restrictions.
  • Shows that: sections are determined by their restrictions to a cover, earning the locality half of the sheaf condition.

title: Snapshot Linked Data id: snapshot-linked-data role: operator status: someday derived_from: computer/function/storage inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Snapshot Linked Data

Persists or restores a Linked Data Graph via computer storage.

Definition

  • Inputs: graph reference and snapshot descriptor.
  • Outputs: saved/restored Linked Data Graph or Computer Failure.
  • Behaviour: serialize graph with provenance/hashes; on restore, verify integrity and reconstruct the graph; emit failure on integrity/policy errors.

Invariants

  • Integrity: hashes/provenance must verify on save/load.
  • Fidelity: round-trips yield equivalent graphs.
  • Failure purity: failures do not mutate graph state.

Proof

  • Derived from: Computer Storage, specialised to linked-data graphs with provenance.

title: Snapshot Plan id: snapshot-plan role: operator status: someday derived_from: computer/function/storage inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Snapshot Plan

Persists or restores a program wiring plan and session via computer storage.

Definition

  • Inputs: Program Wiring Plan and snapshot descriptor.
  • Outputs: Program Snapshot, Program Transcript, or Computer Failure.
  • Behaviour: serializes plan/session to snapshot; on restore, verifies integrity and reconstructs plan/session; emits failure on integrity errors.

Invariants

  • Integrity: snapshot/transcript hashes must verify.
  • Fidelity: save/load round-trips yield equivalent plan/session.
  • Failure purity: failures do not mutate plan/session.
  • Failure modes: integrity verification failure, missing snapshot descriptor, incompatible plan/session state.

Proof

  • Derived from: Computer Storage.
  • Shows that: persisting/restoring program plans via computer storage preserves semantics and provides reusable persistence for runbooks.

title: Snapshot Protocol id: snapshot-protocol role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Snapshot Protocol

Relational analogue of Chandy–Lamport: assembles a consistent global snapshot from markers and channel states.

Definition

  • Inputs: marker-capable Message Channels, Snapshot Markers, and local Persistent Context Carriers.
  • Output: a Memory Carrier capturing local contexts and in-flight channel states.
  • Behaviour: inject markers, record local contexts when marker first seen, record Channel State between sends/receives, assemble into a consistent snapshot.

Invariants

  • Consistency: no partial receive without corresponding send in recorded state.
  • Termination: snapshot collection completes under finite channels.

Proof

  • Uses: markers, channel states, and persistent contexts to collect a global snapshot.
  • Produces: Memory Carrier.
  • Shows that: distributed snapshot can be captured recognitionally without violating causality.

title: Snapshot Schedule id: snapshot-schedule role: operator status: someday derived_from: computer/function/storage inputs:

  • computer/library/type/calendar-schedule outputs:
  • computer/type/computer-snapshot
  • computer/type/computer-failure transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Snapshot Schedule

Persists or restores a calendar schedule via the computer storage operator.

Definition

  • Inputs: Calendar Schedule and snapshot descriptor.
  • Outputs: saved Computer Snapshot or restored schedule, or Computer Failure.
  • Behaviour: serialize schedule to snapshot; on restore, verify integrity and reconstruct schedule.

Invariants

  • Integrity: snapshot hash/metadata must verify.
  • Fidelity: save/load round-trips yield equivalent schedules.
  • Failure purity: failures do not mutate schedule.

Proof

  • Derived from: Computer Storage.
  • Shows that: persisting/restoring schedules via computer storage preserves kernel semantics and schedule integrity.

title: Sniff Endpoints id: sniff-endpoints role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Sniff Endpoints

Discovers and validates exposed endpoints/ports in a computer wiring plan.

Definition

  • Inputs: Wiring Plan and ambient Policy Regime.
  • Outputs: annotated wiring plan with endpoint metadata, or Failure Algebra element on invalid/unknown endpoints.
  • Behaviour: traverses the plan to identify source/sink ports, validates their types/policies, and annotates them; emits failure for mismatches or missing bindings.

Invariants

  • Read-only: does not alter plan topology beyond annotations.
  • Typing/policy enforced; failures surfaced explicitly.

Proof

  • Uses: Recognition of endpoint port metadata against the policy regime to decide validity.
  • Shows that: identifying and validating endpoints is a read-only analysis of the wiring plan, producing annotations or failures without altering topology.

title: Social Flow id: social-flow role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Social Flow

Evolves agents’ epistemic and norm profiles under message exchange.

Definition

  • Inputs: a set of Agents and Message Channels carrying observations.
  • Output: updated agents with refined epistemic profiles (and potential norm reactions) after processing channel observations.
  • Behaviour: for each channel, push observations through Epistemic Update of sender/receiver; apply Flow Context jointly to advance contexts; optionally apply norm violation checks.

Invariants

  • Causality: respects channel ordering; no observation is applied out of order.
  • Commutation: epistemic updates and norms commute with joint flow when admissible.
  • Stability: agents remain within admissible recognitions/norm regimes.

Proof

  • Uses: Agent, Message Channel, Epistemic Update, and Flow Context to define the evolution.
  • Produces: updated Agent states.
  • Shows that: message exchange refines epistemic/normative state while respecting earned flow/nucleus structure.

title: Stabilize Plan id: stabilize-plan role: operator status: someday derived_from: computer/function/evaluate-step inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Stabilize Plan

Adjusts a program wiring plan to resolve conflicts and maintain invariants.

Definition

  • Inputs: Program Wiring Plan.
  • Outputs: updated Program Wiring Plan or Computer Failure.
  • Behaviour: rewrites the plan (reorder, adjust edges) to satisfy typing, dependency, and policy constraints; emits failure if stabilization cannot be achieved.

Invariants

  • Resulting plan remains acyclic/well-typed.
  • Failure purity: on failure, original plan remains unchanged.
  • Failure modes: unresolved conflicts, policy violations, attempts to introduce cycles or type errors.

Proof

  • Derived from: Computer Evaluate Step.
  • Shows that: stabilising plans via evaluation yields a corrected plan or explicit failure, preserving kernel semantics.

title: Stabilize Schedule id: stabilize-schedule role: operator status: someday derived_from: computer/function/evaluate-step inputs:

  • computer/library/type/calendar-schedule
  • computer/library/type/conflict-report outputs:
  • computer/library/type/calendar-schedule
  • computer/type/computer-failure transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Stabilize Schedule

Adjusts a calendar schedule to resolve conflicts and maintain invariants.

Definition

  • Inputs: Calendar Schedule, Conflict Report.
  • Outputs: updated Calendar Schedule or Computer Failure.
  • Behaviour: applies conflict resolutions (reorder, defer, cancel) consistent with policy/trace constraints; emits failure if stabilization cannot satisfy invariants.

Invariants

  • Valid schedules only: resulting plan remains acyclic/well-typed.
  • Failure purity: on failure, original schedule remains unchanged.

Proof

  • Derived from: Computer Evaluate Step.
  • Shows that: resolving conflicts via evaluation yields a stabilized schedule or explicit failure, preserving kernel semantics.

title: Step-Indexed Invariants id: step-indexed-invariant role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Step-Indexed Invariants

Tower and invariant acts induce a step-indexed logic: each tower level refines equivalence by one step; taking the limit yields the step-indexed invariants tracked by reach and stabilize-invariant.

Definition

  • Inputs/Outputs: invariants over tower levels of recognitions.
  • Behaviour: refines equivalence step-by-step along the tower; limits yield step-indexed invariants tracked by reach/stabilize acts.

Invariants

  • Monotonic refinement along tower levels.
  • Limit invariants stable under reach/stabilize acts.

Proof

  • Uses: Stabilize Invariant over the Profile Tower to refine equivalence level-by-level.
  • Shows that: stepwise refinement along the tower yields limit invariants stable under reach/stabilize acts.

title: Computer Storage id: storage-api role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Computer Storage

Definition

  • Inputs: Storage Snapshot identifiers/contents.
  • Outputs: persisted/loaded snapshots or Failure Algebra element.
  • Behaviour:
    1. Validate snapshot descriptor and contents against the storage snapshot carrier schema.
    2. On save, produce persisted snapshot id/hash and update provenance.
    3. On load, reconstruct carriers from stored content up to nucleus equivalence.
    4. Surface schema/integrity failures as failure elements.

Invariants

  • Integrity preserved from machine snapshots; failures reported without state corruption.

Proof

  • Uses: Storage Save and Storage Load semantics over the storage snapshot carrier.
  • Shows that: persistence operations over storage snapshots preserve integrity and either produce reconstructed carriers or failure witnesses without state corruption.

title: Storage Load id: storage-load role: Reconstruct machine state from a storage snapshot. status: plan inputs:

  • id: storage-snapshot name: snapshot outputs:
  • id: recognition name: recognitions
  • id: evaluation-state name: eval-state
  • id: failure-carrier name: failure transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Storage Load

Reconstructs machine state from a Storage Snapshot.

Proof

  • Uses: snapshot reading, context retracing, and persistent recognition reload.
  • Shows that: reconstructing state yields machine carriers or a failure when reconstruction is invalid.

title: Storage Load Operator tags:

  • storage
  • snapshot layer: internal-theorem status: plan depends_on:
  • math/structure/quasicrystalline-hypertensor-sheaf-section
  • math/structure/storage-snapshot-carrier
  • math/acts/retrace-context
  • math/acts/renew-context claims:
  • type: operator judgement: load_snapshot : SnapId ⇀ Section

Storage Load Operator

Statement

There exists an operator

load_snapshot : SnapId ⇀ Section

that retrieves SnapContent for a given SnapId and reconstructs a sheaf Section nucleus-equivalent to the stored one.

Definition by acts

  • Given SnapId, retrieve its SnapContent (observations, baseline remainder, fixed recognitions).
  • Use retrace-context to replay the stored context and renew-context to reintroduce the baseline remainder and fixed recognitions.
  • Produce a Section nucleus-equivalent to the one that was saved.

Uniqueness

Because SnapContent is fixed by the earned carriers and retrace/renew acts determine reconstruction, any operator respecting these produces the same Section up to nucleus equivalence; load_snapshot is unique in the earned sense.

title: Storage Save id: storage-save role: Extract a storage snapshot from machine state. status: plan inputs:

  • id: recognition name: recognitions
  • id: evaluation-state name: eval-state outputs:
  • id: storage-snapshot name: snapshot
  • id: failure-carrier name: failure transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Storage Save

System-level operator to extract a storage snapshot from machine state.

Definition

  • Input: machine recognition and evaluation state.
  • Output: a Storage Snapshot.
  • Behaviour: isolate baseline remainder, collect intake-normalised observations and persistent recognitions, and package them as a storage snapshot.

Failure

  • Emits FailureCarrier at the machine layer on errors.

Proof

Refines the storage snapshot structure into an operator that saves machine state. Pairs with Storage Load to provide round-trip semantics up to nucleus equivalence.

title: Storage Save Operator tags:

  • storage
  • snapshot layer: internal-theorem status: plan depends_on:
  • math/structure/quasicrystalline-hypertensor-sheaf-section
  • math/structure/storage-snapshot-carrier
  • acts/baseline-limit claims:
  • type: operator judgement: save_snapshot : Section → (SnapContent, SnapId)

Storage Save Operator

Statement

There exists an operator

save_snapshot : Section → (SnapContent, SnapId)

that takes a sheaf Section and produces snapshot content paired with a fresh SnapId.

Definition by acts

  • From a Section, extract observations, the baseline remainder via baseline-limit, and fixed recognitions eligible for storage (PersistentRecognitionCarrier).
  • Package these into SnapContent (see storage-snapshot-carrier).
  • Allocate a fresh SnapId and return (SnapContent, SnapId).

Uniqueness

Because SnapContent and SnapId are fixed by the earned carriers and the freshness discipline, any operator respecting these yields the same result up to nucleus equivalence; save_snapshot is unique in the earned sense.

title: Store Section id: store-section role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Store Section

Math-level operator mirroring machine store: encodes a section for persistence.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Hypertensor Section and Section Encoding Schema.
  • Output: section with attached encoding (or emitted encoding payload).
  • Behaviour: serialize per schema; update hashes/digests.

Invariants

  • Round-trip fidelity with load.
  • Deterministic encoding.

Proof

  • Uses: encoding schema.
  • Produces: encoded section payload.
  • Shows that: storing a section is well-defined for persistence.

title: Strategic Flow id: strategic-flow role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Strategic Flow

Flow variant where agents adjust contexts to maximise recognition survival given others’ strategies.

Definition

  • Inputs: a set of Agents and current strategies.
  • Output: updated agents after applying Strategic Update and Social Flow.
  • Behaviour: at each step, agents update strategies to improve utility while interacting via social flow; seek Nash-like fixed points.

Invariants

  • Norm compliance: updates respect each agent’s norm regime.
  • Fixed points: Nash points correspond to flow/nucleus fixed points.

Proof

  • Uses: Strategic Update and Social Flow.
  • Produces: updated agents.
  • Shows that: strategic adaptation can be encoded as a flow on agents.

title: Strategic Update id: strategic-update role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Strategic Update

Agents alter behaviour to improve utility recognitions.

Definition

  • Inputs: an Agent and utility assessments from Utility Recognition.
  • Output: updated Agent with adjusted epistemic/norm choices to improve utility.
  • Behaviour: adjust beliefs/strategies along directions that increase utility recognitions while respecting norms/flow.

Invariants

  • Norm compliance: updates stay within the agent’s norm regime.
  • Improvement: utility recognition should not decrease (locally) after update.

Proof

  • Uses: Agent and Utility Recognition.
  • Produces: updated Agent.
  • Shows that: strategic updates can be modelled recognitionally, keeping consistency with flow/norm structure.

title: Strong Normalization id: strong-normalization role: operator status: plan derived_from: acts/normalize-strongly inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Strong Normalization

Statement

The earned acts normalization, step-trace, and normalize-strongly jointly earn that every well-founded reduction trace collapses to normal form.

Proof sketch

Normalization applied to StepTrace collapses any infinite tail; the act NormalizeStrongly records that collapse explicitly, so every admissible reduction terminates.

Uniqueness

Because the normalization acts fix the reduction relation and its collapse, this strong-normalization property is unique in the earned sense.

Proof

  • Derived from: Normalize Strongly.
  • Inputs: stabilization of reduction traces via Trace Renewal.
  • Shows that: every admissible reduction trace collapses to normal form.

title: Struct id: struct role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: runtime-value alias: fields outputs:
  • id: runtime-value alias: struct transformation:
  • call
  • construct-struct
  • fields proof_refs:
  • construct-struct
  • runtime-value

Struct

Builds a keyed aggregate (record) from supplied field pairs, used to materialize carriers in nested-array programs.

Proof

  • Uses: construct-struct to assemble the record from provided fields.
  • Shows that: record construction can be expressed without primitives, delegating to a dedicated constructor over runtime values.

title: Structure Scan id: structure-scan role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Structure Scan

Scans computer-layer carriers to enumerate/validate structures present in the current evaluation state.

Definition

  • Inputs: Evaluation State, Recognition Carrier criteria, ambient policy/trace.
  • Outputs: recognised structure inventory or Failure Algebra.
  • Behaviour: inspects evaluation bindings, validates against recognition criteria using earned recognition operators, emits recognitions for discovered structures; failures on malformed or policy-forbidden bindings.

Invariants

  • Read-only: no mutation of evaluation state.
  • Policy/trace compliance enforced during recognition; failures emitted when constraints violated.

Proof

  • Uses: Recognition over bindings in the evaluation state with policy/trace constraints.
  • Shows that: discovery/validation over evaluation bindings yields either recognitions or failure witnesses without mutating the evaluation state.

title: Substitute Section id: substitute-section role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Substitute Section

Typed substitution/effect calculus lifted to hypertensor sections.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Hypertensor Section with placeholders/binders and a substitution mapping via Recognition Substitution.
  • Output: section with placeholders replaced by provided recognitions/sections.
  • Behaviour: apply substitution pointwise, respecting sheaf/flow structure and section typing.

Invariants

  • Type preservation: substitution maintains section types if mapping is well-typed.
  • Sheaf compatibility: substitutions commute with restriction/gluing.

Proof

  • Uses: recognition substitution rules lifted to sections.
  • Produces: substituted section.
  • Shows that: substitution is well-defined and type-preserving on sections.

title: Substitution Discipline tags:

  • substitution
  • reducibility layer: internal-theorem status: plan depends_on:
  • acts/seed-term
  • acts/substitution-term
  • acts/substitute-reducibility claims:
  • type: operator judgement: Substitute : Term × Term × Context ⇀ Term preserves reducibility

Substitution Discipline

Statement

The acts seed-term, substitution-term, and substitute-reducibility earn a substitution operator

Substitute : Term × Term × Context ⇀ Term

that is well-defined on the earned term grammar and preserves reducibility.

Properties

  • Well-defined on earned terms/contexts (seed-term, substitution-term).
  • Preserves reducibility predicates used in normalization (substitute-reducibility).
  • Interacts coherently with evaluation contexts built from the evaluation context structure.

Uniqueness

Given these acts, the substitution discipline is fixed up to nucleus equivalence.

            • payload

            • payload
          • return
          • false
    • return

  • runtime-value
  • literal-equals

Symbol Equals

Raw equality check over tag symbols/strings, returning a boolean runtime value.

Invariants

  • Symbols compare equal only when both tags and payloads match.
  • Outputs a boolean runtime value usable for dispatch branching.

title: Task Commutation id: task-commutation role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Task Commutation

Lifts the math-layer task commutation act to the calculus layer, parameterised by the indexed trace.

Definition

  • Inputs: the Trace Carrier describing ordered effects across contexts.
  • Outputs: an updated Trace Carrier annotated with the commutation relation on tasks whose effects do not interfere.
  • Behaviour: identifies pairs of tasks whose effects commute (no shared writes or interfering reads in the trace) and marks them as reorderable within the indexed trace.

Invariants

  • Soundness: only tasks with non-interfering effects are marked commuting.
  • Closure: commutation is symmetric and closed under reindexing of the trace.
  • Ordering preservation: non-commuting tasks retain their enforced order.

Proof

  • Uses: Task Commutation criteria over the Trace Carrier to decide reorderability.
  • Shows that: parameterising by the trace carrier preserves interference analysis and yields a commutation relation used by schedulers.

title: Task Parallelization id: task-parallelization role: Partition tasks into parallelizable groups consistent with precedence and commutation. status: plan inputs:

  • id: task-carrier name: tasks outputs:
  • id: task-carrier name: tasks-with-partitions transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Task Parallelization

Partitions tasks into parallelizable groups respecting the precedence relation and commutation.

Proof

  • Uses: task-precedence and task-commutation to compute allowable groupings.
  • Shows that: grouping commuting tasks consistent with precedence yields partitions that preserve observable outcomes under parallel execution.

title: Task Precedence id: task-precedence role: Establish a precedence relation over tasks based on trace read/write sets and commutation. status: plan inputs:

  • id: task-carrier name: tasks
  • id: trace-carrier name: trace outputs:
  • id: task-carrier name: tasks-with-precedes transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Task Precedence

Defines a precedence relation over tasks: writes must precede dependent reads; commuting tasks need not be ordered.

Behaviour

  • Compute read/write sets per task from the trace context.
  • Use task-commutation to identify commuting tasks.
  • Build the Precedes relation accordingly and annotate tasks.

Invariants

  • Dependency soundness: every read is preceded by all writers to that trace region/channel.
  • Commutation: only commuting tasks may be unordered.
  • Acyclicity: precedence is acyclic.

Proof

  • Uses: read/write set extraction and commutation to compute precedence.
  • Shows that: imposing ordering for interfering tasks and omitting it for commuting tasks yields a sound, acyclic precedence relation.

title: Temporal Kernel Evolve id: temporal-kernel-evolve role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Temporal Kernel Evolve

Applies a temporal kernel to evolve recognitions over time.

Definition

  • Inputs: a Temporal Kernel and initial recognitions.
  • Output: evolved recognitions after a time step/interval.
  • Behaviour: apply the kernel’s time evolution operator; compose semigroup style for multiple steps.

Invariants

  • Semigroup: evolution over t1+t2 equals composition over t1 then t2.
  • Flow-respecting: evolution adheres to admissible flow.

Proof

  • Uses: temporal kernel operations.
  • Produces: evolved recognitions.
  • Shows that: deterministic time evolution can be executed via the kernel.

  • id: observable-carrier name: observed outputs:

Test Assert

Uses measurement structures/dynamics to compare observed results against expected observables, respecting nucleus equivalence where applicable.

Proof

  • TODO: fill proof.

Test Run

Proof

Top Frame

Topological Order

Computes a topological ordering of steps in a Wiring Plan. Fails if cycles remain.

Proof

  • Uses: primitive topological sort over the plan’s edges.
  • Shows that: an execution order exists for acyclic plans.

title: Primitive Topological Sort id: primitive-topological-sort role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: wiring-plan alias: plan outputs:

Primitive Topological Sort

Performs a topological sort over the steps/edges of a Wiring Plan.

Proof

  • Uses: plan’s edge relation to order steps.
  • Shows that: acyclic plans admit an ordered execution sequence.

title: “Trace\u2013Context Bridge” id: trace-context-bridge role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Trace–Context Bridge

seed-first-context, extend-context, and retrace-context earn the link between traces and contexts: anchoring, extending, and replaying contexts along TraceCarrier compatibly.

Definition

  • Inputs: Trace Carrier and earned context acts (seed/extend/retrace).
  • Outputs: updated Trace Carrier reflecting context anchoring/extension/replay.
  • Behaviour: seeds the first context, extends along trace steps, and retraces to replay contexts, ensuring compatibility with trace ordering.

Invariants

  • Trace-context consistency: context anchoring/replay respects trace order.
  • Stability: retracing preserves earned context invariants.

Proof

  • Uses: Retrace Context and supporting acts (seed/extend) to anchor, extend, and replay contexts.
  • Shows that: lifting context-seeding/extension/retracing yields a bridge operator over the trace carrier with trace-order consistency.

title: Trace Context id: trace-context role: operator status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/trace-context inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Trace Context

Operator TraceContext pushing recognised contexts forward along the trace.

Proof

Sketch: TraceContext = (ContextualizeTrace RecognizeContext) pushes recognitions forward; show composites reduce to identity in the nucleus with RetraceContext.

title: Trace Head/Tail Operators id: trace-head-tail role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Trace Head/Tail Operators

head-trace, tail-trace, and bind-trace earn projections on the trace spine and a binding witness that reassembles the original trace up to nucleus equivalence. These operators work over TraceCarrier.

Definition

  • Inputs: Trace Carrier.
  • Outputs: head/tail projections and a binding operation to reassemble the trace.
  • Behaviour: compute head (first position) and tail (remainder) of a trace and provide a bind/concatenate that reconstructs the original trace up to earned equivalence.

Invariants

  • Reconstruction: bind(head, tail) equals the original trace up to nucleus equivalence.
  • Stability: projections respect trace ordering and do not alter payload.

Proof

  • Uses: Tail Trace, head, and bind acts to define projections and recomposition.
  • Shows that: lifting head/tail/bind operations yields trace projections/compositions over the trace carrier, preserving reconstruction up to nucleus equivalence.

title: Trace Judgement id: trace-judgement role: operator status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/trace-judgement inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Trace Judgement

Operator pushing recognised judgements forward along the trace, earned by TraceJudgement.

Proof

Sketch: TraceJudgement = (ContextualizeTrace RecognizeJudgement) pushes recognitions forward; show composites with retrace reduce appropriately in the nucleus.

title: Trace Renewal Operators id: trace-renew role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Trace Renewal Operators

renew-trace and restep-trace earn re-entry of stabilized tails into the trace without altering earned history, acting on TraceCarrier.

Definition

  • Inputs: Trace Carrier.
  • Outputs: renewed/restep traces within the same carrier.
  • Behaviour: reintroduces stabilised tails into the trace, preserving earned prefix history.

Invariants

  • History preservation: prefix/history unchanged by renewal.
  • Stability: renewed segments respect trace ordering and earned constraints.

Proof

  • Uses: Renew Trace and restep-trace acts to manage re-entry of stabilized tails.
  • Shows that: renewal/restep operators re-enter tails without altering earned history, preserving trace carrier invariants.

title: Trace Renewal id: trace-renewal role: operator status: plan derived_from: acts/renew-trace inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Trace Renewal

Statement

The acts renew-trace, restep-trace, and iterative-persistence earn re-entry of a stabilized tail into the trace without altering its baseline remainder.

Proof sketch

RenewTrace rethreads the remainder into the head of the trace. RestepTrace witnesses that after renewal the tail matches the previous limit. IterativePersistence guarantees this behaviour persists under further iterations, so renewal does not disturb the limit.

Uniqueness

Given these earned acts, the renewal behaviour is fixed up to nucleus equivalence.

Proof

  • Derived from: Renew Trace.
  • Shows that: renewing and re-stepping traces leaves the baseline remainder unchanged under iterative persistence.

title: Trace Shuffle Count tags:

  • trace
  • shuffle
  • combinatorics layer: internal-theorem status: plan depends_on:
  • math/structure/trace-shuffle-class claims:
  • type: operator judgement: |Reorder(T_n)| satisfies the Fibonacci recurrence

Trace Shuffle Count

Statement

The sizes of shuffle classes satisfy

|Reorder(T_0)| = 1, |Reorder(T_1)| = 1,
|Reorder(T_n)| = |Reorder(T_{n-1})| + |Reorder(T_{n-2})| for n ≥ 2

so |Reorder(T_n)| = F_n.

Proof sketch

Decomposition and free shuffle imply that Reorder(T_n) splits into reorderings beginning with a T_{n-1} progress and those beginning with a T_{n-2} progress, yielding the Fibonacci recurrence.

Uniqueness

Given the earned shuffle classes, the count is determined.

title: Trace Shuffle Flow id: trace-shuffle-flow role: operator status: plan derived_from: acts/shuffle-flow inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Trace Shuffle Flow

Statement

The act shuffle-flow acts on shuffle classes Reorder(T_n), and commute-shuffle-flow-and-nucleus ensures the action respects nucleus equivalence.

Proof sketch

ShuffleFlow evolves shuffle classes along evaluation contexts; commutation with the nucleus guarantees orbits are admissible shuffle families within each Reorder(T_n).

Uniqueness

Given the earned shuffle classes and commutation act, this flow is fixed up to earned equivalence.

Proof

  • Derived from: Shuffle Flow.
  • Inputs: Trace Shuffle Class.
  • Shows that: shuffle flow acts on admissible reorderings, with commutation ensuring orbits respect nucleus equivalence.

title: Transport Flow Fix id: transport-flow-fix role: operator status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/transport-flow-fix inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Transport Flow Fix

Operator moving flow fixed points along morphisms without breaking stabilization.

Proof

Sketch: earned by TransportFlowFix; show that transported points remain in FixFlow under the flow action.

Transport Nucleus Fix

Operator moving nucleus fixed points along morphisms without breaking stabilization.

Proof

Sketch: earned by TransportNucleusFix; show transported points remain in FixNucleus under the nucleus action.

title: Type Judgment Rule id: type-judgment-rule role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Type Judgment Rule

Applies a typing rule in the typed judgment system over hypertensor sections.

Definition

  • Inputs: premises from the Typed Judgment System and a Judgment Term/Hypertensor Section.
  • Output: Proof Trace for the derived judgment.
  • Behaviour: check premises, apply rule schema, record derivation step.

Invariants

  • Soundness: only valid rules produce proof traces.
  • Compositionality: proof traces compose to full derivations.

Proof

  • Uses: typing context/rules.
  • Produces: proof trace.
  • Shows that: typing derivations can be recorded and checked.

title: Typed Recognition Combinator id: typed-recognition-combinator role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Typed Recognition Combinator

Applies a typed logical combinator (and/or/not/implication/modus ponens) within the recognition type system, producing a proof trace.

Definition

  • Inputs: premises in the Recognition Type System.
  • Output: Proof Trace of the combined recognition.
  • Behaviour: check typing, apply the combinator (e.g., modus ponens), and emit derivation trace.

Invariants

  • Type soundness: only well-typed combinators produce proofs.
  • Normalisation: resulting recognition can be semantically collapsed to normal form.

Proof

  • Uses: type system rules and combinators.
  • Produces: proof trace.
  • Shows that: logical operations are type-directed and derivations are recorded.

Union Scope

Proof

Union Window

Computes the union/covering window of two hypertensor windows when admissible.

Proof

  • Shows that: windows can be combined when coverage admits their union.

title: Universe Lowering id: universe-lowering role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Universe Lowering

Maps a recognition in the recognition universe down to a base-level recognition via nucleus collapse.

Definition

  • Inputs: recognition-of-recognition in the Recognition Universe.
  • Output: base Recognition Carrier element representing its semantics.
  • Behaviour: apply Nucleus to collapse higher-order structure to base-level recognition.

Invariants

  • Soundness: lowering preserves meaning; removes higher-order packaging.
  • Idempotent: repeated lowering yields same base recognition.

Proof

  • Uses: recognition universe and nucleus.
  • Produces: base recognition.
  • Shows that: universe elements can be coherently lowered to base recognitions.

  • id: recognition alias: carrier
  • id: recognition-ring alias: predicate outputs:
  • id: recognition alias: updated transformation:
  • return
    • updated proof_refs:

  • recognition-ring

Update Predicate

Replaces the predicate component of a recognition while keeping support/window, evidence, scope, provenance, collapse depth, and nucleus unchanged.

Proof

  • Uses: recognition structure.
  • Shows that: predicate updates can be applied without disturbing other fields.

title: Update Stack id: update-stack role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: evaluation-context-stack alias: stack
  • id: hypertensor-section alias: section outputs:
  • id: evaluation-context-stack alias: updated transformation:
  • return
    • stack
      • tail proof_refs:
  • evaluation-context-stack
  • hypertensor-section

Update Stack

Pops the head frame after it has been applied, yielding the remaining Evaluation Context Stack.

Proof

  • Uses: stack/frame discipline to push/pop as needed.
  • Shows that: stack evolution after a step can be expressed without violating context invariants.

title: Utility Recognition id: utility-recognition role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Utility Recognition

Recognises the “goodness” or desirability of states for an agent as a recognition-valued utility.

Definition

  • Inputs: an Agent and a recognition/state candidate.
  • Output: recognition reflecting agent-specific utility (not numeric).
  • Behaviour: evaluate the state against the agent’s norms/epistemics and encode desirability as a recognition in the logic lattice.

Invariants

  • Contextual: utility depends on agent’s current epistemic/norm state.
  • Monotone: improving compliance/knowledge raises utility recognition.

Proof

  • Uses: Agent and Recognition Carrier.
  • Produces: utility-valued Recognition Carrier element.
  • Shows that: utility can be represented recognitionally for strategic updates.

title: Validate Arguments id: validate-arguments role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: operator-signature alias: signature
  • id: argument-list alias: args outputs:

    • signature
    • args proof_refs:

  • argument-list
  • judgment-typing

Validate Arguments

Checks an Argument List against an Operator Signature, enforcing arity and carrier types before dispatch.

Proof

  • Uses: typing data in the signature to ensure each argument position matches the expected carrier.
  • Shows that: only well-formed, correctly typed argument lists proceed to execution; otherwise a failure carrier would be emitted by the underlying ensure-arity-and-types primitive.

title: Validate Matching Family id: validate-matching-family role: operator status: plan inputs:

  • id: coverage-site alias: site
  • id: matching-family alias: family outputs:
  • id: matching-family alias: validated transformation:

    • ensure-overlap-agreement
    • site
    • family proof_refs:
  • coverage-site
  • matching-family

Proof

  • Uses: overlaps and restriction maps from the coverage site to verify agreement across the matching family.
  • Shows that: only compatible data passes to gluing; non-matching families are rejected.

Validate Matching Family

Checks that a Matching Family over a cover in a Coverage Site satisfies the overlap and coherence conditions required for sheaf gluing.

Proof

  • Uses: cover overlaps and restriction maps from the coverage site to verify pairwise and triple-overlap agreement.
  • Shows that: only matching data passes through to gluing; mismatches are rejected.

title: Validate Snapshot Descriptor id: validate-snapshot-descriptor role: operator status: plan inputs:

Validate Snapshot Descriptor

Checks snapshot id/version/provenance fields for correctness and uniqueness, returning the descriptor or a failure.

Proof

  • Shows that: descriptor validation is explicit and non-mutating.

Validate Snapshot Descriptor

Checks snapshot id, version, and provenance fields for correctness/uniqueness.

Invariants

  • Returns validated snapshot or explicit failure without mutating inputs.

title: Witness Judgment id: witness-judgment role: operator status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/witness-judgement inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:
  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Witness Judgment

The Witness Judgment operator classifies stabilized judgments and produces their witnesses.

Definition

  • Input: a judgment triad trace.
  • Output: a judgment triad annotated with stabilized witnesses.
  • Behaviour: composes stabilization and reflex acts to classify judgment acts and attach observational content.

Invariants

  • Witnesses remain aligned with the earned observation layer.
  • Judgments remain within the triad carrier.

Proof

Derived from acts/witness-judgement and related stabilization acts, which record witnesses for stabilized judgments.

title: Witness Observation id: witness-observation role: operator status: plan derived_from: acts/witness-observation inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

Witness Observation

Definition

  • Inputs: Observation.
  • Outputs: Observation Witness certifying contextual equivalence.
  • Behaviour: applies Witness Observation to produce a witness that earned observations suffice to establish contextual equivalence over the full profile.

Invariants

  • Uses only earned observations; witnesses remain within the observation carrier and earned recognitions.
  • Witnesses are stable under reindexing across the base.

Proof

  • Derived from: Witness Observation.
  • Shows that: the act yields observation witnesses, realized here as a math operator that produces the corresponding witness structure over the full profile.

title: World Sum and Product id: world-sum-product role: operator status: plan inputs: [] outputs: [] transformation:

  • return
  • value proof_refs: []

World Sum and Product

Constructs combined relational worlds either by disjoint sum (multi-scenario) or synchronous product (combined geometry/logical structure).

Definition

  • Inputs: two Relational Worlds with compatible World Morphisms into a target universe if needed.
  • Outputs: a new relational world:
    • Sum: disjoint union of carriers/geometry/topos, preserving each component.
    • Product: product geometry/topos with synchronized flow/nucleus.
  • Behaviour: forms categorical sum/product in the category of relational worlds and world morphisms.

Invariants

  • Preservation: embeddings into the sum/product are world morphisms.
