Derived from acts/fixed-kernel using fixed-point iteration; uniqueness and mixing follow from the underlying acts.

title: Fixed Nucleus id: fixed-nucleus role: structure status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/fix-nucleus proof_refs: []

Fixed Nucleus

Structure capturing fixed points of the nucleus modality, earned by the act FixNucleus.

Components

  • Carrier FixNucleus of fixed points.
  • Inclusion ι_nuc : FixNucleus ↪ Carrier.
  • Stability: nucleus ∘ ι_nuc = ι_nuc.

Proof

Sketch: FixNucleus selects points stabilised by the nucleus act; show inclusion and stability using the earned nucleus modality.

title: Fixed Observation Witness id: fixed-observation-witness role: structure status: plan derived_from: acts/fixed-witness-observation proof_refs: []

Fixed Observation Witness

Certificate that fixed observations suffice to witness contextual equivalence in the fixed profile.

Definition

  • Carrier: witnesses produced by applying Fixed Observation through the act Fixed Witness Observation to establish contextual equivalence in the fixed profile, by restricting the base Observation Witness.
  • Role: evidence that two contexts are indistinguishable using only fixed observations.

Invariants

  • Witnesses reference only fixed-profile observations and contexts.
  • Stable under fixed-profile restrictions; no unfixed observations are admitted.
  • Restriction: obtained by restricting observation witnesses; no new witnesses beyond the fixed carrier.

Proof

  • Derived from: Fixed Witness Observation.
  • Shows that: the act yields a carrier of witnesses confined to fixed observations, sufficient to decide contextual equivalence in the fixed profile.

title: Fixed Observation Carrier id: fixed-observation role: structure status: plan derived_from: acts/profile-observation-recognitions proof_refs: []

Fixed Observation Carrier

Definition

  • Carrier: observations whose domains and codomains lie in the fixed profile, as a restriction of the full Observation carrier.
  • Source: earned from Profile Observation Recognitions, restricted to the fixed-profile subset.

Invariants

  • Fixedness: observations remain invariant under the fixed-profile flow/nucleus stack.
  • Profile-compatibility: domains/codomains reside in the fixed-profile recognitions.

Proof

  • Derived from: Profile Observation Recognitions, with fixedness enforced via Fixed Profile Recognitions.
  • Shows that: restricting profile observations to the fixed-profile recognitions yields the fixed observation carrier; it is the fixed-profile subcarrier of Observation.

title: Fixed-Scale Profile id: fixed-scale-profile role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Fixed-Scale Profile

Profile stable under repeated coarse-graining/rescaling at a given scale level.

Definition

  • Carrier: sections/profiles in the Scale Tower that return to themselves (up to nucleus) after rescaling.
  • Condition: RescaleSection around a loop in the tower yields equivalent section.

Invariants

  • Stability: invariant under tower rescaling at the fixed level.
  • Universality: corresponds to universality classes in RG analysis.

Proof

  • Uses: Scale Tower and Rescale Section.
  • Shows that: fixed-scale behaviour can be captured as a stable carrier.

title: Fixed Shape id: fixed-shape role: structure status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/fixed-shape proof_refs: []

Fixed Shape

Fixed point of the shuffle-indexed implication in the shuffle profile, earned by the act FixedShape.

Components

  • Shuffle flow ShuffleFlow.
  • Shuffle profile recognitions (ShuffleProfileSemantics).
  • Fixed point FixedShape invariant under ShuffleFlow and Nucleate.

Proof

Sketch: from shuffle flow/geometry/profile acts, apply the fixed-shape act to select the unique profile-equivalent fixed point of the shuffle-indexed implication dynamics.

title: Flow Attractor id: flow-attractor role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Flow Attractor

Set of recognitions such that all admissible flow paths in a region converge to them (attractor basin representative).

Definition

  • Attractor: recognition/set stable under flow/nucleus; nearby states converge to it.
  • Basin: recognised via Recognition Attracting Basin.

Invariants

  • Stability: attractor is fixed under flow/nucleus.
  • Attraction: neighbourhood under flow maps into basin converging to attractor.

Proof

  • Uses: flow dynamics and nucleus to define fixed points/basins.
  • Shows that: attractors can be identified as convergence targets for flows.

title: Flow Gradient id: flow-gradient role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Flow Gradient

Measures the “steepness” of flow evolution within a profile.

Definition

  • Data: rate of change of recognitions under Flow Context, possibly annotated with entropy or metric.
  • Role: quantify how rapidly recognitions evolve.

Invariants

  • Non-negativity; zero indicates fixed under flow.
  • Compatible with metrics/neighborhoods if defined.

Proof

  • Uses: flow dynamics and recognition carrier.
  • Shows that: flow rate can be encoded as a structure for analysis/optimization.

title: “Flow\u2013Nucleus Adjunction” id: flow-nucleus-adjunction role: structure status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/context-flow
  • acts/context-nucleus
  • acts/context-adjunction-witness proof_refs: []

Flow–Nucleus Adjunction

Adjunction data between context flow (left adjoint) and context nucleus (right adjoint) with unit/counit and triangle identities.

Definition

  • Left adjoint: flow functor on Context.
  • Right adjoint: nucleus functor on Context.
  • Unit/counit: earned by Context Adjunction Witness.

Invariants

  • Triangle identities hold (flow followed by nucleus/unit is identity up to earned equivalence; likewise for counit).
  • Functoriality: flow/nucleus respect composition/identity of contexts.

Proof

  • Derived from: Context Flow, Context Nucleus, and Context Adjunction Witness.
  • Shows that: flow and nucleus form an adjoint pair with unit/counit satisfying triangle laws.

title: Flow Potential id: flow-potential role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Flow Potential

Assigns an energy/potential value to recognitions based on flow resistance and entropy production; aids scheduling and optimisation.

Definition

  • Potential: function on recognitions measuring expected entropy production or “effort” along flow paths.
  • Inputs: flow rules and entropy functional.

Invariants

  • Monotone: higher potential indicates greater resistance/entropy production.
  • Gradient-driven: flow tends toward decreasing potential when allowed.

Proof

  • Uses: flow and entropy to define potential.
  • Shows that: potential can be used to guide flow decisions.

title: Fractal and Dynamical Systems Playground Program id: fractal-dynamics-playground-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Fractal and Dynamical Systems Playground Program

This program explores iterated maps, attractors, and parameter sweeps as recognitions so dynamical systems can be traced and compared inside the relational computer.

Behaviour

  • Define systems (maps/flows with equations, parameters, domains, initial conditions), orbits, attractors, and parameter sweeps as recognitions with provenance.
  • Capture measurements such as Lyapunov exponents, fractal dimensions, escape times, and orbit statistics; attach them to orbits/attractors.
  • Map topology: parameter spaces, orbit→attractor→basin relationships.

Intended operators

  • Iterate systems from seeds and record orbits with measurements.
  • Detect attractors and basins; relate them across parameter sweeps.
  • Run sweeps over parameter ranges, keeping results traceable and comparable.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Free Energy Functional id: free-energy-functional role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Free Energy Functional

Functional combining entropy with observables to express free energy in the relational/stochastic setting.

Definition

  • Inputs: entropy from Entropy Functional and chosen observables from Observable Algebra.
  • Output: free-energy valuation (numeric F = E - T·S in semantic models or relational inequality in purely recognitional form).
  • Behaviour: evaluates observables under the current state and combines with entropy to guide equilibrium reasoning.

Invariants

  • Monotonicity: along admissible flows, free energy decreases toward equilibria under fixed temperature/constraints.
  • Consistency: compatible with entropy monotonicity and observable algebra operations.

Proof

  • Uses: Entropy Functional and Observable Algebra to define the functional.
  • Shows that: free-energy reasoning can be expressed within earned relational observables without introducing external semantics.

title: Free Energy Typed Operator id: free-energy-typed-operator role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Free Energy Typed Operator

Typed combinator for evaluating free energy on thermodynamic/observable carriers.

Definition

  • Operator: evaluation of Free Energy Functional as a typed observable operator (alongside other observables in Observable Typed Operator).
  • Domain: thermodynamic/observable states; codomain: recognition-valued result.

Invariants

  • Type safety: applied only to compatible observable carriers.
  • Semantic correctness: respects free-energy combination F = E - T·S (or relational inequality) semantics.

Proof

  • Uses: free-energy functional within the observable typed framework.
  • Shows that: free-energy evaluation fits the typed observable combinators.

title: Freudian Psychology Program id: freudian-psychology-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Freudian Psychology Program

This program models drives, defenses, repetition, and transference as recognitions so psychoanalytic patterns can be traced across domains.

Behaviour

  • Represent drives (life/libido, death/aggression), defense mechanisms, repetition compulsions, and transference events as recognitions with context and provenance.
  • Link these recognitions to discourse, behaviour, dreams, and relationships to see how they manifest and change over time.
  • Measure frequency and contexts of defenses and repetitions; track whether patterns intensify or diminish.

Intended operators

  • Annotate discourse/behaviour for defenses and transference.
  • Detect repetition across situations and timelines.
  • Surface how psychoanalytic motifs intersect with inner-work and interpersonal programs.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Fundamental Groupoid id: fundamental-groupoid role: structure status: plan derived_from: math/structure/loop-space proof_refs: []

Fundamental Groupoid

Groupoid-like carrier of contexts/recognitions and homotopy classes of directed paths between them, earned from the higher-inductive path data supplied by LoopHIT.

Definition

  • Objects: contexts/recognitions carved out of Relationlessness and its first recognitions.
  • Morphisms: homotopy classes of Directed Paths between objects, with composition from Path Compose when endpoints match.
  • Identities: constant paths from Identify Path.
  • Associator/unitor: homotopies witnessing associativity/unit laws.

Invariants

  • Composition well-defined on homotopy classes; endpoint matching is required and preserved.
  • Associativity/unit laws witnessed by earned homotopies from the loop HIT.
  • Reversibility: invertible paths exist precisely when directed paths admit opposites; otherwise the carrier remains a fundamental category enriched by higher cells.
  • Coverage compatibility: path composition respects coverings on the Coverage Site, ensuring groupoid transport can be glued across overlaps.

Proof

  • Derived from: Loop Space giving composition/unit data on loops and paths.
  • Uses: Directed Path, Path Compose, Identify Path to assemble the groupoid data.
  • Shows that: directed paths and their homotopies assemble into a fundamental groupoid over contexts, giving the base homotopical carrier for path composition, transports, and coverings.

title: Gate Recognition id: gate-recognition role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Gate Recognition

Recognitions that unlock access to a subprofile or modal layer.

Definition

  • Elements: recognitions in the Recognition Carrier that satisfy constraints of a Norm Regime or modal gate.
  • Role: eligibility tokens for entering restricted disciplines/modal layers.

Invariants

  • Soundness: only admissible recognitions are gates.
  • Stability: gates remain valid under allowed flow/nucleus within the gate’s context.

Proof

  • Uses: recognition carrier and norm regime to define gating predicate.
  • Shows that: access control can be expressed recognitionally.

title: Generated Music Program id: generated-music-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • Curate, compare, and trace motifs across generated outputs.

title: Generated Personalities and Agents Program id: generated-personalities-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

title: Generative Paracosms Program id: generative-paracosms-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

Generative Paracosms Program

This program represents rich, evolving imaginary worlds—paracosms—as recognitions and lets them grow generatively inside the relational computer.

Behaviour

  • Define paracosms (premise, themes, boundaries), regions, entities, rules, and history events as recognitions with provenance.
  • Provide templates/grammars and procgen runbooks to expand regions, introduce entities or conflicts, and simulate consequences under stated rules.
  • Map intra-paracosm topology (maps, kinship/social graphs, causal links) and inter-paracosm links to real-world recognitions or other paracosms.
  • Measure visit frequency, recurring motifs, and paracosm “health” (stagnant vs generative).

Intended operators

title: Collaborative Governance and Community Program id: governance-community-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

Collaborative Governance and Community Program

  • Keep invariants: every decision traces back to proposals, votes, and policies in force; role changes are recorded with reasons and constraints.
  • Support collective work by linking governance recognitions to the domains or programs they affect.

Intended operators

  • Attach measurements (participation, transparency, conflict/appeals) to governance processes.
  • Surface audit trails for any decision, showing who proposed, who voted, and which policies applied.

title: Government Decisions Measurement Program id: government-decisions-measurement-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

Government Decisions Measurement Program

Behaviour

  • Link decisions to affected recognitions (parcels, species, communities, institutions, budgets, contracts, health metrics) so downstream impacts can be traced.
  • Attach measurement channels: implementation metrics (timeliness, completeness costs), outcome metrics (ecology, health, housing, emissions, mobility, safety), and process metrics (participation, transparency, appeals).

Intended operators

  • Compare decisions across topics/places/times.

title: GUI Graph Workbench Program id: gui-graph-workbench-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

GUI Graph Workbench Program

Behaviour

  • Define view workspaces, panels, interactions, and annotations as recognitions (no hidden UI state).
  • Express projections as queries over recognitions; panels declare what they show and how (tables, timelines, graph layouts).
  • Record interactions and annotations with provenance so views are auditable.
  • Measure coverage (which parts of the graph are visible) and usage/performance (panel load times, query durations, interaction frequency).

Intended operators

  • Attach panels bound to queries; record interactions as events.
  • Export views as static documentation to accompany textual specs.

  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Happens-Before Relation id: happens-before role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Happens-Before Relation

Partial order capturing causality for concurrent flows and observations.

Definition

  • Elements: events/observations in a Trace Shuffle Class plus Message Channel ordering.
  • Relation: transitive closure of program order within traces and send→receive edges on channels.

Invariants

  • Partial order: reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive.
  • Shuffle stability: relation is invariant under shuffle-equivalent interleavings.

Proof

  • Uses: trace/shuffle and channel ordering to define causality.
  • Shows that: causality can be encoded as a happens-before relation for concurrency semantics.

title: Higher Judgment id: higher-judgment role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Higher Judgment

Recognitions of judgments themselves—judgments about judgments.

Definition

  • Carrier: Judgment Terms lifted into the Recognition Carrier as higher-order elements.
  • Structure: supports inference/recognition at a higher level.

Invariants

  • Closure: higher judgments obey recognition logic laws.
  • Reflection: relates base judgments to their higher recognitions.

Proof

  • Uses: judgment terms and recognition carrier to form higher-level judgments.
  • Shows that: meta-judgments can be handled within the recognition framework.

title: Historical Collapse id: historical-collapse role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Historical Collapse

Collapse that depends on full flow history, not just current recognition.

Definition

  • Components: recognition plus its Trace Shuffle Class history; collapse outcome may vary by history.
  • Role: memory-aware nucleus variant.

Invariants

  • History sensitivity: different histories may yield different collapses even for same current recognition.
  • Consistency: collapse respects admissible histories; avoids paradox.

Proof

  • Uses: trace history and nucleus semantics.
  • Shows that: collapse can be made history-dependent when required.

title: Home and Environment Automation Program id: home-automation-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

Behaviour

  • Map topology of rooms, zones, devices, and occupants; measure energy, comfort indices, noise, CO₂, and other environmental metrics.
  • Constrain routines by measurement (comfort/energy), policy (safety, privacy), and traceability (who configured what).

Intended operators

  • Monitor energy and comfort metrics; adjust automations accordingly.
  • Audit configuration changes and execution histories.

Proof

  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Household Chores and Maintenance Program id: household-chores-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

Intended operators

title: Hypertensor Analytics Program id: hypertensor-analytics-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Hypertensor Analytics Program

Runs analytics over quasicrystalline hypertensors: substitution dynamics, pattern catalogues, and measurements (entropy, densities).

Behaviour

  • Maintain window/section carriers; apply substitution and flow/nucleus operators via Evaluate Step; schedule inflations.
  • Index patterns; detect aperiodicity/relative-denseness issues.
  • Compute measurements via measurement algebra; emit telemetry and exports.
  • Emit Computer Failure on coherence or measurement failures.

Invariants

  • Flow/nucleus commutation within tolerance.
  • Pattern catalogue canonical; duplicates trigger alerts.
  • Exports reference the exact substitution stage and traceability node.

Proof

Refines the computer Kernel by specialising to hypertensor analytics wiring, preserving failure semantics.

title: Hypertensor Coverage id: hypertensor-coverage role: structure status: plan proof_refs:

  • hypertensor-window fields:
  • name: base type: hypertensor-window description: window poset forming the base.
  • name: covers type: matching-family description: admissible cover families over windows.
  • name: overlaps type: matching-family description: intersections/overlaps used for matching.

Hypertensor Coverage

Coverage (pretopology) on hypertensor windows defining admissible covers and overlaps.

Definition

  • Base: Hypertensor Window poset.
  • Covers: families of sub-windows that jointly cover a window, as earned by the hypertensor window act.
  • Overlaps: intersections of cover elements used for matching.

Invariants

  • Covers are stable under refinement and pullback along inclusions.
  • Finite intersections of cover elements are admissible overlaps.

Proof

  • Uses: Hypertensor Window act that provides window refinements and admissible covers/overlaps.
  • Shows that: the window poset carries an earned coverage suitable for sheaf definitions and matching/gluing.

title: Hypertensor Dynamics Endomorphism id: hypertensor-dynamics-endomorphism role: structure status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/flow proof_refs: []

Hypertensor Dynamics Endomorphism

The Hypertensor Dynamics Endomorphism is a local endomorphism of the relational quasicrystalline hypertensor sheaf induced by an earned act in the flow/measurement/tower stacks.

Definition

  • For each open set U, acts on sections over U by modifying only the fibres/towers determined by the act’s footprint.
  • Commutes with restriction: for inclusion V ⊂ U, res_{V,U} ∘ F(U) = F(V) ∘ res_{V,U}.
  • Built from flow/nucleus and measurement acts over the Hypertensor Sheaf.

Invariants

  • Locality: only touches fibres/towers in the act’s footprint.
  • Closure: endomorphisms form a monoid under composition (include identity).
  • Compatibility: respects the sheaf structure (restriction/gluing).

Proof

Derived from flow/nucleus/measurement acts applied to the hypertensor sheaf, ensuring locality and restriction-compatibility.

title: Hypertensor Fiber id: hypertensor-fiber role: structure status: plan emerges_from: profile-recognitions proof_refs:

  • profile-recognitions fields:
  • name: context type: trace-carrier description: traced context serving as the base.
  • name: recognitions type: recognition description: stabilised recognitions/measurements over the context.
  • name: flow_nucleus type: flow-nucleus-adjunction description: compatibility data for flow and nucleus on the fibre.

Hypertensor Fiber

Definition

  • Carrier: traced contexts with stabilised recognitions/measurements.
  • Source: earned from Profile Recognitions applied to traced contexts.

Invariants

  • Flow–nucleus compatibility: recognitions respect flow and nucleate and their commutation.
  • Composite-profile stability: fibres remain stable under flow-composite-profile.

Proof

  • Derived from: Profile Recognitions, using trace infrastructure and flow/nucleus compatibility acts.
  • Shows that: applying profile recognitions to traced contexts yields fibres carrying stabilised recognitions/measurements, satisfying hypertensor fibre requirements.

title: Hypertensor Section Carrier id: hypertensor-section-carrier role: structure status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/sheafification proof_refs: []

Hypertensor Section Carrier

Global section carrier of the hypertensor sheaf, defining math-level state.

Definition

  • Carrier: Section of the Hypertensor Sheaf over the hypertensor site.
  • Fields: sections assign to each Hypertensor Window a compatible element of the Hypertensor Fiber, respecting restriction/gluing.

Invariants

  • Sheaf compatibility: respects restriction, gluing, locality from the sheaf condition.
  • Flow/nucleus commute with section operations (earned in sheaf acts).

Proof

  • Derived from: Sheafification producing the section carrier.
  • Uses: Hypertensor Sheaf.
  • Shows that: the section carrier is well-defined and forms the math-level state for hypertensor constructions.

title: Hypertensor Section id: hypertensor-section role: structure status: plan proof_refs:

  • presheaf
  • sheaf-condition
  • sheafification
  • commute-flow-and-nucleus fields:
  • name: base type: hypertensor-window description: window/fiber/tower base for the section.
  • name: restrictions type: coverage-site description: restriction maps along admissible inclusions on the coverage site.
  • name: gluing type: matching-family description: gluing operation data for compatible local sections.
  • name: flow_nucleus type: flow-nucleus-adjunction description: compatibility data for flow/nucleus actions on sections.

Hypertensor Section

Sheaf of sections over hypertensor windows, earned from presheaf/sheaf acts with flow–nucleus compatibility.

Components

  • Base coverage from Hypertensor Window, Hypertensor Fiber, and Hypertensor Tower.
  • Presheaf Section : Open → Set with restriction/gluing from sheaf acts (Presheaf, Sheaf Condition, Sheafification).
  • Flow–nucleus compatibility (flow, nucleate, Commute Flow and Nucleus) on sections.

Proof

Sketch: use presheaf/sheaf acts over hypertensor windows/towers to define Section with restriction/gluing. Apply flow/nucleus acts and their commutation to ensure compatibility on sections. The section data is earned from these acts; no unearned structure is assumed.

title: Hypertensor Sheaf Condition id: hypertensor-sheaf-condition role: structure status: plan derived_from: hypertensor-site proof_refs:

  • hypertensor-site
  • sheaf-glue
  • sheaf-locality
  • matching-family

Hypertensor Sheaf Condition

Packages gluing and locality for the hypertensor sheaf over the hypertensor site.

Definition

  • Gluing: every Matching Family over a cover has a unique amalgam (Sheaf Glue).
  • Locality: sections equal on a cover are equal globally (Sheaf Locality).

Invariants

  • Sheaf axioms hold for the hypertensor site.
  • Stable under cover refinement.

Proof

  • Derived from: Hypertensor Site.
  • Uses: Sheaf Glue and Sheaf Locality.
  • Shows that: the hypertensor presheaf satisfies the sheaf condition.

title: Relational Quasicrystalline Hypertensor Sheaf id: hypertensor-sheaf role: structure status: plan emerges_from: sheafification proof_refs:

  • presheaf
  • sheaf-condition
  • sheafification
  • hypertensor-coverage
  • hypertensor-window
  • hypertensor-fiber
  • hypertensor-tower fields:
  • name: section type: hypertensor-section description: section carrier of the sheaf.
  • name: restrict type: matching-family description: restriction maps over the coverage site.
  • name: glue type: matching-family description: gluing operation for compatible families.

Relational Quasicrystalline Hypertensor Sheaf

The Relational Quasicrystalline Hypertensor Sheaf packages the earned window, fiber, and tower carriers into a sheaf with sections, restriction, and gluing over the relational site.

Definition

  • Carriers:
    • Window: earned lattice windows from trace decomposition and fixed-point substitution (see Hypertensor Window).
    • Fiber: traced-context carriers over windows (see Hypertensor Fiber).
    • Tower: profile towers attached to each fiber (see Hypertensor Tower).
  • Sheaf: applying the sheaf acts (presheaf, sheaf condition, sheafification) yields (Section, restrict, glue) as a sheaf over the earned coverage.
  • State: set State := Section; this is the math-level state carrier.

Invariants

  • Sheaf axioms hold (matching families glue uniquely over covers).
  • Flow/nucleus commute with restrict/glue as earned by the flow/nucleus acts.
  • State is unique up to earned equivalence.

Proof

  • Derived from the sheaf acts applied to the earned window/fiber/tower carriers over the relational coverage.
  • Flow/nucleus commutation acts ensure stability under dynamics.
  • Shows that: the sheaf data (section, restrict, glue) is earned; no unearned primitives are introduced.

title: Hypertensor Site id: hypertensor-site role: structure status: plan proof_refs:

  • hypertensor-coverage fields:
  • name: objects type: hypertensor-window description: windows and inclusions forming the site base.
  • name: covers type: hypertensor-coverage description: admissible covering families over windows.
  • name: sieves type: matching-family description: sieves generated from covers for sheaf checks.

Hypertensor Site

Grothendieck site formed by hypertensor windows with the earned coverage.

Definition

  • Underlying category: windows and inclusions from Hypertensor Window.
  • Covers: admissible families from Hypertensor Coverage.
  • Sieves: generated by covers; used for sheaf condition.

Invariants

  • Pullback-stable covers; generated sieves closed under refinement.
  • Enough covers to define matching families and gluing.

Proof

  • Derived from: Hypertensor Coverage over Hypertensor Window, generating covers and sieves.
  • Shows that: hypertensor windows with their coverage form a site suitable for defining hypertensor sheaves and matching/gluing.

title: Hypertensor Stochastic Evolution id: hypertensor-stochastic-evolution role: structure status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/stochastic-dynamics proof_refs: []

Hypertensor Stochastic Evolution

The Hypertensor Stochastic Evolution assembles Stochastic-type dynamics on finite windows of the hypertensor sheaf, consistent with tower/modal structure.

Definition

  • For each finite window, a Stochastic kernel on the finite-dimensional state space of sections over that window.
  • Induced dynamics on towers/slices agree with compositions of hypertensor endomorphisms.

Invariants

  • Stationary distributions and detailed balance correspond to fixed points of composed endomorphisms.
  • Evolutions respect tower/modal structure on each window.

Proof

Derived from acts/stochastic-dynamics over the hypertensor sheaf and tower structures, aligning Stochastic kernels with sheaf endomorphism compositions.

title: Hypertensor Tower id: hypertensor-tower role: structure status: plan emerges_from: build-profile-tower proof_refs:

  • build-profile-tower fields:
  • name: base_fiber type: hypertensor-fiber description: fibre over which the tower is built.
  • name: profiles type: matching-family description: layered profiles reconstructed upward from the fibre.
  • name: flow_nucleus type: flow-nucleus-adjunction description: flow/nucleus compatibility along the tower.

Hypertensor Tower

Definition

  • Carrier: profile towers attached to each Hypertensor Fiber.
  • Source: earned from Build Profile Tower over stabilised recognitions.

Invariants

  • Compatible with composite towers via Build Composite Profile Tower.
  • Respects flow/nucleus stability from flow and nucleate.

Proof

  • Derived from: Build Profile Tower, applying tower construction to fibres.
  • Shows that: tower construction over fibres yields a well-defined carrier of hypertensor towers, stable under composition and flow/nucleus laws.

title: Hypertensor Window Section id: hypertensor-window-section role: structure status: plan derived_from:

  • theorem/internal/universal-state-hypertensor proof_refs: []

Hypertensor Window Section

The Hypertensor Window Section is a finite section of the relational quasicrystalline hypertensor sheaf over a finite lattice window.

Definition

  • Components:
    • a finite Hypertensor Window recording dimension, sites, and window metadata;
    • a finite family of Profile Towers attached to fibres whose trace/context positions lie inside the window.
  • Equality: two representations (W, {T_i}) and (W', {T'_j}) describe the same section when related by sheaf restriction/gluing and nucleus-equivalence from the hypertensor and tower theorems.

Invariants

  • Restriction: restricting to a subwindow applies sheaf restriction on the window and restricts each tower to fibres over the subwindow, discarding towers outside.
  • Gluing: compatible families over covers glue to a window section.

Proof

Derived from Theorem (Relational Quasicrystalline Hypertensor Sheaf) and the tower interaction/lifting results. These ensure that finite sections over open charts supported on finite windows are represented by a window plus a finite family of attached profile towers.

title: Hypertensor Window id: hypertensor-window role: structure status: plan emerges_from: decompose-trace proof_refs:

  • decompose-trace fields:
  • name: window type: trace-carrier description: lattice window identifier extracted from trace decomposition.
  • name: fiber type: hypertensor-fiber description: fibre data over the window.
  • name: tower type: hypertensor-tower description: tower index capturing nested windows. fields:
  • name: window type: runtime-value description: lattice window identifier with admissible coordinates.
  • name: fiber type: hypertensor-fiber description: fiber data over the window.
  • name: tower type: hypertensor-tower description: tower index capturing nested windows.

Hypertensor Window

Statement

There exists a single lattice window carrier

Window

earned by trace decomposition/shuffle and fixed-point acts, subject to the quasicrystalline constraints.

Construction by acts

  • decompose-trace, shuffle-trace, and collapse-shuffle extract lattice windows from traces.
  • ensure-fixed-point fixes the substitution flow, yielding a stable window basis.
  • External quasicrystal constraints from reference/quasicrystalline-relational-hypertensors restrict admissible windows.

Uniqueness

Given these acts and constraints, any earned window carrier is nucleus-equivalent; Window is unique up to earned equivalence.

Proof

  • Uses: acts Decompose Trace, Shuffle Trace, Collapse Shuffle, and Ensure Fixed Point to extract stable windows plus quasicrystalline constraints on admissible coordinates.
  • Shows that: the window/fiber/tower trio is earned from trace decomposition and fixed-point discipline; no unearned fields are added.

title: Identity & Access Program id: identity-access-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Identity & Access Program

The Identity & Access Program is a computer-layer assembly that runs over the kernel to authenticate principals and enforce policy on every request. It packages recognition, wiring, and failure handling into an acyclic program that can be instantiated by services needing identity and access control.

Carrier

  • Wiring plan. A finite acyclic plan over computer operators that sequences recognition, credential verification, policy checks, and telemetry hooks using Computer Wiring Plan.
  • Recognition carriers. Inputs and outputs that hold authentication claims, policy scopes, and audit tags via Computer Recognition.
  • Failure carriers. Typed failures emitted when credentials are invalid, policy is violated, or wiring constraints are not met, expressed as Computer Failure.

Behaviour

  • Ingest authentication material (tokens/credentials) into a recognition carrier.
  • Apply recognition and policy checks according to the wiring plan; enrich the carrier with audit and topology scope.
  • Emit a success carrier for authorised requests, or a failure carrier with origin, arguments, and violated recognitions.

Invariants

  • No request is marked authorised without a valid recognition and policy check.
  • All failures are emitted as Computer Failure with sufficient context to audit.
  • Wiring remains acyclic and uses only earned kernel operators.

Proof

This program refines the documentation interpreter by wiring recognition and policy operators into a concrete identity/access flow. The refinement preserves the kernel’s acyclicity and failure semantics while specialising inputs/outputs to identity carriers. A full proof shows that every successful path maintains the policy and audit invariants, and every unsuccessful path yields a failure carrier.

title: Inconsistency Region id: inconsistency-region role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Inconsistency Region

Collects recognitions that cannot be jointly realised in any profile—regions of contradiction.

Definition

  • Carrier: set of recognitions whose joint flow leads to bottom/contradiction in the Recognition Carrier.
  • Computation: explore flow combinations; mark those that collapse.

Invariants

  • Closure: closed under flow/nucleus toward bottom.
  • Detection: presence indicates degeneracy/chaos in traces.

Proof

  • Uses: recognition lattice and flow to detect collapse to bottom.
  • Shows that: inconsistent recognitions can be isolated as a carrier.

title: Index Base id: index-base role: structure status: plan derived_from: acts/context-geometry proof_refs: []

Index Base

Definition

  • Carrier: the base category of contexts provided by ContextGeometry.
  • Morphisms: context morphisms from the same act; composition and identities are inherited directly from the contextual geometry.
  • Role: serves as the indexing base for fibrewise semantic constructions.

Invariants

  • Context identities compose as in ContextGeometry, preserving the underlying flow–nucleus compatibility.
  • Every morphism in the base preserves the contextual shape fixed by the geometry; no additional structure is introduced beyond the earned geometry.

Proof

  • Derived from: ContextGeometry as the act that earns the contextual category.
  • Shows that: reusing the contextual geometry without further enrichment yields the base category required for indexed semantics, retaining the composition and identity laws established by the act.

title: Indexed Category id: indexed-category role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Indexed Category

Definition

  • Base: Index Base supplies contexts and their morphisms.
  • Fibres: Fiber Category gives a category of recognised judgements over each base context.
  • Reindexing: Reindexing maps each base morphism to a functor between the corresponding fibres.
  • Structure: assembles these into a functor IndexBaseᵒᵖ → Cat, yielding the indexed category of contextual judgements.

Invariants

  • Reindexing preserves fibre identities and composition, making each base morphism act functorially on fibres.
  • Base-change coherence is inherited from the functoriality of reindexing: pullbacks along composed base morphisms agree with iterated pullbacks.

Proof

  • Uses: Index Base as the domain category, Fiber Category for each fibre, and Reindexing to transport along base morphisms.
  • Shows that: these pieces satisfy the definition of an indexed category—every context has a fibre category and every base morphism induces a functor between fibres—forming a contravariant functor into Cat.

title: Infinity Site Program id: infinity-site-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Infinity Site Program

This program envisions a continuously evolving “infinity site” that renders the relational universe as an explorable web experience.

Behaviour

  • Map recognitions and runbooks to navigable web views with provenance-aware links.
  • Provide progressive disclosure: shallow overviews that drill into full traces, measurements, and policies.
  • Support live updates from the relational graph while preserving traceability of what was shown when.

Intended operators

  • Generate and refresh site pages from recognition queries.
  • Record user navigation as interactions for audit and learning.
  • Export snapshots for offline or archival viewing.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Influence Kernel id: influence-kernel role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Influence Kernel

Kernel mapping recognitions to how they transform others under flow—a relational Jacobian analogue.

Definition

  • Mapping: for each recognition, maps target recognitions to their change under flow influenced by the source.
  • Encodes differential effect of one recognition on another.

Invariants

  • Linearity/monotonicity where applicable; respects flow compositionality.
  • Stability: kernel consistent with nucleus/flow laws.

Proof

  • Uses: flow context to measure influence.
  • Shows that: transformation effects can be encapsulated as a kernel.

title: Influence Recognition id: influence-recognition role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Influence Recognition

Detects how much one agent’s recognitions alter another’s flow evolution.

Definition

  • Carrier: mapping from pairs of Agents to recognitions indicating sensitivity of one agent’s state to the other’s messages/updates.
  • Computation: derivative-like effect measured via Social Flow responses when perturbing one agent.

Invariants

  • Monotonicity: stronger influence yields higher recognition.
  • Symmetry: influence need not be symmetric; structure records directionality.

Proof

  • Uses: agent interactions under social flow to measure impact.
  • Shows that: inter-agent influence can be recognised and reasoned about.

title: Information Flow Constraint id: info-flow-constraint role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Information Flow Constraint

Constraints on how recognitions may flow across channels/ports to enforce security.

Definition

  • Policy: lattice of security levels/labels on ports/channels and recognitions.
  • Constraint: forbids flows from higher to lower levels unless declassified.

Invariants

  • Noninterference: low outputs unaffected by high inputs when constraint holds.
  • Stability: constraints preserved under channel composition and port use.

Proof

  • Uses: channel/port labels to define admissible flows.
  • Shows that: policies can be enforced to prevent illicit information flow.

title: Relational Information Engine Program id: information-engine-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Relational Information Engine Program

This program realises the relational computer as an “information engine”: intake payloads become recognitions, measurements become towers, and finite sections of the hypertensor sheaf become the working state. No additional notion of persistent state is introduced beyond the global sheaf section described by the Relational Machine Kernel.

Behaviour

  • Provide a canonical pipeline:
    1. intake/coding from external payloads into recognition carriers with trace,
    2. measurement/normalisation to observables and profiles,
    3. tower construction (profile towers over fibres),
    4. sheaf/hypertensor sections (window, towers),
    5. domain views as constrained projections of those sections.
  • Maintain stable carriers: recognition carriers, profile towers, lattice windows/sites, sheaf sections, and domain-specific carriers where needed.
  • Enforce invariants: schema validity and policy at intake; tower consistency; sheaf-section integrity; row-stochastic Stochastic kernels and fixed-chain consistency; flow–nucleus commutation where applicable.

Intended operators

  • Intake and validation runbooks for observations with traceability.
  • Measurement/tower runbooks to build and evolve towers.
  • Sheaf-section runbooks to build, persist, and step (window, towers) under dynamics.
  • Flow/nucleus runbooks to witness the earned commutation properties.
  • Domain view runbooks that read/write sections under additional domain constraints without altering the underlying relational behaviour.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Information Structure Program id: information-structure-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Information Structure Program

This program models topic and focus so the machine can see what is presented as given versus new or emphasized in an utterance.

Behaviour

  • Represent topics, foci, and given/new partitions as recognitions with links to the entities and contrasts involved.
  • Track how sources topicalize the same events differently and how focus shifts across conversations or news cycles.
  • Measure which recognitions repeatedly appear as topic versus focus, and where content is consistently de-focused or backgrounded.

Intended operators

  • Annotate text or speech for information-structure elements.
  • Compare topicalization across sources for the same event.
  • Guide agents to choose appropriate focus for a goal and audience.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Ingestion Gateway Program id: ingestion-gateway-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Ingestion Gateway Program

Gateway program for ingesting external data into the relational machine. It normalises, validates, and routes intake through policy and topology checks.

Behaviour

  • Accept input streams; normalise payloads to Computer Recognition.
  • Apply policy/intake rules; reject or enrich with traceability.
  • Route valid recognitions to downstream modules via computer wiring plan.
  • Emit Computer Failure on invalid schema, policy violation, or routing error.

Invariants

  • All ingested data is validated, policy-compliant, and traceable.
  • Routing respects topology and wiring constraints.
  • Failures are reported as failure carriers.

Proof

Refines the documentation interpreter by specialising to ingestion wiring and intake checks, preserving kernel failure semantics.

title: Institutions Program id: institutions-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Institutions Program

This program represents institutional structures (schools, orgs, labs) as recognitions so they can be reasoned about like any other system.

Behaviour

  • Model institutions, roles, programs/courses, policies, and governance decisions as recognitions with provenance.
  • Map topology: org charts, program structures, committees, membership.
  • Measure participation, outcomes, resource allocation, churn.

Intended operators

  • Create programs, assign roles, and run reviews using auditable runbooks.
  • Attach policies and decisions to the domains they affect.
  • Surface institutional health via the recorded measurements.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Intake Context Carrier id: intake-context role: structure status: plan derived_from: acts/recognize-context proof_refs: []

Intake Context Carrier

Definition

  • Carrier: links intake observations to recognised contexts and types.
  • Source: earned from Recognize Context; supporting acts Collect Contexts and Collect Types populate the carrier.

Invariants

  • Context/type associations are consistent with the collected recognitions.
  • Closed under additional collected contexts/types without violating prior associations.

Proof

  • Derived from: Recognize Context, which witnesses intake contexts.
  • Shows that: recognising contexts and collecting their instances yields the intake context carrier linking observations to recognised contexts/types.

title: Intake Observation Carrier id: intake-observation role: structure status: plan derived_from: acts/seed-observation proof_refs: []

Intake Observation Carrier

Definition

  • Carrier: intake observations backed by recognitions.
  • Source: earned from Seed Observation; refined by Profile Observation Recognitions to align with profiled recognitions.

Invariants

  • Seeded observations are compatible with profile observation recognitions.
  • Stable under additional profiling without altering existing observation semantics.

Proof

  • Derived from: Seed Observation, which seeds intake observations.
  • Shows that: seeding observations and aligning them with profile recognitions yields the intake observation carrier.

title: Intake Payload Carrier id: intake-payload role: structure status: plan derived_from: acts/seed-observation proof_refs: []

Intake Payload Carrier

Definition

  • Carrier: raw intake data that can be seeded into observations.
  • Source: earned from Seed Observation, which introduces payloads for observation seeding.

Invariants

  • Payloads are admissible inputs for the seeding act.
  • Payload content remains stable through seeding into observations.

Proof

  • Derived from: Seed Observation, which establishes payloads as observation inputs.
  • Shows that: the seeding act induces a carrier of payloads suitable for intake observations.

title: Intake Structure Program id: intake-stack-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Intake Structure Program

This program instantiates the intake structure: a pipeline for normalising, validating, and contextualising incoming payloads into recognised, measured observations before they enter the relational computer.

Behaviour

  • Maintain schema registry recognitions that enumerate admissible payloads, bindings, and normalisation procedures.
  • Record validation stages (seed, recognise, transport) as ordered recognitions so evaluation can replay the same sequence.
  • Seed contexts via the earned context acts and record the trace; nothing is buffered outside the relational state.

Intended operators

  • Normalise payloads into canonical syntax; produce observation recognitions backed by registered schemas.
  • Apply policy/discipline filters in the recorded order; reject unregistered or invalid data.
  • Seed contexts for accepted payloads and emit observations with schema/policy metadata into distribution.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Inter-Ontology Treaty Program id: inter-ontology-treaty-program role: structure status: someday derived_from: computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Inter-Ontology Treaty Program

Program that constructs stable shared structures (“treaties”) between distinct ontological profiles.

Definition

  • Inputs: party ontologies ingested as recognitions via computer Recognition into Computer Recognition carriers; initial Computer Evaluation State.
  • Plan: build composite profiles and candidate treaties as a Computer Wiring Plan, evaluate stability with Evaluate Step.
  • Persistence: checkpoint candidate/accepted treaties via Computer Storage and Computer Snapshot.
  • Outputs: stable treaty wiring plan/recognitions or failures.

Behaviour

  • Represent each party’s ontology as a profile of recognitions with its own constraints and context regime.
  • Construct composite profiles expressing candidate treaty regions.
  • Evaluate stability of candidate agreements under flow/nucleus closure.
  • Emit treaty recognitions for stable intersections; emit failure carriers for impossible alignments.

Invariants

  • Policy/trace alignment: treaties respect both parties’ constraints.
  • Stability: only nucleus-stable intersections are accepted.
  • Failure purity: conflicts emit computer failures; no partial treaty commits.

Proof

  • Derived from: Computer Kernel.
  • Shows that: profile composition and invariant checking express treaty computation within earned semantics via computer operators/carriers.

title: Interface Carrier id: interface-carrier role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Interface Carrier

Math-level carrier for interfaces linking ports/channels and their expected payload schemas.

Definition

  • Components: a collection of Ports Carrier endpoints, associated Message Channels, and schema references for transmitted recognitions/sections.
  • Role: defines the shape of IO for a subsystem.

Invariants

  • Schema adherence: interface enforces schemas on all ports.
  • Connectivity: ports map to channels consistently.

Proof

  • Uses: port and channel definitions to build interface metadata.
  • Shows that: IO interfaces can be specified as math carriers for projection to system/machine layers.

title: Interface Ports id: interface-port role: structure status: plan derived_from: math/structure/policy-regime proof_refs: []

Interface Ports

Statement

Input/output ports through which external data enters and observables exit the system, constrained by policy and measurement stacks.

Definition

  • Carrier: port endpoints parameterised by Policy Regime; each port declares admissible payload types, policy regime, and observability.
  • Ports: InPorts accept intake payloads; OutPorts expose observables.
  • Compatibility: a port is compatible with another when payload/observable types and policy regimes agree.

Invariants

  • Payload/observable typing matches the earned intake/measurement carriers.
  • Policy conformance: ports enforce the attached policy regime.
  • Compatibility is symmetric and respects policy/type constraints.

Proof

  • Derived from: Policy Regime, attaching policy to intake/measurement endpoints.
  • Shows that: parameterising ports by policy yields well-typed interface endpoints for system intake and distribution.

title: Interleaving Reduction id: interleaving-reduction role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Interleaving Reduction

Reduction system for interleaved flows, turning shuffle classes into small-step reductions.

Definition

  • State: a Trace Shuffle Class of interleavings and a current reduction position.
  • Rules: small-step reductions corresponding to Interleaving Flow choices.

Invariants

  • Confluence up to shuffle equivalence.
  • Progress: either a reduction applies or the interleaving is in normal form.

Proof

  • Uses: interleaving flow semantics to define reduction rules.
  • Shows that: concurrency can be modelled as a reduction system aligned with shuffle classes.

title: Interpersonal Relationships and Communication Program id: interpersonal-communication-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Interpersonal Relationships and Communication Program

This program models relationships, boundaries, and conversations as recognitions to see patterns of contact and care.

Behaviour

  • Represent relationships, conversations, agreements/expectations, and ruptures or repairs as recognitions with provenance.
  • Map social graphs and overlapping communities; attach policies and boundaries to relationships.
  • Measure contact frequency, imbalance, conflict frequency, and repair attempts.

Intended operators

  • Check in, hold boundaries, and run repair processes via runbooks.
  • Analyse relationship health using the recorded measurements.
  • Align interpersonal behaviour with stated values and policies.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Interpreter Program id: interpreter-program role: structure status: someday derived_from: computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Interpreter Program

Program-level runbook that turns source text into an executable computer wiring plan and drives it over the documentation interpreter carriers.

Definition

  • Inputs: source text/commands ingested via computer Recognition.
  • Plan: build a Computer Wiring Plan representing the interpreted program, bind it into a Computer Evaluation State, and step it with Evaluate Step.
  • Persistence: checkpoint via Computer Storage and Computer Snapshot.
  • Outputs: executable wiring plan and updated evaluation state/failures surfaced through computer carriers.

Invariants

  • No machine bypass: all parsing/binding goes through computer recognition and wiring operators.
  • Determinism: given the same source and snapshot, produces the same wiring plan and evaluation behaviour.
  • Failure purity: failures surface as Computer Failure; no state mutation on failure.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: interpreting source into a wiring plan and executing it via computer operators yields a runnable interpreter without extending machine semantics.

title: Interprofile Functor id: interprofile-functor role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Interprofile Functor

Functor mapping recognitions from one profile to another while preserving flow/nucleus coherence.

Definition

  • Data: functor between Profile Topos objects of source and target profiles, respecting the Flow–Nucleus Adjunction.
  • Preservation: carries recognitions, flow, and nucleus structure to the target without violating admissibility.

Invariants

  • Functorial: preserves identities/composition of profile morphisms.
  • Adjunction-compatible: commutes with flow/nucleus witnesses.
  • Logical preservation: respects classifier/modal structure in profiles.

Proof

  • Uses: profile toposes and flow/nucleus adjunction to constrain the functor.
  • Shows that: profile recognitions can be transported across worlds coherently.

title: InterTower Map id: intertower-map role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

InterTower Map

Mapping recognitions across different tower types (modal, profile, hypertensor) while preserving coherence.

Definition

  • Data: functorial correspondences between layers of modality/profile towers and hypertensor sections, respecting flow/nucleus.
  • Purpose: transport recognitions coherently between tower geometries.

Invariants

  • Compatibility: maps commute with tower transitions and flow/nucleus.
  • Functorial: preserves composition/identities across towers.

Proof

  • Uses: tower structures to define coherent mappings.
  • Shows that: tower-to-tower transport can be structured as an intertower map.

title: Introspection Channel id: introspection-channel role: structure status: plan derived_from: math/structure/trace-carrier proof_refs: []

Introspection Channel

Channels in the distribution/network used to carry probe outputs to monitoring consumers, preserving trace semantics and storage invariants.

Definition

  • Carrier: specialised Distribution Channel dedicated to introspection payloads emitted by Introspection Probe.
  • Fields: channel endpoints, packet queue of probe outputs, trace span, storage metadata.

Invariants

  • Port compatibility and trace ordering inherited from distribution channels.
  • Payload typing restricted to introspection probe outputs.
  • Integrity of buffered data matches storage metadata.

Proof

  • Derived from: Trace Carrier to attach ordered trace spans.
  • Uses: Distribution Channel Carrier and Introspection Probe to define endpoints/payloads.
  • Shows that: specialising distribution channels to introspection payloads yields an introspection channel carrier ready for monitoring flows.

title: Introspection Probe id: introspection-probe role: structure status: plan derived_from: math/structure/trace-carrier proof_refs: []

Introspection Probe

Defines measurement points attached to ports, channels, or tasks. Each probe declares which observables (progress, entropy, invariants, etc.) it can emit.

Definition

  • Carrier: probe metadata binding an Interface Port or task to a set of observables and a trace span (from Trace Carrier).
  • Fields: target reference, observable types, sampling policy, trace context.

Invariants

  • Target typing: probe target must be a valid interface/task endpoint.
  • Trace alignment: emitted observables are tagged with the trace span.
  • Policy compliance: probes respect policy regimes attached to targets.

Proof

  • Derived from: Trace Carrier to attach ordered spans to observables.
  • Uses: Interface Port to anchor probes to endpoints.
  • Shows that: defining probes as trace-scoped observability points yields telemetry suited for introspection channels.

title: Introspection Program id: introspection-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Introspection Program

This program realises monitoring and feedback loops promised by the introspection stack: observe, analyse, and feed back without perturbing trace semantics.

Behaviour

  • Attach observation hooks to module outputs (entropy, convergence, etc.) using measurement operators.
  • Run monitoring agents that consume observations, analyse them, and optionally trigger alerts while recording provenance.
  • Feed results back through intake so modules can adapt scheduling or behaviour without breaking invariants.

Intended operators

  • Collect metrics periodically or on demand from subscribed observables.
  • Apply predicates to detect anomalies; emit alerts when thresholds are violated.
  • Persist observations and feedback via intake for later audit.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Inventory and Assets Program id: inventory-assets-program role: structure status: plan fields:

  • name: assets type: runtime-value description: recognitions representing physical/digital assets with ids and attributes.
  • name: locations type: runtime-value description: recognitions for where assets reside.
  • name: assignments type: runtime-value description: relations between assets and people/projects/policies.
  • name: maintenance type: runtime-value description: scheduled/completed maintenance recognitions with provenance. proof_refs: []

Inventory and Assets Program

This program models physical and digital assets as recognitions to track what you own, where it is, and how it is used.

Behaviour

  • Represent assets, locations, assignments, and maintenance events as recognitions with provenance.
  • Map topology: asset↔location, asset↔person, asset↔projects.
  • Measure usage frequency, age, condition, and cost-of-ownership.

Intended operators

  • Add and move assets; schedule maintenance; decommission assets.
  • Assign assets to people or projects with policy constraints.
  • Review asset health and lifecycle using recorded measurements.

Proof

  • Uses: earned carrier/operator stack (recognitions, wiring, persistence) to model asset/location/assignment/maintenance recognitions.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the earned kernel to realise the described behaviour; no extra semantics beyond earned operators are assumed.

title: Job Orchestrator Program id: job-orchestrator-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Job Orchestrator Program

Schedules and runs jobs (runbooks) over the documentation interpreter. Wires policy, scheduling, and telemetry to execute acyclic plans safely using computer carriers and operators.

Behaviour

  • Register jobs with dependencies and policy constraints as Computer Recognition.
  • Build and validate Computer Wiring Plans; schedule execution respecting dependencies.
  • Drive execution via Evaluate Step; monitor via telemetry and handle retries/failures.
  • Emit Computer Failure on policy, wiring, or execution failure.

Invariants

  • Jobs run in dependency order and under policy constraints.
  • Wiring remains acyclic and traceable.
  • Failures are captured as failure carriers with provenance.

Proof

Refines the Documentation Interpreter by specialising to job registration and scheduling over computer plans, preserving kernel semantics and failure paths.

title: Job Search and Career Program id: job-search-career-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Job Search and Career Program

This program represents jobs, applications, interviews, and skill gaps as recognitions to manage career development systematically.

Behaviour

  • Model job opportunities, applications, interviews, and skill gaps as recognitions with provenance.
  • Link jobs to skills, people/organizations, and projects; track application stages and outcomes.
  • Measure application counts, response rates, time-in-stage, and offer quality.

Intended operators

  • Track applications and prepare for interviews as auditable runbooks.
  • Review offers and align choices with skills, goals, and value recognitions.
  • Surface skill gaps and connect to learning/curriculum programs.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Judgment Binder id: judgment-binder role: structure status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/binder-seed proof_refs: []

Judgment Binder

Binder/abstraction structure for the judgment calculus, earned by binder acts over term bodies.

Components

  • Binder seed introducing a binding for a term body.
  • Transport of binders across contexts/terms.
  • Application of binders to arguments (abstraction over term bodies).

Proof

Sketch: BinderSeed introduces a binding; BinderTransport moves it through contexts; BinderApplication applies it to arguments, yielding the abstraction constructor for judgment terms.

title: Judgment Calculus Program id: judgment-calculus-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Judgment Calculus Program

This program treats judgments and their compositions as recognitions so logical reasoning remains aligned with the earned calculus.

Behaviour

  • Represent judgments, sequents, and derivations as recognitions with provenance.
  • Ensure operators on judgments respect admissibility and normalization obligations from the underlying math.
  • Measure derivation depth, branching, and reuse to surface proof health.

Intended operators

  • Form and compose judgments; check admissibility and normalization.
  • Trace derivations back to the acts and structures they rely on.
  • Export judgment traces for other programs (e.g., policy, reasoning agents).

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Judgment Dual id: judgment-dual role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Judgment Dual

Dual structure of a judgment: premises and conclusions swapped via flow reversal.

Definition

  • Carrier: Judgment Triad transformed by reversing flow direction, interchanging premise/conclusion roles.
  • Structure: includes dual flow/nucleus behaviour.

Invariants

  • Duality: applying dual twice returns to original (up to equivalence).
  • Compatibility: duals respect admissible flow reversal.

Proof

  • Uses: judgment triads and flow reversal to define duals.
  • Shows that: judgments admit a dual structure capturing reverse entailment.

title: Judgment Evaluation Context id: judgment-evaluation-context role: structure status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/seed-evaluation-context proof_refs: []

Judgment Evaluation Context

Evaluation frames for the judgment calculus, earned by SeedEvaluationContext and BuildEvaluationContext.

Components

  • Carrier EvalCtx of frames with a single hole.
  • Neutral frame empty from SeedEvaluationContext.
  • Frame extension along the trace from BuildEvaluationContext.

Proof

Sketch: generate frames by iterating BuildEvaluationContext from empty; frames extend contexts along the trace with a unique hole.

title: Judgment Calculus Soundness id: judgment-soundness role: structure status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/sound-syntax proof_refs: []

Judgment Calculus Soundness

Metatheory of the internal judgment calculus: preservation and progress earned by SoundSyntax, PreserveTyping, and ProgressTyping.

Components

  • Preservation: if Γ ⊢ t : A and t → t' (relational evaluation), then Γ ⊢ t' : A.
  • Progress: every well-typed closed term is either a value or can take a relational evaluation step.

Proof

Sketch: SoundSyntax sets admissible derivations; PreserveTyping and ProgressTyping acts witness preservation and progress for the earned evaluation relation and typing judgments. Combine to obtain soundness for the judgment calculus.

title: Judgment Term id: judgment-term role: structure status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/form-term-body proof_refs: []

Judgment Term

Term structure for the internal judgment calculus, earned by the term-forming acts (variables/bodies, abstractions/applications, fixpoint constructors).

Components

  • Variables and bodies (EquateEventually, FormTermBody).
  • Abstractions/applications (FormFunctionTerm, FormApplicationTerm).
  • Least/greatest fixpoint constructors (PairLeastFix, FormLeastFixTerm, PairGreatestFix, FormGreatestFixTerm).

Proof

Sketch: each syntactic constructor has a corresponding earned act; their compositions generate all well-formed judgment-calculus terms. Show closure and well-formedness under these constructors.

title: Judgment Triad id: judgment-triad role: structure status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/trace-judgement-triad proof_refs: []

Judgment Triad

The Judgment Triad bundles context, term, and observation recognitions along a trace.

Definition

  • Built by the seed/step/trace judgment triad acts, producing a trace of judgments (context, term, observation) over the trace infrastructure.

Invariants

  • Trace captures the full history of judgments with admissible steps.
  • Components remain in their earned carriers.

Proof

Derived from the triad acts (seed-judgement-triad, step-judgement-triad, trace-judgement-triad), which inductively build the judgment trace over contexts, terms, and observations.

title: Judgment Type id: judgment-type role: structure status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/type-application proof_refs: []

Judgment Type

The Judgment Type structure is the inductive type grammar of the internal judgment calculus.

Definition

  • Base types from TypeSeed.
  • Type variables from TypeVariable.
  • Arrow and other type formers from TypeApplication.

Invariants

  • Types are built inductively from the earned constructors.
  • Well-formedness follows from the generating acts.

Proof

Derived from the type seed/variable/application acts, yielding the usual inductive type grammar mirrored by the term structure.

title: Judgment Typing id: judgment-typing role: structure status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/flow-judgement proof_refs: []

Judgment Typing

The Judgment Typing structure encodes the typing judgements Γ ⊢ t : A of the internal judgment calculus as earned judgment triads.

Definition

  • Built from the judgment triad acts (seed-judgement-triad, flow-judgement) over contexts, terms, and types.
  • Typing rules (variable, abstraction, application, fixpoint) correspond to compositions of these acts along the trace.

Invariants

  • Typing derivations are admissible traces of judgment triads.
  • Flow of judgments preserves typing structure.

Proof

Derived from the judgment triad and flow acts, which encode typing derivations as traces over the judgment triad carrier. Each typing rule is realised as an earned composition of these acts.

title: Judgment Value id: judgment-value role: structure status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/seed-value proof_refs: []

Judgment Value

Value carrier for the judgment calculus, earned by SeedValue and RecognizeValue.

Components

  • Seeded values from SeedValue.
  • Recognition of values at the head of the trace via RecognizeValue.

Proof

Sketch: SeedValue provides initial values; RecognizeValue identifies values in the trace. Together define the value carrier for the evaluation structure.

title: Jungian Psychology Program id: jungian-psychology-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Jungian Psychology Program

This program models Jungian constructs (archetypes, shadow, individuation) as recognitions to trace how they appear across narratives and experience.

Behaviour

  • Represent archetypes, complexes, shadow material, and individuation stages as recognitions with context and provenance.
  • Link these recognitions to dreams, discourse, and interpersonal patterns.
  • Measure frequency and evolution of motifs over time.

Intended operators

  • Annotate texts or experiences for Jungian motifs.
  • Track individuation progress and shadow integration attempts.
  • Relate Jungian patterns to other psychology programs for synthesis.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Kernel Machine Program id: kernel-machine-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Kernel Machine Program

A computer-layer packaging of the machine kernel for deployment. It instantiates the earned carriers and operators of the kernel as a runnable computer target.

Behaviour

  • Initialise kernel carriers (section, evaluation state, recognitions, snapshots, wiring plan).
  • Provide endpoints/runbooks to execute kernel operators.
  • Expose traceability and telemetry for kernel-level operations.

Invariants

  • Kernel semantics are preserved: no extra instructions or state beyond the earned kernel.
  • Programs run as acyclic wirings over kernel operators.
  • Failures use Computer Failure.

Proof

This program instantiates the machine kernel in the computer layer; proof shows it introduces no new semantics beyond the kernel and preserves kernel invariants while using computer carriers/operators as the exposed interface.

title: Knowledge Edge id: knowledge-edge role: structure status: plan derived_from: math/structure/trace-carrier proof_refs: []

Knowledge Edge

Edges identify a source/destination knowledge node and an act-justified edge type, plus trace/topology/policy/audit context. Edge records live in the relational state.

Definition

  • Carrier: connects two Knowledge Nodes with an act-justified edge type, trace span, and policy/audit metadata.
  • Fields: source node, destination node, edge type, justification acts, trace context, tags/policy.

Invariants

  • Node validity: endpoints must be valid knowledge nodes.
  • Traceability: edges carry trace spans for provenance.
  • Policy coherence: edge tags/policy align with node policies.

Proof

  • Derived from: Trace Carrier to attach sequencing/provenance.
  • Uses: Knowledge Node as endpoints.
  • Shows that: wiring recognitions with justified edges yields a knowledge edge carrier suitable for the system knowledge graph.

title: Knowledge Graph Program id: knowledge-graph-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Knowledge Graph Program

This program treats knowledge graphs as recognitions so ontology, provenance, and measurement stay aligned with the relational calculus.

Behaviour

  • Represent entities, relations, and statements as recognitions with explicit provenance and policy bindings.
  • Support typed schemas and alignment with external vocabularies without breaking the earned relational semantics.
  • Measure coverage, consistency, and change over time.

Intended operators

  • Ingest and align external graphs into recognition carriers.
  • Query, merge, and diff slices with traceable results.
  • Enforce schema/policy constraints and surface inconsistencies for repair.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Knowledge Node id: knowledge-node role: structure status: plan derived_from: math/structure/recognition proof_refs: []

Knowledge Node

Nodes in the knowledge graph are recognition carriers annotated with role, justifying acts, payload, and tags/metadata for classification/policy.

Definition

  • Carrier: a Recognition Carrier instance with additional fields: role (e.g., fact, hypothesis), justifying acts, tags, and policy bindings.

Invariants

  • Recognition validity: node must be an earned recognition.
  • Policy/tag coherence: tags/policies align with recognition payload.
  • Traceability: retains recognition provenance/trace.

Proof

  • Derived from: Recognition Carrier.
  • Shows that: annotating recognitions with role/tags yields knowledge nodes for system-level knowledge graphs.

title: Lab Notebook Program id: lab-notebook-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Lab Notebook Program

This program records experiments, observations, and analyses as recognitions so scientific work is traceable and auditable.

Behaviour

  • Capture experiments, hypotheses, procedures, observations, and analyses as recognitions with provenance and measurement channels.
  • Link entries to instruments, samples, and versions; keep timelines coherent.
  • Measure replication status, data quality, and outcome validity.

Intended operators

  • Log experiments and observations with trace.
  • Attach analysis steps and outcomes; flag replication or follow-up needs.
  • Export slices for publication or collaboration while preserving provenance.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Lacanian Subjectivity Program id: lacanian-subjectivity-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Lacanian Subjectivity Program

This program models Lacanian concepts (symbolic, imaginary, real; desire; subject positions) as recognitions to trace how they manifest in discourse and relation.

Behaviour

  • Represent subject positions, objet petit a, signifiers, and mirror-stage constructs as recognitions with context and provenance.
  • Link to discourse, dreams, and interpersonal recognitions to observe shifts and repetitions.
  • Measure patterns of desire, lack, and symbolic/imaginary interplay over time.

Intended operators

  • Annotate discourse for Lacanian motifs.
  • Trace subject positions across interactions.
  • Compare Lacanian readings with other psychoanalytic programs.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.

title: Language Learning Program id: language-learning-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Language Learning Program
