  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Operations & Control Program id: operations-control-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Operations & Control Program

The Operations & Control Program assembles documentation-interpreter operators into an acyclic plan that manages configuration, approvals, maintenance, and incident response across the relational computer.

Carrier

  • Config recognitions: declarative configs (routing, policies, resource limits) expressed as Computer Recognition with traceability and topology metadata.
  • Approval workflow: recognition/state capturing multi-step approvals in Computer Evaluation State.
  • Maintenance tasks: wiring of scheduler, storage/replication, and testing steps for drains, rollouts, and backups encoded as Computer Wiring Plan over computer operators.
  • Failure carriers: emitted when policy, topology, or wiring constraints are violated, as Computer Failure.

Behaviour

  1. Validate proposed changes against policy and topology; record approvals.
  2. Execute maintenance tasks via scheduler, using storage/replication to drain and resync modules; publish telemetry.
  3. Coordinate incident response with monitoring/audit services; trigger rollbacks/failovers when guardrails allow.
  4. Emit Computer Failure on authorization failure, invalid config, or maintenance errors.

Invariants

  • Every operational change is policy-authorized, traceable to acts, and audited.
  • Maintenance tasks respect dependency order and topology constraints.
  • Deployments/failovers rely on validated artifacts and restorable snapshots.

Proof

Refines the computer Kernel by specialising wiring to operations/control tasks and constraining ports to config/approval/maintenance carriers plus failure handling. Shows that successful executions maintain policy/traceability invariants; failures yield Computer Failure.

title: Operator Id id: operator-id role: structure status: plan proof_refs:

  • expression fields:
  • name: symbol type: runtime-value description: canonical operator identifier used in expressions and signatures.

Operator Id

Identifier naming an operator in the documentation DAG.

Proof

  • Uses: the nested-array call form uses operator ids as heads; this carrier records that identifier for typing and dispatch.

title: Operator Signature id: operator-signature role: structure status: plan emerges_from: judgment-typing proof_refs:

  • judgment-typing
  • judgment-triad
  • recognition fields:
  • name: op_id type: operator-id description: canonical operator identifier.
  • name: inputs type: runtime-value description: ordered list of {id, alias} carrier references consumed.
  • name: outputs type: runtime-value description: ordered list of {id, alias} carrier references produced.
  • name: typing_judgment type: runtime-value description: witness showing op_id : inputs -> outputs.

Operator Signature

Typed descriptor bundling an operator id with the ordered inputs/outputs it is permitted to consume/produce, expressed as an earned typing judgment. The dispatcher consults signatures before evaluating transformations.

Definition

  • op_id: canonical operator identifier (string/symbol).
  • inputs: ordered list of {id, alias} carrier references expected by the operator.
  • outputs: ordered list of {id, alias} carrier references produced by the operator.
  • typing_judgment: the Judgment Typing witness that op_id : inputs → outputs.

Invariants

  • Arity: inputs length matches the arity in typing_judgment.
  • Typing: every listed carrier id is an earned structure; typing_judgment proves the mapping is total (or explicitly returns failure carriers).
  • Identity: op_id is unique within the operator registry; signatures are immutable once published.

Proof

  • Uses: Judgment Typing and Judgment Triad to encode operator domains/codomains as judgments.
  • Shows that: packaging typing evidence into a signature allows the dispatcher to validate arity/types mechanically before applying a transformation.

title: Ops Monitoring and Dashboards Program id: ops-monitoring-dashboards-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Ops Monitoring and Dashboards Program

This program provides operational monitoring and dashboards as recognitions so observability is traceable.

Behaviour

  • Represent metrics, alerts, dashboards, and incidents as recognitions with provenance.
  • Map service topologies and dependencies; attach measurements to components.
  • Measure reliability, latency, error rates, and alert quality.

Intended operators

  • Collect and aggregate ops metrics with trace.
  • Configure dashboards and alerts; record incidents and responses.
  • Analyse reliability and tune alerting to reduce noise.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Parameterized Base-Change Condition id: parameterized-base-change-condition role: structure status: plan derived_from: math/structure/base-change-condition proof_refs: []

Parameterized Base-Change Condition

Executable lift of the Base-Change Condition to the parameterized coverage/sheaf setting, providing code-level stability of pullback and pushforward for subobjects/recognitions.

Definition

  • Context: pullback squares and covers in the Parameterized Coverage Site with sheaves implemented by Parameterized Sheaf.
  • Condition: for any subobject represented by a classifier/recognition payload, the computed pullback of a pushforward equals the pushforward of a pullback, using executable restriction and direct-image routines.
  • Representation: characteristic maps as serialized payloads into the classifier type; base-change witness includes provenance of the two computed routes.

Invariants

  • Stability: pullback/pushforward procedures commute on every admissible square using the same parameterized routines.
  • Naturality: base-change witnesses compose under refinement of covers or pullback of squares.
  • Determinism: given identical inputs, the two routes produce byte-identical classifier payloads or emit explicit failure.

Proof

  • Derived from: Base-Change Condition by instantiating pullback/pushforward and classifier calculations with executable routines from the parameterized sheaf.
  • Shows that: substitution/logical predicates remain stable under reindexing in the calculus setting, with concrete witnesses for commuting diagrams.

title: Parameterized Coverage Site id: parameterized-coverage-site role: structure status: plan derived_from: math/structure/coverage-site proof_refs: []

Parameterized Coverage Site

Lift of the Coverage Site that pins down computational parameters: index sets for covers, overlap resolution, and explicit restriction maps suitable for executable flows.

Definition

  • Parameters: finite index sets for covers, overlap computation rules, and datatype representations of sections.
  • Components: concrete cover tables, restriction functions, and explicit overlap constructors that can be iterated in evaluation contexts.
  • Base-change hooks: algorithms for pulling a cover back along a morphism and simplifying the resulting family.

Invariants

  • Parameter soundness: chosen index sets and overlap rules respect the abstract coverage axioms and do not omit required refinements.
  • Total restriction: every cover arrow carries an executable restriction map that composes correctly when arrows are composed.
  • Glue readiness: outputs matching-family data in a format consumed by sheaf gluing lifts and path-composition lifts.

Proof

  • Derived from: Coverage Site by instantiating covers/restrictions with explicit indices and computational overlap rules.
  • Shows that: the abstract coverage can be executed with explicit indices, enabling system-level distributions and machine-level runtime representations.

title: Parameterized Matching Family id: parameterized-matching-family role: structure status: plan derived_from: math/structure/matching-family proof_refs: []

Parameterized Matching Family

Executable matching-family carrier over a Parameterized Coverage Site, refining the math matching family with concrete indices, overlap computations, and serialized sections.

Definition

  • Cover: finite-index family {Ui → U} drawn from the parameterized coverage site, with overlap constructors and restriction functions available as code.
  • Sections: parameterized section payloads si over each Ui whose restrictions agree on overlaps Ui ×_U Uj, expressed as explicit equality checks over serialized section data.
  • Coherence: cached witnesses for triple overlaps to ensure pairwise equality extends simplicially.
  • Packaging: stores cover indices, section payloads, and coherence evidence so calculus operators can glue or transport families.

Invariants

  • Compatibility: restrict(si, Ui∩Uj) = restrict(sj, Ui∩Uj) for all pairs, checked using the executable restriction maps.
  • Refinement stability: refining the cover and re-running restrictions yields a new parameterized matching family with preserved coherence.
  • Transport: restriction along parameterized paths/covers preserves the matching property, enabling path composition lifts to reuse cached checks.

Proof

  • Derived from: Matching Family by instantiating covers, overlaps, and section data with executable parameters from the parameterized coverage site.
  • Shows that: the abstract matching-family notion is realizable in calculus with concrete indices and serialization, ready for distributed/system lifts.

title: Parameterized Section id: parameterized-section role: structure status: plan derived_from: math/structure/hypertensor-section proof_refs: []

Parameterized Section

Lift of the Hypertensor Section that fixes concrete encodings of sections over the parameterized coverage site.

Definition

  • Base: windows from the Parameterized Coverage Site.
  • Payload: serialized section data (e.g., JSON/YAML or binary) together with executable restriction hooks supplied by the parameterization.
  • Restriction: a callable restrict(section, V→U) that re-encodes the section on sub-windows using the parameterized overlap and restriction tables.

Invariants

  • Total restriction: restriction is defined for every admissible cover arrow and composes correctly with identities and composites.
  • Encoding stability: serialization/deserialization of sections is lossless for the allowed payloads; hashes or digests can be attached for integrity.
  • Cover compatibility: restrictions respect cover refinements and are usable by matching-family and gluing operators.

Proof

  • Derived from: Hypertensor Section by fixing executable restriction functions and concrete encodings tied to the parameterized coverage site.
  • Shows that: sections can be manipulated programmatically (restricted, glued, composed) while preserving the earned sheaf structure at the calculus level.

title: Parameterized Sheaf id: parameterized-sheaf role: structure status: plan derived_from: math/structure/sheaf proof_refs: []

Parameterized Sheaf

Lift of the Sheaf that equips the coverage site with executable restriction maps, matching-family checks, and gluing constructors.

Definition

  • Objects: parameterized presheaves on the coverage site with concrete restriction functions and section encodings.
  • Matching/gluing: every cover yields a Parameterized Matching Family and a computable amalgamation routine that returns a section over the covered window.
  • Interfaces: explicit APIs for restriction, overlap computation, and gluing that can be called by evaluation contexts.

Invariants

  • Locality: equal restrictions on a cover imply equality of sections, checked via deterministic restriction functions.
  • Glueability: every parameterized matching family glues uniquely; the implementation must be deterministic and total on valid inputs.
  • Base-change stability: pulling a cover back via parameterized morphisms preserves sheaves and their gluing procedures.

Proof

  • Derived from: Sheaf by providing executable counterparts to restriction, matching, and gluing over the parameterized coverage site.
  • Shows that: the sheaf condition survives concretization, enabling system and machine layers to execute gluing and locality checks over real payloads.

title: Parametric Relation id: parametric-relation role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Parametric Relation

Family of relations stable under all reindexings in an indexed category, used to state parametricity.

Definition

  • Carrier: relations R_C in each fibre of the Indexed Category, compatible with Parametric Semantics.
  • Stability: for any base morphism, reindexing preserves relatedness.

Invariants

  • Naturality: reindexing a related pair yields a related pair.
  • Closure: relations are closed under earned modal/flow structure in the fibres.

Proof

  • Uses: Indexed Category and Parametric Semantics to define fibrewise relations.
  • Shows that: parametric relations capture uniform behaviour across contexts.

title: Parametric Runtime Program id: parametric-runtime-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Parametric Runtime Program

The Parametric Runtime Program is a computer-layer refinement of the kernel that runs parameterised runbooks. It wires recognition, evaluation, and storage operators to load parameters, instantiate runbooks, and execute them under policy/topology constraints.

Carrier

  • Runbook parameters: recognitions describing parameter sets and admissible bindings.
  • Execution wiring: a Computer Wiring Plan instantiated with those parameters.
  • Failure carriers: emitted when bindings are invalid or evaluation fails as Computer Failure.

Behaviour

  1. Validate parameter bindings against policy and topology constraints.
  2. Instantiate the wiring plan with the bindings.
  3. Execute evaluation steps over computer carriers via Evaluate Step; persist snapshots as needed.
  4. Emit Computer Failure on invalid parameters, wiring errors, or evaluation failures.

Invariants

  • Parameter bindings respect earned constraints (policy, topology, typing).
  • Execution remains acyclic and deterministic per the wiring plan.
  • Snapshots permit rollback if a parameterised run fails.

Proof

Refines the documentation interpreter by introducing parameter binding before execution. Shows that only admissible bindings produce a valid plan and that failures are captured as Computer Failure.

title: Parametric Semantics Program id: parametric-semantics-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Parametric Semantics Program

This program treats parametric semantics as recognitions to make meaning under parameters explicit and traceable.

Behaviour

  • Represent semantic parameters, environments, and interpretations as recognitions with provenance.
  • Link parameters to domains and contexts; ensure parameter lifting respects earned semantics.
  • Measure stability and variation of meanings under parameter changes.

Intended operators

  • Instantiate and apply parametric interpretations.
  • Compare semantic outcomes across parameter settings.
  • Surface where parameters must be constrained for coherence.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Parametric Semantics id: parametric-semantics role: structure status: plan derived_from: acts/calculate-semantics proof_refs: []

Parametric Semantics

Parametric Semantics capture meanings that vary with explicit parameters but remain grounded in the base semantics earned by Calculate Semantics.

Definition

  • Carrier: interpretations indexed over the Index Base that remain functorial with respect to base morphisms.
  • Structure: each interpretation is accompanied by a proof that reindexing along the context flow/nucleus adjunction preserves its meaning.
  • Parameters: the admissible base morphisms and profiles supplied by the index base; no additional modal structure is assumed at math level.

Invariants

  • Reindexing-stable: pulling an interpretation along any base morphism yields a meaning related by the adjunction witness (flow/nucleus).
  • Context-closed: only interpretations over the context geometry are permitted, matching the index base act.

Proof

  • Derived from: Calculate Semantics, which earns the core semantic interpretation.
  • Shows that: once semantics are calculated over the context geometry, they can be organised into an indexed family that is stable under reindexing, giving a parametric semantics carrier in the math layer.

title: Parametric Uniformity id: parametric-uniformity role: Uniformity theorem packaging indexed contexts and modal structure coherence. status: plan inputs: [] outputs:

  • id: parametric-uniformity name: uniformity proof_refs: []

Parametric Uniformity

Parametric semantics live entirely inside the math layer. The acts listed below witness that reindexing and modal structure are already present and interact coherently; calculus instances import this theorem.

1. Indexed contexts

Theorem 1 (Indexed context category).
Indexed Category equips the context layer with reindexing functors. Context Flow, Context Nucleus, and Dynamic Residuation form the adjoint pair used for reindexing, and Context Adjunction Witness records the unit/counit equations.

Proof sketch. IndexedCategory earns the fibred structure. The flow is left adjoint to the nucleus by Dynamic Residuation, so every reindexing operation arises from composing these adjoints. The adjunction witness ensures coherence across base-change. ∎

2. Modal structure

Theorem 2 (Modal structure coherence).
Modal Structure extends the indexed category with modal operators and their adjunctions. Reindexing along the base preserves the modal structure via the adjointness given by the acts above.

Proof sketch. The adjunction data (flow/nucleus, residuation, adjunction witness) carries through modal structure; reindexing is given by composing these adjoints, so modal laws hold in every fibre. ∎

3. Abstraction theorem

Theorem 3 (Parametric abstraction).
Given an indexed category with coherent modal structure, any morphism that respects reindexing is uniform across all contexts: related arguments remain related after flowing along the adjunction.

Proof sketch. Reindexing along the base preserves the modal structure; a morphism natural with respect to all such reindexings cannot distinguish contextual changes, yielding uniformity. ∎

4. Logical relations and contextual equivalence

Theorem 4 (From parametricity to contextual equivalence).
Let ~ₚ be the parametric relation induced by naturality with respect to all reindexings and ~ctx the contextual equivalence witnessed in Relational Equivalence. Then ~ₚ refines ~ctx: parametric equals imply contextual equality.

Proof sketch. Uniformity under all reindexings implies indistinguishability by any context built from flow/nucleus; relational equivalence characterises that indistinguishability, so the parametric relation is contained within it. ∎

Summary

Feature Witnessing acts
Indexed contexts Indexed Category, ContextFlow, ContextNucleus, DynamicResiduation, ContextAdjunctionWitness
Modal structure Modal Structure
Abstraction theorem this doc
Link to contextual equivalence this doc + Relational Equivalence

This theorem packages the entire parametric semantics story inside the calculus, so later texts can cite it whenever uniformity across contexts is required.

Proof

  • TODO: fill proof.

title: Parametricity Instance id: parametricity-instance role: Instance of parametric uniformity for the indexed monad/comonad data. status: plan inputs: [] outputs:

  • id: parametricity-instance name: instance proof_refs: []

Calculus Parametricity Instance

Instantiation of the math-level parametric uniformity theorem, showing that the parametricity operator over the indexed monad/comonad data realises the math theorem.

Statement

  • Given the effect/coeffect structures and reindexing operators listed above, the Parametricity operator satisfies the math-level parametric uniformity properties (naturality under reindexing, uniformity across contexts).

Proof

  • Derived from: Math Parametric Uniformity.
  • Uses: modal structures/operators (effect/coeffect monads, fibres, reindexing, modules, natural transformations) to show the instance meets the hypotheses of the math theorem.
  • Shows that: the parametricity operator is an instance of the math parametric uniformity, so abstraction inherits the uniformity and contextual equivalence refinement.

title: Parametricity Typing Bridge id: parametricity-typing-bridge role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Parametricity Typing Bridge

Integrates parametric relations/abstraction into the typed judgment system to ensure contextual equivalence for DSL optimizations.

Definition

  • Components: typing rules annotated with Parametric Relation obligations; appeal to Abstraction Theorem to infer contextual equivalence.
  • Purpose: guarantee that well-typed, parametric terms are indistinguishable across contexts.

Invariants

  • Uniformity: typed terms respect all parametric relations.
  • Contextual equivalence: parametric relatedness implies observational equality.

Proof

  • Uses: abstraction theorem to lift parametricity into typing.
  • Shows that: typed derivations carry parametric guarantees for optimization and refinement in the DSL/ISA.

title: Pattern Carrier id: pattern-carrier role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Pattern Carrier

Carrier of recognitions representing stable patterns across hypertensor windows.

Definition

  • Elements: recognitions in Hypertensor Section that remain invariant (or periodic/aperiodic) under Flow Context and restriction/gluing.
  • Structure: includes pattern descriptors and stability witnesses.

Invariants

  • Stability: patterns persist under admissible flow and nucleus.
  • Coverage: patterns are compatible with window covers and restrictions.

Proof

  • Uses: hypertensor sections and flow to detect stable shapes.
  • Shows that: recurring shapes form a carrier for pattern analysis.

title: Persistence Program id: persistence-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Persistence Program

This program specifies persistence of recognitions and sections so durability is controlled and auditable.

Behaviour

  • Represent persistence policies, snapshots, and retention schedules as recognitions with provenance.
  • Tie persistence to trace carriers and storage backends without breaking relational invariants.
  • Measure durability, integrity checks, and recovery performance.

Intended operators

  • Persist and restore recognitions/sections according to policy.
  • Run integrity checks and recoveries with traceable results.
  • Manage retention and deletion aligned with policy and consent.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Persistence Structure id: persistence-structure kind: structure status: plan role: “Contexts/recognitions that can be saved and restored without losing meaning.” inputs: [] outputs:

  • {id: persistence-structure, name: persistence} program: [“struct”, “persistence-structure”] depends_on:
  • seed-first-context
  • renew-context
  • extend-context
  • retrace-context
  • baseline-limit
  • fixed-profile-recognitions
  • fixed-invariant
  • reach

Persistence Structure

The context, baseline, fixed-invariant, and reachability acts induce a persistence structure: contexts and recognitions that can be saved and restored without losing their relational meaning.

Formally, there exists a structure

Persistence = (PersistentContexts, PersistentRecognitions, SaveContext, LoadContext)

such that:

  1. Context anchoring.
    SeedFirstContext, RenewContext, ExtendContext, and RetraceContext ensure any earned context in PersistentContexts can be saved and restored without altering its trace interpretation. Saving a context corresponds to RenewContext; loading corresponds to RetraceContext.

  2. Baseline retention.
    BaselineLimit records the stabilised tail that survives collapse. Persisted contexts always reference this baseline so that rehydrated traces resume from the same remainder.

  3. Fixed recognitions.
    FixedProfileRecognitions and FixedInvariant identify PersistentRecognitions: the subset of recognitions eligible for storage, namely those fixed by the flow/nucleus stack and stable over time.

  4. Reachability guarantees.
    Reach shows that once a recognition is marked as persistent (fixed invariant), it remains reachable whenever the stored context is reloaded.

Together these acts and this persistence structure prove that any stored context/recognition can be reloaded later, picks up the same baseline tail, and still satisfies the invariants that justified storing it in the first place. This is part of the mathematical backbone for persistent relational storage.

Proof sketch

  1. Context anchoring.
    SeedFirstContext, RenewContext, ExtendContext, and RetraceContext earn the ability to save and replay contexts along traces without changing their interpretation. This establishes PersistentContexts with save_context/load_context given by renewal and retracing.

  2. Baseline retention.
    BaselineLimit isolates the stabilised tail. Persisted contexts carry this baseline so that rehydrated traces pick up from the same remainder; this is enforced by composing renewal with the baseline limit.

  3. Fixed recognitions.
    FixedProfileRecognitions and FixedInvariant identify the recognitions that are stable under flow/nucleus. Define PersistentRecognitions as this fixed subset; only these may be stored alongside contexts.

  4. Reachability.
    Reach earns that fixed recognitions remain reachable when a context is reloaded. Therefore load_context restores both context and its fixed recognitions without losing meaning.

  5. Uniqueness.
    Any persistence mechanism respecting these acts yields the same saved content and round-trips contexts/recognitions up to nucleus equivalence. Hence Persistence is unique in the earned sense.

title: Persistent Context Carrier id: persistent-context-carrier role: structure status: plan derived_from: acts/retrace-context proof_refs: []

Persistent Context Carrier

Definition

  • Carrier: contexts that can be saved and replayed with a stabilised baseline tail without changing relational meaning.
  • Components: initial seeding, renewal/extension along a trace, retracing over a stored baseline.
  • Source: earned via Retrace Context, with supporting acts Seed First Context, Renew Context, Extend Context, and Baseline Limit.

Invariants

  • Closed under renewal and retrace: replaying a persisted context yields an equivalent context.
  • Baseline stability: the tail provided by BaselineLimit remains fixed across save/reload cycles.

Proof

  • Derived from: Retrace Context, which earns the ability to replay stored contexts.
  • Shows that: the retrace act, together with seeding, renewal/extension, and baseline fixation, induces a carrier of contexts stable under persistence and replay.

title: Persistent Recognition Carrier id: persistent-recognition role: structure status: plan derived_from: acts/fixed-profile-recognitions proof_refs: []

Persistent Recognition Carrier

Definition

  • Carrier: recognitions fixed by the flow/nucleus stack that remain reachable after persistence.
  • Components: fixed-profile recognitions earned by Fixed Profile Recognitions and stability witness from Fixed Invariant; reachability earned by Reach.

Invariants

  • Flow/nucleus fixed: recognitions remain unchanged under the earned flow and nucleus actions.
  • Reachable on reload: persisting and reloading a context preserves the ability to arrive at these recognitions.

Proof

  • Derived from: Fixed Profile Recognitions, which isolates the stable subset under flow/nucleus.
  • Shows that: adding the reachability condition from Reach yields a carrier of recognitions that survive persistence, forming the persistent recognition carrier.

Invariants

  • Phase coherence: relative phases stay locked.
  • Robustness: small perturbations do not break synchrony.

Proof

  • Shows that: synchronizable patterns form a recognisable carrier.

title: Physics Soundness and Invariants id: physics-soundness-invariants role: Soundness/invariant package for the fixed physics profile. status: plan inputs: [] outputs:

  • id: physics-soundness-invariants name: physics-soundness proof_refs: []

Physics Soundness and Invariants

In the setting of the fixed physics profile, with acts

  • FixedProfileRecognitions = (ShuffleProfileRecognitions FixedShape),
  • ProfileSemantics = (StructureSemantics ProfileRecognitions),
  • FixedProfileSemantics = (ProfileSemantics FixedProfileRecognitions),
  • FixedFlowProfile = (ShuffleFlow FixedProfileRecognitions),
  • FixedNucleateProfile = (Nucleate FixedProfileRecognitions),
  • FixedGeometryProfile = (FixedFlowProfile FixedNucleateProfile),
  • FixedPhysicsProfile = (FlowObservation FixedFlowProfile),

and the derived acts

  • SoundProfilePhysics = (PhysicsProfile ProfileSemantics),
  • SoundFixedPhysicsSemantics = (SoundProfilePhysics FixedProfileSemantics),
  • SoundFixedPhysicsGeometry = (FixedPhysicsProfile FixedGeometryProfile),
  • FixedInvariant = (StabilizeInvariant FixedProfileRecognitions),

the following hold:

  1. Semantic soundness.
    SoundFixedPhysicsSemantics witnesses that every judgement realised by FixedPhysicsProfile is valid in FixedProfileSemantics: fixed-physical evolutions and observations never leave the fixed profile semantics.

  2. Geometric soundness.
    SoundFixedPhysicsGeometry witnesses that fixed physics respects FixedGeometryProfile: the flow used by FixedPhysicsProfile remains compatible with the fixed nucleus, and all observed behaviours satisfy the geometric laws captured by FixedGeometryProfile.

  3. Fixed invariant states.
    FixedInvariant picks out precisely those physics states that are fixed by the global flow (StabilizeInvariant) and recognised inside the fixed profile (FixedProfileRecognitions). These are the fixed invariant states: states whose evolution is both dynamically stable and compatible with FixedShape.

Proof sketch

  1. ProfileSemantics = (StructureSemantics ProfileRecognitions) interprets the recognition algebra in any profile, and SoundProfilePhysics = (PhysicsProfile ProfileSemantics) records that profile-level physics stays within those semantics. FixedProfileSemantics = (ProfileSemantics FixedProfileRecognitions) further restricts the semantics to the fixed recognitions, so composing it with SoundProfilePhysics cannot produce a judgement outside that calculus. The act SoundFixedPhysicsSemantics records this preservation.

  2. FixedGeometryProfile packages the compatible pair (FixedFlowProfile, FixedNucleateProfile). FixedPhysicsProfile uses FixedFlowProfile as its dynamical backbone, and FlowObservation was constructed to respect the same commutation with the nucleus (via CommuteFlowAndNucleus and its shuffle specialization). Thus the observations produced by FixedPhysicsProfile satisfy the same compatibility laws as the geometry, and SoundFixedPhysicsGeometry records this geometric soundness.

  3. StabilizeInvariant = (FixFlow PhysicsState) identifies states fixed by the global flow, while FixedProfileRecognitions identifies recognitions that lie inside the fixed profile. Their composite FixedInvariant therefore selects states that simultaneously (a) are genuine physical states with globally fixed dynamics and (b) are recognised inside the fixed region determined by FixedShape. No other states satisfy both constraints, so these are exactly the fixed invariant states.

title: Policy Audit Console Program id: policy-audit-console-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Policy Audit Console Program

Program-layer wiring that exposes policy inspection, audit browsing, and traceability queries under strict access control using computer carriers.

Behaviour

  • Ingest audit/policy recognitions; validate access credentials via Computer Recognition.
  • Serve queries over policy state and audit logs; attach traceability.
  • Emit Computer Failure on auth or policy violations.

Invariants

title: Policy Regime id: policy-regime role: structure status: plan derived_from: math/acts/govern-regime proof_refs: []

Policy Regime

Earned policy carrier combining discipline/filter/regime data with trace context.

Definition

  • Carrier: policies earned by Govern Regime and its supporting acts (discipline, filter), attached to a Trace Carrier context.
  • Fields: discipline partitions (admissible flows), filters (constraints on recognitions/flows), regime composition data, and associated trace scope.

Invariants

  • Partition coherence: disciplines/filters partition admissible flows without overlap.
  • Trace scope: policy applicability is scoped to the attached trace spine.
  • Composition: regimes compose disciplines/filters associatively with identity.

Proof

  • Derived from: Govern Regime with trace context from Trace Carrier.
  • Shows that: lifting policy acts into calculus yields a parameterised policy carrier usable by recognition and system scheduling.

title: Port Set id: port-set role: structure status: plan proof_refs:

  • operator-signature fields:
  • name: ports type: runtime-value description: list of {port_id, step_id, direction ∈ {in,out}, carrier_id}.
  • name: signatures type: runtime-value description: optional back-reference from ports to their inducing operator signatures.
  • name: policy type: runtime-value description: optional discipline/filter annotations per port.

Port Set

Carrier for the collection of ports in a connection graph or wiring plan, including their types, directions, and policy annotations.

Definition

  • ports: list of {port_id, step_id, direction ∈ {in,out}, carrier_id}.
  • signatures: optional back-reference to Operator Signature that induced the port typing.
  • policy: optional discipline/filter annotations per port.

Invariants

  • Direction: each port direction is either in or out.
  • Typing: carrier_id is earned; for ports induced from signatures, the type matches the signature’s corresponding input/output position.
  • Uniqueness: (step_id, port_id) pairs are unique.

Proof

  • Uses: Operator Signature to induce port typings on steps in a Connection Graph.
  • Shows that: collecting ports with their types/directions and policy annotations yields a reusable set for typing and wiring checks; no unearned defaults are assumed.

title: Ports Carrier id: ports-carrier role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Ports Carrier

Math-level carrier for ports/interfaces: endpoints for serialized recognitions and messages.

Definition

  • Components: input/output endpoints tied to Message Channels, plus Section Encoding Schema for serialization.
  • Role: boundary for injecting/extracting recognitions into channels.

Invariants

  • Type safety: ports accept recognitions encoded per schema.
  • Consistency: port directions (in/out) respected; messages tagged with channel.

Proof
