  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Computer State id: relational-state role: structure status: later proof_refs: []

Computer State

Definition

  • Carrier: relational state sheaf section capturing recognitions, evaluation state, and wiring snapshots as a single carrier.
  • Purpose: expose the earned relational state to downstream operators without bypassing invariants encoded in the carriers.

Invariants

  • Consistency: recognitions, evaluation state, and wiring plan references are mutually consistent (bindings align with ports, recognitions align with evaluation contexts).
  • Section law: behaves as a sheaf section over the underlying site of ports and steps; restriction/gluing respect earned locality laws.
  • Mutation discipline: updates occur via earned operators (evaluation, recognition, wiring); no out-of-band mutation.

Proof

  • Uses: Recognition Carrier, Evaluation State, and Wiring Plan as components; sheaf laws from the profile/sheaf acts to enforce section behaviour.
  • Shows that: bundling recognition, evaluation, and wiring into a single relational state carrier yields a stable, type-safe snapshot of computation, updated only through earned operators.

title: Relational Structure id: relational-structure role: structure status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/structure proof_refs: []

Relational Structure

The Relational Structure is the foundational carrier for all mathematical objects in this programme.

Definition

  • Underlying set |S|.
  • Distinguished relations {R_i} each with arity n_i ≥ 1.
  • Operations are treated as (n+1)‑ary relations that are single-valued and total in the last coordinate.

Invariants

  • |S| is nonempty.
  • Relations/operations are well-typed over |S|.

Proof

Directly derived from acts/structure, which introduces the general notion of relational structure used throughout the repository.

title: Relational Vacuum id: relational-vacuum role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Relational Vacuum

Ground-like state: recognition with minimal shape, entropy, and flow amplitude.

Definition

  • Elements: sections/recognitions with minimal entropy from the Entropy Functional and minimal flow variation.
  • Role: baseline state for perturbation/energy comparisons.

Invariants

  • Minimality: no admissible state has lower entropy/shape complexity.
  • Stability: vacuum is fixed under flow/nucleus.

Proof

  • Uses: entropy functional and hypertensor section to define minima.
  • Shows that: a ground state exists as a reference point in the relational system.

title: Relational World id: relational-world role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Relational World

Profile endowed with its earned topos and geometry, treated as a “world” for multi-scenario reasoning.

Definition

  • Carrier: Profile Topos equipped with Context Geometry describing spatial/temporal extent.
  • Structure: inherits flow/nucleus, logical structure, and geometric relations; closed under earned recognitions in the profile.

Invariants

  • Stability: closed under profile flow/nucleus and internal logical operations.
  • Geometric compatibility: geometry morphisms respect the profile topos maps.
  • Functorial: world morphisms preserve both geometry and logical structure.

Proof

  • Uses: Profile Topos and Context Geometry to package logic and geometry into a single carrier.
  • Shows that: a profile with geometry/topos data forms a stable world suitable for combination and transport.

title: REPL Program id: repl-program role: structure status: someday derived_from: computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

REPL Program

Interactive read–eval–print loop over the documentation interpreter carriers/operators.

Definition

  • Inputs: user commands/expressions recognised via computer Recognition, plus an initial Computer Evaluation State.
  • Loop:
    1. Read: ingest input and recognise it into wiring-plan fragments or commands.
    2. Eval: splice into the Computer Wiring Plan as needed and step with Evaluate Step.
    3. Print: surface results/failures via computer carriers; optionally persist with Storage.
  • Outputs: updated wiring plan/evaluation state and printed results/failures.

Invariants

  • Safety: all actions go through computer operators; no machine bypass.
  • Incrementality: each input produces a deterministic successor state given the prior plan/state.
  • Failure purity: failures are Computer Failure outputs; state unchanged on failure.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: exposing a read–eval–print loop via computer carriers/operators yields an interactive interface without extending kernel semantics.

title: Replication Controller Program id: replication-controller-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Replication Controller Program

The Replication Controller Program orchestrates snapshotting and replica coordination across the relational computer. It wires storage, policy, and topology checks to keep replicas consistent and recoverable using computer carriers/operators.

Carrier

  • Snapshot recognitions: identifiers and metadata for primary and replica snapshots as Computer Snapshot plus Computer Recognition for policy.
  • Replication wiring: steps to ship, validate, and apply snapshots across regions/strata.
  • Failure carriers: emitted on divergence, policy violations, or topology conflicts.

Behaviour

  1. Trigger snapshot creation per schedule or policy.
  2. Transfer and apply snapshots to replicas via Computer Storage; validate integrity.
  3. Monitor lag/health via measurement fabric; reconcile discrepancies.
  4. Emit Computer Failure on integrity failures or constraint violations.

Invariants

  • Replicas converge to the earned snapshot state within allowed lag.
  • Snapshots remain restorable and are validated before promotion.
  • Replication respects policy and topology placement constraints.

Proof

Refines the computer Kernel by specialising wiring to replication tasks over snapshot carriers, preserving failure semantics and acyclicity while enforcing replication invariants.

title: Replication Program id: replication-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Replication Program

This program coordinates replication of recognitions and sections across installations while preserving invariants.

Behaviour

  • Represent replication policies, peers, and logs as recognitions with provenance.
  • Synchronise slices of the relational universe without breaking earned semantics; record trace of what was replicated when.
  • Measure freshness, consistency, and divergence.

Intended operators

  • Configure replication peers and scopes.
  • Push/pull recognitions and sections with trace and policy checks.
  • Detect and resolve divergence or quarantine invalid data.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Replication Structure id: replication-structure kind: structure status: plan role: “Replicate snapshots across stores using distribution and commutation.” inputs: [] outputs:

  • {id: replication-structure, name: replication} program: [“struct”, “replication-structure”] depends_on:
  • storage-snapshot
  • distribution-channel-carrier
  • distribution-route
  • task-commutation
  • task-parallelization

Replication Structure

The storage, distribution, and concurrency structures induce a replication structure: a way to maintain multiple copies of a snapshot in sync despite concurrent updates.

Formally, there exists a structure

Replication = (Replicas, Snapshot, UpdateLog, Propagate, Resolve)

such that:

  1. Snapshot equality.
    Snapshot ranges over canonical snapshots from the storage structure; each element of Replicas stores the same triple (observations, baseline, fixed recognitions) when it is in a consistent state.

  2. Propagation.
    Propagate uses the distribution structure to deliver updates (derived from new observations or evaluation results) to all replicas by routing packets along appropriate channels.

  3. Conflict resolution.
    The concurrency structure shows that the relevant flow/nucleus operations commute, so applying updates in different orders yields equivalent states; the scheduling structure enforces dependencies to avoid inconsistent merges. UpdateLog records the sequence of updates applied at each replica, and Resolve reconciles logs when divergence is detected by rebuilding from a canonical snapshot.

Therefore we can replicate relational data across multiple stores without losing consistency: replication is captured by the Replication structure and its relation to storage, distribution, and concurrency.

title: Repository Environment Program id: repository-environment-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Repository Environment Program

Packages the documentation program and kernel wiring needed to manage this repository as a runnable environment. Treats the repo as state, the docs as inputs, and the documentation program as the maintenance loop.

Behaviour

  • Load documentation snapshots; run measurement and nucleation loops.
  • Apply operational controls (approvals, tests) before accepting changes.
  • Emit Computer Failure when documentation invariants or wiring constraints are broken.

Invariants

  • Docs obey layer/derivation rules enforced by the documentation program.
  • Changes are traceable and policy-controlled.
  • Failures surface as Computer Failure and do not mutate committed state.

Proof

Refines the Computer Kernel by wiring the documentation program and operational controls over the repository state via computer wiring plans and snapshots, preserving kernel semantics and failure handling.

title: Repository Process Program id: repository-process-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Repository Process Program

This program captures repository processes (branches, commits, reviews) as recognitions to keep development transparent and auditable.

Behaviour

  • Represent branches, commits, reviews, releases, and CI runs as recognitions with provenance.
  • Link code changes to specifications, policies, and tests.
  • Measure velocity, review health, and defect rates.

Intended operators

  • Record and review changes with traceable provenance.
  • Enforce policies (review requirements, testing) via recognitions.
  • Report repository health metrics to guide improvements.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Research Reading Program id: research-reading-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Research Reading Program

This program structures research reading as recognitions so sources, notes, and insights are traceable.

Behaviour

  • Represent papers, notes, questions, and syntheses as recognitions with provenance.
  • Link readings to projects, hypotheses, and experiments.
  • Measure coverage, recall, and influence on downstream work.

Intended operators

  • Plan and track reading lists; capture notes and summaries.
  • Connect readings to experiments or designs; surface gaps.
  • Share syntheses with traceable links to sources.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Resource Manager Program id: resource-manager-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Resource Manager Program

Manages allocation and lifecycle of computational resources (compute/storage) under policy and topology constraints.

Behaviour

  • Accept resource requests; validate policy and placement via Computer Recognition.
  • Allocate/deallocate resources; update recognitions; emit telemetry.
  • Emit Computer Failure on policy or placement violations.

Invariants

  • Allocations respect policy, topology, and traceability.
  • State changes are auditable and reversible.
  • Failures are recorded as failure carriers.

Proof

Refines the documentation interpreter by specialising to resource allocation wiring, preserving kernel failure semantics.

title: Reversible Flow Law id: reversible-flow-law role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Reversible Flow Law

Law governing when a flow step is reversible (entropy-neutral) or irreversible.

Definition

  • Criteria: a flow on recognitions is reversible if entropy (from Entropy Functional) is preserved and an inverse flow exists on admissible states.
  • Data: witness of inverse flow or entropy-neutrality.

Invariants

  • Reversible steps compose to reversible flows.
  • Irreversible steps strictly increase entropy or lose information.

Proof

  • Uses: flow context and entropy to classify reversibility.
  • Shows that: reversibility can be formally characterised with entropy bounds.

title: RG Fixed Point id: rg-fixed-point role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

RG Fixed Point

Sections or profiles invariant under repeated renormalisation steps.

Definition

  • Carrier: sections/profiles x such that RGStep(x) = x up to nucleus equivalence.
  • Role: phases or universality classes in relational physics models.

Invariants

  • Stability: applying RG Step leaves the element unchanged up to earned equivalence.
  • Attraction: nearby sections may flow toward the fixed point under RG.

Proof

  • Uses: RG Step to define invariance.
  • Shows that: fixed points of renormalisation form a carrier capturing large-scale phases.

title: Robotics and Embodied Systems Program id: robotics-embodied-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Robotics and Embodied Systems Program

This program models robots and embodied systems as recognitions so perception, planning, and control remain traceable.

Behaviour

  • Represent robots, sensors, actuators, environments, and tasks as recognitions with provenance.
  • Link perception, planning, and control loops to earned operators; record traces of executions.
  • Measure performance (success, energy, safety) and adaptation over time.

Intended operators

  • Configure robots and missions; run perception→planning→control loops with trace.
  • Analyse executions, failures, and adaptations.
  • Coordinate multiple robots with policy and safety constraints.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Runtime Value id: runtime-value role: structure status: plan proof_refs:

  • relationlessness

Runtime Value

Generic carrier for values manipulated by the interpreter kernel. It stands for any earned structure instance flowing through transformations.

Fields

  • tag: identifies the concrete carrier or primitive literal.
  • payload: the value for that tag (could be another carrier or a literal).
  • provenance: optional {origin, timestamp} for tracing value flow.

Invariants

  • Typed: tag must correspond to an earned structure id or literal tag.
  • Total: payload must satisfy the invariants of its tagged carrier.
  • Stable: values are immutable; transformations produce new runtime values rather than mutating existing ones.

Proof

  • Uses: the need to flow earned carriers (recognitions, sections, snapshots, etc.) through interpreter operators.
  • Shows that: a tagged/payload pair suffices to carry any earned value while preserving type information and provenance for interpreter steps; this carrier is induced by operator interactions rather than a base act.

title: Scale Embedding id: scale-embedding role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Scale Embedding

Morphisms between scale windows indicating how fine windows embed into coarser ones (and vice versa when reversible).

Definition

  • Data: inclusion/rescaling maps between Scale Windows that respect coverage and hypertensor structure.
  • Properties: embeddings preserve admissible sections/restrictions and align overlaps across scales.

Invariants

  • Compatibility: embeddings commute with window coverage/refinement.
  • Stability: composing embeddings yields another embedding.

Proof

  • Uses: Scale Window hierarchy to define admissible embeddings.
  • Shows that: scale transitions can be treated as morphisms for coarse-graining and RG steps.

title: Scale Tower id: scale-tower role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Scale Tower

Extends modality/profile towers with an explicit scale dimension for multi-scale analysis.

Definition

  • Components: levels of Scale Window organised as a tower aligned with Modality Tower.
  • Structure: rescalings between levels; flow/nucleus per scale.

Invariants

  • Compatibility: scale transitions respect modality/profile transitions.
  • Stability: tower closed under earned rescalings and restrictions.

Proof

  • Uses: scale windows and modality tower to build a multi-scale stack.
  • Shows that: scale can be treated as an additional tower dimension.

title: Scale Window id: scale-window role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Scale Window

Subcarrier of hypertensor windows with an explicit scale parameter to model coarse/fine views.

Definition

  • Levels: refinement order small → medium → large windows drawn from Hypertensor Window.
  • Embeddings: inclusions/rescalings between scales compatible with coverage and flow.

Invariants

  • Compatibility: restriction/rescaling preserves admissible sections.
  • Stability: scale hierarchy is closed under earned window refinements.

Proof

  • Uses: Hypertensor Window with added scale metadata.
  • Shows that: windows can be organised by scale to support renormalisation steps.

title: Computer SDK Surface id: sdk-surface role: structure status: later derived_from: machine/structure/kernel proof_refs: []

Computer SDK Surface

Program-facing API surface that exposes the documentation interpreter carriers and operators in a stable form for SDKs and applications.

Definition

  • Exposed carriers: Computer Recognition, Computer Evaluation State, Computer Snapshot, Computer Wiring Plan, and Computer Failure.
  • Exposed operators: Recognition, Evaluate Step, Storage, and Wiring.
  • Binding rules: SDKs may only manipulate kernel state through these operators; all machine-level fields not surfaced here are opaque.
  • Data formats: carriers are serializable in a schema-stable format (JSON/YAML) with fields defined in each computer structure doc; schemas should be emitted as JSON Schema or equivalent to enable SDK codegen.

Invariants

  • Stability: field names and failure modes are stable so programs compiled against the SDK remain valid as the machine evolves.
  • Fidelity: every exposed operation corresponds to a machine operator; results and failures are preserved exactly.
  • Safety: no direct mutation of machine carriers is allowed; all changes go through the exposed operators.

Proof

  • Derived from: Relational Machine Kernel, projecting its carriers/operators into a program-facing interface.
  • Shows that: constraining programs to this surface yields a well-defined API that preserves machine semantics while enabling external SDK/tooling.

title: Search Index and Views Program id: search-index-views-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Search Index and Views Program

This program expresses search indexes and views as recognitions so querying is traceable and honest to the underlying graph.

Behaviour

  • Represent indexes, queries, and view definitions as recognitions with provenance.
  • Bind indexes to underlying recognitions; avoid denormalised hidden stores.
  • Measure recall, precision, latency, and freshness.

Intended operators

  • Build and update indexes with trace.
  • Serve queries/views with provenance; surface what slice was searched.
  • Audit search quality and tune indexing strategies.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Section Algebra id: section-algebra role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Section Algebra

Algebra of hypertensor sections: sum/product/tensor and convolution/composition.

Definition

  • Operations: coproduct/product of sections, tensor-like combination when admissible, and composition via internal hom.
  • Convolution: section-level convolution induced by flow/gluing (aligning with Recognition Convolution lifted to sections).

Invariants

  • Associativity/commutativity for sums/products; distributivity where defined.
  • Compatibility: operations respect sheaf structure (restrictions/gluing).
  • Closure: results remain hypertensor sections.

Proof

  • Uses: internal hom and (co)limit structures to define algebraic operations.
  • Shows that: sections form a rich algebra suitable for program/data composition.

title: Section Collapse Depth id: section-collapse-depth role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Section Collapse Depth

Number of nucleus applications required to reach normal form.

Definition

  • Metric: counts collapse steps from a section to its Section Normal Form.
  • Role: measures simplification complexity.

Invariants

  • Non-negative integer; zero iff already in normal form.
  • Bounded for finite sections.

Proof

  • Uses: normalization process to count steps.
  • Shows that: collapse depth is well-defined and finite for admissible sections.

title: Section Encoding Schema id: section-encoding-schema role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Section Encoding Schema

Schema describing fields/types/serialization for hypertensor sections at the math/computer boundary.

Definition

  • Fields: identifiers, coordinates, payload types for a Hypertensor Section.
  • Serialization: canonical ordering, hash/digest expectations, allowed formats (JSON/YAML/binary).

Invariants

  • Determinism: encoding/decoding round-trips preserve section content.
  • Completeness: schema covers all fields needed for machine/computer layers.

Proof

  • Uses: section structure to specify schema.
  • Shows that: sections can be encoded unambiguously for computer use.

title: Section Internal Hom id: section-internal-hom role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Section Internal Hom

Internal hom/exponential on hypertensor sections.

Definition

  • Carrier: for sections A,B in Hypertensor Section, the internal hom B^A of morphisms respecting sheaf structure and coverage.
  • Evaluation/coevaluation maps satisfying exponential adjunction.

Invariants

  • Exponential adjunction: Hom(C×A,B) ≅ Hom(C,B^A).
  • Compatibility: internal hom respects restriction/gluing of the sheaf.

Proof

  • Uses: sheaf cartesian closed structure on sections to define exponentials.
  • Shows that: section category admits internal homs for higher-order operations.

title: Section Limits and Colimits id: section-limits-colimits role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Section Limits and Colimits

Limits/colimits of section diagrams in the hypertensor sheaf category.

Definition

  • Limits: products, equalizers of Hypertensor Section diagrams computed pointwise over the sheaf.
  • Colimits: coproducts, coequalizers computed pointwise with sheaf gluing.

Invariants

  • Sheaf compatibility: limits/colimits respect restriction/gluing; computed sectionwise.
  • Preservation: sheaf morphisms preserve these limits/colimits when exact.

Proof

  • Uses: sheaf structure to compute sectionwise (co)limits.
  • Shows that: section categories admit small limits/colimits needed for program/data composition.

title: Section Normal Form id: section-normal-form role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Section Normal Form

Canonical representative of a hypertensor section obtained via nucleus/collapse steps for deterministic comparison.

Definition

  • Representative: section collapsed via nucleus and simplification rules to a unique normal form.
  • Role: enables deterministic equality checks and hashing.

Invariants

  • Uniqueness up to earned equivalence.
  • Idempotent: normalizing twice yields the same result.

Proof

  • Uses: nucleus and section simplification to define canonical form.
  • Shows that: sections admit canonical representatives for comparison/encoding.

title: SelfWorld Renormalization Program id: selfworld-renormalization-program role: structure status: someday derived_from: computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

SelfWorld Renormalization Program

Program that identifies, aligns, and stabilises cross-profile patterns between inner symbolic life and external experiential worlds.

Definition

  • Inputs: inner (dreams, symbols, narratives) and outer (events, projects, relationships) artefacts recognised via computer Recognition into Computer Recognition carriers; initial Computer Evaluation State.
  • Plan: maintain separate profile towers and correspondences as a Computer Wiring Plan; evaluate/adjust via Evaluate Step.
  • Persistence: save/replay via Computer Storage and Computer Snapshot.
  • Outputs: stabilised wiring plan/recognitions, or failures.

Behaviour

  • Map correspondences between inner and outer recognitions via earned part–whole, association, and flow relations.
  • Detect unstable or recursive patterns across profiles (discontinuities, loops, attractors).
  • Propose stabilisations by adjusting flows or contexts in either profile.

Invariants

  • Profile separation: inner/outer profiles remain distinct but linked via earned relations.
  • Stability: accepted interventions increase stability of cross-profile invariants.
  • Failure purity: rejected correspondences emit computer failures; no partial mutations.

Proof

  • Derived from: Computer Kernel.
  • Shows that: multi-profile renormalisation across inner/outer domains can be expressed via computer recognition/flow/trace operators without extending machine semantics.

title: Semantic Subset id: semantic-subset role: structure status: plan derived_from: acts/subset-semantics proof_refs: []

Semantic Subset

Carrier of an inclusion-preserving subset that inherits the full semantic structure of its parent.

Definition

  • Carrier: subset S of a recognised structure X such that inclusion preserves all earned operations/relations on X.
  • Inclusion: a monic embedding i : S → X witnessing inheritance of structure.
  • Semantic closure: operations of X restrict to S; recognitions in S remain valid in X.

Invariants

  • Closure: S is closed under the earned operations of X.
  • Preservation: recognitions/relations in S coincide with those induced from X via inclusion.
  • Substructure uniqueness: given i : S → X satisfying closure/preservation, the inherited semantics on S are unique up to earned equivalence.

Proof

  • Derived from: Structuring Semantic Subset, which asserts that recognition-complete subsets inherit the entire semantic structure.
  • Shows that: the inherited operations/relations define a valid substructure carrier, giving Semantic Subset as the earned subset semantics of X.

title: Semantics Checker Program id: semantics-checker-program role: structure status: someday derived_from: computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Semantics Checker Program

Program-level checker that runs semantic validation/witnessing passes as wiring plans over the documentation interpreter.

Definition

  • Inputs: artefacts (docs/code) recognised into a Computer Wiring Plan and bound into a Computer Evaluation State.
  • Execution: run semantic checks (typing, invariants, linkage) by stepping the plan with Evaluate Step; use Recognition to validate witnesses/constraints.
  • Persistence: checkpoint checker state/reports via Computer Storage and Computer Snapshot.
  • Outputs: updated wiring plan/evaluation state and failure carriers for semantic violations.

Invariants

  • Completeness: checks cover all artefacts in the plan; no unchecked nodes.
  • Determinism: given the same inputs, the checker yields the same results.
  • Failure purity: violations reported as Computer Failure; no state mutation on failure.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: encoding semantic checks as wiring plans executed via computer operators yields a checker that preserves kernel semantics while validating program/doc artefacts.

title: Semantics Frames Program id: semantics-frames-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Semantics Frames Program

This program models frame semantics as recognitions so roles and fillers are explicit and traceable.

Behaviour

  • Represent frames, roles, and frame elements as recognitions with provenance.
  • Link frames to lexical units, domains, and discourse contexts.
  • Measure frame usage, ambiguity, and shifts across domains.

Intended operators

  • Annotate text for frames and roles.
  • Query and compare frame usage across corpora or domains.
  • Support generation/translation with frame-aware constraints.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Semantics as Normalization Equivalence id: semantics-normalization-equivalence role: structure status: plan derived_from:

  • math/operator/strong-normalization proof_refs: []

Semantics as Normalization Equivalence

Semantic equivalence on recognitions: two recognitions are semantically equal when all admissible derivations lead them to the same nucleus.

Definition

  • Admissible derivations fixed by the syntax layer.
  • Equivalence: x ≡ y iff every admissible derivation from x and y converges (strongly normalises) to nuclei that are equal via flow–nucleus compatibility.

Invariants

  • An equivalence relation on recognitions covered by normalization/confluence.
  • Changing syntax (admissible derivations) changes the induced semantics; the nuclei geometry remains earned.

Proof

Derived from strong normalization, confluence, and flow–nucleus compatibility acts: together they ensure unique normal forms (up to nuclei) for admissible derivations, yielding the semantic equivalence.

title: Semiolinguistics Program id: semiolinguistics-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Semiolinguistics Program

This program links semiotics and linguistics as recognitions to analyse signs and meaning-making.

Behaviour

  • Represent signs, signifiers/signifieds, codes, and interpretants as recognitions with provenance.
  • Connect semiotic structures to linguistic structures and contexts.
  • Measure sign usage, ambiguity, and shifts across media/domains.

Intended operators

  • Annotate and analyse sign structures in text/media.
  • Trace interpretive chains and code-switching.
  • Compare semiotic patterns across communities.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Semiosis Program id: semiosis-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Semiosis Program

This program studies semiosis—processes of sign creation and interpretation—as recognitions.

Behaviour

  • Represent sign events, interpretants, and chains of interpretation as recognitions with provenance.
  • Link semiosis to contexts, media, and agents involved.
  • Measure propagation, mutation, and stabilization of meanings over time.

Intended operators

  • Record semiosis events; trace interpretive chains.
  • Analyse how meanings change across contexts and media.
  • Surface where interpretations diverge or converge.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: S-expression Template Language Program id: sexp-template-language-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

S-expression Template Language Program

This program expresses templates in S-expression form as recognitions so code and data generation are traceable.

Behaviour

  • Represent templates, parameters, and expansions as recognitions with provenance.
  • Bind templates to earned operators; avoid ad-hoc generation.
  • Measure reuse, expansion cost, and correctness of generated artefacts.

Intended operators

  • Define and expand templates with traceable inputs/outputs.
  • Validate expansions against schemas or tests.
  • Reuse templates across programs with provenance.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Sheaf Assembly Program id: sheaf-assembly-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Sheaf Assembly Program

Builds and maintains sheaf assemblies from earned windows, towers, and sections for runtime and analytics consumption.

Behaviour

  • Ingest sheaf component recognitions (windows, towers, sections) as Computer Recognition.
  • Validate gluing/restriction constraints; assemble sections.
  • Emit assembled sheaf bundles or Computer Failure on constraint violations.

Invariants

  • Gluing/restriction satisfy earned sheaf axioms.
  • Assemblies are traceable to component recognitions.
  • Failures use FailureCarrier.

Proof

Refines the documentation interpreter by specialising to sheaf assembly wiring while preserving kernel failure semantics.

title: Sheaf Morphism id: sheaf-morphism role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Sheaf Morphism

Morphisms between hypertensor sheaves over the hypertensor site, preserving restriction/gluing structure.

Definition

  • Components: natural transformation between functors underlying Hypertensor Sheaf objects on the Hypertensor Site.
  • Preservation: commutes with restrictions and gluing/locality laws.

Invariants

  • Naturality: squares with restrictions commute.
  • Gluing: morphism respects matching/glued sections.
  • Exactness: pushforwards/pullbacks preserve finite limits where applicable.

Proof

  • Uses: sheaf structure and restriction algebra to define natural transformations.
  • Shows that: morphisms preserve earned sheaf structure on the hypertensor site.

title: Sheaf Restriction Algebra id: sheaf-restriction-algebra role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Sheaf Restriction Algebra

Algebraic laws for restriction maps of the hypertensor sheaf: functoriality and naturality with respect to the hypertensor site.

Definition

  • Carrier: restriction maps of Hypertensor Sheaf over Hypertensor Site.
  • Structure: identities on each window; composition along chains of inclusions.

Invariants

  • Functoriality: res_U^U = id; res_W^U = res_W^V ∘ res_V^U for inclusions W→V→U.
  • Naturality: restriction commutes with pullbacks along site morphisms.

Proof

  • Uses: sheaf axioms on the hypertensor site to derive restriction laws.
  • Shows that: restrictions form a functor from the site to sets/sections with naturality under site morphisms.

title: Sheaf Topos Program id: sheaf-topos-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/relational-state proof_refs: []

Sheaf Topos Program

This program investigates the topos-theoretic aspects of the earned sheaf structure.

Behaviour

  • Represent sheaf-theoretic constructions (limits, exponentials, subobject classifiers) as recognitions aligned with the earned sheaf structure.
  • Explore internal logic and categorical properties relevant to the kernel.
  • Measure coherence and adequacy of topos constructions for our sections.

Intended operators

  • Build and check categorical constructions on the sheaf.
  • Relate topos properties to machine/kernel behaviour.
  • Export findings to guide downstream program design.

Proof

  • Derived from: Computer Relational State.
  • Shows that: this program studies topos constructions over the computer-facing state without extending machine semantics.

title: Sheaf id: sheaf role: structure status: plan derived_from: math/acts/sheafification proof_refs: []

Sheaf

Earned subcategory of presheaves that satisfy the SheafCondition over a Coverage Site, packageable through the sheafification act.

Definition

  • Objects: presheaves on the coverage site whose restriction maps turn every cover into a Matching Family with a unique amalgamated section.
  • Morphisms: natural transformations preserving restriction maps and the matching-to-gluing property.
  • Sheafification: left adjoint to inclusion of sheaves into presheaves; freely adds amalgams so the sheaf condition holds for the chosen coverage.
  • Locality data: equalizer diagrams for restrictions over overlaps encode locality, and gluing diagrams encode existence/uniqueness.

Invariants

  • Locality: sections agreeing on all overlaps are equal after restriction.
  • Glueability: every Matching Family has a unique amalgam consistent with restrictions.
  • Base-change stability: pullbacks along the coverage preserve sheaves and their gluing data.
  • Path compatibility: restriction along composed paths in the Fundamental Groupoid matches iterated restriction in the site, preventing holonomy from breaking sheaf laws.

Proof

  • Derived from: Sheafification which freely enforces the SheafCondition on presheaves over the chosen coverage.
  • Shows that: once coverage, restrictions, and matching families are fixed, the sheaf structure is the smallest stable subcategory closed under pullbacks and the gluing/locality laws, providing the logical substrate for classifiers and base change.

title: Snapshot Marker id: snapshot-marker role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Snapshot Marker

Special observation that tags the start/end of a snapshot on a channel.

Definition

  • Components: marker observations from Seed Observation inserted into Message Channels.
  • Role: delineate snapshot windows without altering channel semantics.

Invariants

  • Transparency: markers do not change channel behaviour aside from tagging.
  • Propagation: markers flow with causality and can be detected to cut snapshots.

Proof

  • Uses: Message Channel and marker act Seed Observation.
  • Shows that: snapshot boundaries can be marked recognitionally without perturbing message semantics.

title: Snapshot id: snapshot role: structure status: plan proof_refs:

  • storage-snapshot fields:
  • name: id type: runtime-value description: snapshot lineage identifier (symbol/string).
  • name: version type: runtime-value description: monotone counter for successive saves.
  • name: provenance type: runtime-value description: {actor, origin, timestamp} of the save.
  • name: payload type: recognition description: recognitions and evaluation state being persisted.
  • name: hash type: runtime-value description: integrity hash over canonical payload.

Snapshot

Snapshot carrier refining the storage snapshot with identifiers, hashes, provenance, and payload (recognitions and evaluation state) suitable for encoding, saving, and loading.

Fields

  • id: stable identifier for the snapshot lineage.
  • version: monotone counter for successive saves.
  • provenance: {actor, origin, timestamp} describing who/when/where saved.
  • payload: recognitions and evaluation state being persisted.
  • hash: integrity hash over the canonical payload.

Invariants

  • Integrity: hash covers payload and metadata; must verify on load.
  • Fidelity: save/load round-trips yield observationally equivalent recognitions/evaluation state, or an explicit failure.
  • Stability: payload adheres to the storage snapshot schema; no extra state.
  • Monotonicity: version increases on each successful save in a lineage.

Proof

  • Uses: storage snapshot schema and the round-trip requirement captured in Persistence Stack.
  • Shows that: adding ids/provenance/hash to the storage snapshot yields a carrier that supports encoding, saving, and loading with explicit integrity and round-trip guarantees.

title: Socket Gateway Program id: socket-gateway-program role: structure status: someday derived_from: computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Socket Gateway Program

Program-level gateway that exposes the documentation interpreter over line-oriented network sockets (TCP/WebSocket), translating socket I/O into recognised commands and wiring-plan updates.

Definition

  • Inputs: socket connections carrying line/JSON frames; initial Computer Evaluation State.
  • Behaviour:
    1. Accept connections; recognise inbound frames via computer Recognition into commands.
    2. Splice commands into a Computer Wiring Plan and step with Evaluate Step.
    3. Surface results/failures back over the socket; optionally checkpoint via Storage.
  • Outputs: updated wiring plan/evaluation state and socket responses (results or Computer Failure).

Invariants

  • Protocol safety: only recognised commands are accepted; malformed frames are rejected without mutating state.
  • Isolation: per-connection bindings are isolated; shared state changes flow through the documentation interpreter.
  • Failure purity: failures are surfaced as computer failures; state unchanged on failure.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: translating socket I/O into recognised commands and executing them via computer operators yields a network gateway without extending kernel semantics.

title: Spectral Gap id: spectral-gap role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Spectral Gap

Records the difference between the leading harmonic mode and subsequent modes, bounding mixing/relaxation rates.

Definition

  • Data: ordered Spectrum with distinguished leading Harmonic Mode; gap witness between the first and second modes (numeric or relational).
  • Role: controls convergence rates in stochastic evolutions and entropy decay.

Invariants

  • Positivity: gap is non-negative; larger gap implies faster mixing.
  • Stability: gap persists under admissible perturbations of the kernel.

Proof

  • Uses: Spectrum and Harmonic Mode to define the gap.
  • Shows that: the gap data can bound convergence in Entropy Spectral Fixed Point scenarios.

title: Spectral Theorem id: spectral-theorem role: structure status: plan derived_from: [] proof_refs: []

Spectral Theorem

This stub notes the intent to earn a spectral theorem appropriate to the relational calculus. It awaits acts establishing linear operators and spectral decompositions within the earned structures.

Proof

  • TODO: fill proof.

title: State Transition id: state-transition role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

State Transition

Math-level description of a state update over sections for persistence/consistency.

Definition

  • Components: source Hypertensor Section, target section, and flow rule from Flow Context mapping source to target.
  • Purpose: model snapshots/state updates abstractly.

Invariants

  • Consistency: transition follows admissible flow; preserves sheaf invariants.
  • Idempotence for no-op transitions; compositional over sequences.

Proof

  • Uses: flow context to define valid transitions.
  • Shows that: state updates can be specified as math carriers for downstream machine snapshots.

title: Step Set id: step-set role: structure status: plan emerges_from: relationlessness proof_refs:

  • relationlessness

Step Set

Carrier for the collection of operator steps, including entry/exit designations and any scheduling metadata.

Proof

  • Uses: Connection Graph step metadata.
  • Shows that: collecting steps into a set allows entry/exit extraction and scheduling checks for wiring.

title: Stochastic Fixed Point id: stochastic-fixed-point role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Stochastic Fixed Point

States stable in distribution under repeated stochastic (Markov-like) evolution.

Definition

  • Carrier: states/recognitions invariant under Hypertensor Stochastic Evolution up to distribution (fixed invariant measures).
  • Relation: aligns with Fixed Invariant State but phrased for distributional stability.

Invariants

  • Stationarity: evolution leaves the distribution unchanged.
  • Stability: small perturbations return to the fixed distribution under evolution.

Proof

  • Uses: stochastic evolution kernel and fixed invariant states.
  • Shows that: distributional fixed points are a natural carrier for stochastic dynamics.

title: Storage Service Program id: storage-service-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Storage Service Program

The Storage Service Program is a computer-layer wiring of kernel storage, policy, and topology constraints to provide snapshot read/write, backup, and restore capabilities to clients via computer carriers and operators.

Carrier

  • Snapshot carriers: identifiers and contents for saved state as Computer Snapshot.
  • Policy/topology recognitions: constraints on who may read/write and where snapshots may reside, expressed as Computer Recognition.
  • Failure carriers: emitted on policy, integrity, or placement violations as Computer Failure.

Behaviour

  1. Validate storage requests against policy and topology constraints.
  2. Perform save/load operations on snapshots via Computer Storage; verify integrity hashes.
  3. Emit telemetry and traceability for each operation.
  4. Emit Computer Failure on invalid requests or integrity failures.

Invariants

  • Snapshots round-trip under save/load with validated integrity.
  • Access obeys policy and placement constraints.
  • All operations are traceable and auditable.

Proof

Refines the computer Kernel by wiring storage and recognition operators over computer carriers, preserving kernel failure semantics while enforcing storage invariants.

title: Storage Snapshot id: storage-snapshot role: structure status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/baseline-limit proof_refs: []

Storage Snapshot

The Storage Snapshot is the system-layer carrier for storing relational state. It packages the minimal data needed to save and restore a state: intake-normalised observations, a baseline remainder, and persistent recognitions.

Definition

  • Elements: triples (obs, baseline, fixed) where
    • obs ∈ Intake Observation,
    • baseline ∈ Baseline Remainder,
    • fixed ∈ Persistent Recognition Carrier.
  • acts/baseline-limit earns the baseline component; intake/persistence acts earn the observation and fixed recognitions. This structure lifts those math carriers into the system context.

Invariants

  • Each triple uniquely determines the stored state up to earned equivalence.
  • Components remain in their earned carriers (intake-normalised, baseline tail, persistent recognitions).

Proof

Derived from acts/baseline-limit and the intake/persistence acts that earn the observation and fixed-recognition carriers. This system structure lifts the math carriers into a storage payload usable by system/machine storage operators.

title: Stratified Infinity-Site Structure id: stratified-site-structure role: “Directed, stratified \u221E-site induced by discipline/filter/tower acts.” status: plan inputs: [] outputs:

  • id: stratified-site-structure name: site proof_refs: []

Stratified Infinity-Site Structure

The profile, discipline, filter, and tower acts induce the structure of a directed, stratified ∞-site. In particular, they produce:

  • a small ∞-category C of recognitions and their directed morphisms,
  • a Grothendieck-style coverage (tau) on C, and
  • a stratification function σ : Ob(C) → ℕ compatible with the directed structure.

Concretely:

  • Discipline and filter acts stratify recognitions into levels (σ = 0,1,2,…) and define covering families that are stable under flow–nucleus behaviour and base change, as summarised in the external theorem on directed stratified ∞-sites.
  • Tower acts (such as profile towers) organise objects and morphisms into higher-dimensional cells, giving the ∞-level structure of C.
  • The sheaf condition and presheaf-topos acts ensure that the coverage data behaves like a Grothendieck coverage on this stratified directed category.

We call any such triple (C, (tau), σ) a stratified ∞-site in the relational setting: objects are judgements/recognitions, morphisms are derivations, and the stratification records their level in the profile/tower hierarchy.

Proof

  • TODO: fill proof.

title: Subobject Classifier id: subobject-classifier role: structure status: plan derived_from: math/structure/relational-sheaf-topos proof_refs: []

Subobject Classifier

Logical carrier that classifies monomorphisms in the earned presheaf/sheaf topos; provides truth values and characteristic maps.

Definition

  • Carrier: object Omega equipped with:
    • true: 1 → Omega (global truth),
    • For every mono m: A → B, a unique characteristic map chi_m: B → Omega such that A is the pullback of true along chi_m.
  • Logical structure: operations induced from the Relational Logic Algebra (meet/join/implication) acting on subobjects via sieves/recognitions.

Invariants

  • Classifier property: monos are in bijection with characteristic maps into Omega.
  • Pullback stability: characteristic squares commute for any morphism.
  • Truth values coincide with recognitions in the relational logic algebra.

Proof

  • Derived from: Relational Sheaf Topos as the ambient category supporting subobjects.
  • Uses: Relational Logic Algebra to endow Omega with logical operations and to interpret characteristic maps as recognitions.
  • Shows that: equipping the earned topos with Omega and true satisfying the classifier property yields a subobject classifier whose logical content is the relational logic algebra.

title: Subscriptions and Bills Program id: subscriptions-bills-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Subscriptions and Bills Program

This program tracks subscriptions and bills as recognitions so obligations and payments are traceable.

Behaviour

  • Represent subscriptions, bills, payments, and renewals as recognitions with provenance.
  • Link to budgets, accounts, and policies; schedule renewals and reminders.
  • Measure spending, upcoming obligations, and compliance with policy.

Intended operators

  • Register subscriptions and bills; schedule and record payments.
  • Alert on upcoming or failed payments; reconcile with accounts.
  • Review obligations against budgets and policies.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Syntax as Admissible Derivations id: syntax-admissible-derivations role: structure status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/step-trace proof_refs: []

Syntax as Admissible Derivations

The syntactic structure is the graph of admissible derivations: recognitions with traces and the allowed single-step witnesses between them.

Definition

  • Acts: StepTrace, Reduce, SeedEvaluationContext, BuildEvaluationContext fix the step witnesses and context lifting.
  • Derivation graph: vertices are recognitions with traces; edges are single-step witnesses lifted into contexts.
  • Admissible subgraph: select the allowed edges per proof/runtime policy.

Invariants

  • Syntax equality/legality is evaluated with respect to the chosen admissible edges.
  • Changing syntax means changing the set of allowed edges; recognitions/traces remain fixed.

Proof

Derived from the step/context acts; admissible edges are a chosen subset of the earned derivation graph, yielding the syntactic structure.

title: Syntax Service Program id: syntax-service-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Syntax Service Program

Provides syntactic services (parsing, normalisation checks) over earned acts and structures.

Behaviour

  • Ingest terms/derivations as Computer Recognition.
  • Validate admissibility and normalisation obligations via Evaluate Step; emit results or failures.
  • Expose APIs for syntax queries under policy controls.

Invariants

  • Only earned syntax/semantics are accepted.
  • Outputs are traceable to inputs; failures are Computer Failure.

Proof

Refines the documentation interpreter by specialising to syntax validation wiring, preserving kernel failure semantics.

title: Task Carrier id: task-carrier role: structure status: plan derived_from: math/structure/trace-carrier proof_refs: []

Task Carrier

Finite units of work in an informational system: evaluation steps, flow operations, measurement updates, each annotated with trace positions and port/channel metadata.

Definition

  • Carrier: tasks as records containing a reference to an earned act step, required inputs/outputs, associated System Trace spans, and port/channel bindings.
  • Purpose: schedule and route work within the system subject to trace ordering, interface constraints, and policy.

Invariants

  • Trace alignment: each task references the trace spans it reads/writes.
  • Port typing: inputs/outputs reference well-typed system interface ports.
  • Policy awareness: tasks carry policy regime references when required.
  • Finiteness: task sets are finite to admit precedence linearisation.

Proof

  • Derived from: Trace Carrier, using its ordered spine to attach trace spans to tasks.
  • Shows that: enriching trace positions with port/channel bindings yields a schedulable task carrier suitable for system scheduling operators.

title: Temporal Commutator id: temporal-commutator role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Temporal Commutator

Witnesses noncommutation of two time evolutions (e.g., flow with different contexts).

Definition

  • Components: pair of flows/evolutions applied in different orders around a Time Slice; recognition of their commutator.
  • Interpretation: difference between flow_A ∘ flow_B and flow_B ∘ flow_A.

Invariants

  • Zero commutator when flows commute; nonzero indicates temporal asymmetry.
  • Stability: commutator recognitions preserved under nucleus.

Proof

  • Uses: Flow Context compositions and time slice reference.
  • Shows that: temporal ordering effects can be captured as recognitions.

title: Temporal Kernel id: temporal-kernel role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Temporal Kernel

Kernel describing time evolution of recognitions (distinct from stochastic Markov evolution).

Definition

  • Transition: time-indexed operator on recognitions driven by Flow Context without probabilistic weighting.
  • Purpose: model deterministic or schedulable time evolution.

Invariants

  • Semigroup property: kernels compose over time intervals.
  • Flow compatibility: respects admissible flow/nucleus.

Proof

  • Uses: flow context to define deterministic evolution.
  • Shows that: time kernels can be defined apart from stochastic kernels.

title: Temporal Recognizer id: temporal-recognizer role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Temporal Recognizer

Binds recognitions to timestamps and stabilises them under time flow.

Definition

  • Components: recognition plus temporal metadata referencing a Time Slice.
  • Behaviour: propagates with time flow, maintaining consistency with saved memory/state.

Invariants

  • Temporal stability: recognitions carry valid timestamps; remain valid under time evolution.
  • Restoration: can be aligned with time slices for rollback or replay.

Proof

  • Uses: Recognition Carrier and Time Slice.
  • Shows that: recognitions can be temporally anchored for persistence/rollback.

title: Terminal Client Program id: terminal-client-program role: structure status: someday derived_from: computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Terminal Client Program

Program-level terminal interface (CLI/TTY) that drives the documentation interpreter via interactive commands.

Definition

  • Inputs: user keystrokes/commands; initial Computer Evaluation State.
  • Behaviour:
    1. Read commands; recognise them via computer Recognition.
    2. Update the Computer Wiring Plan as needed and step with Evaluate Step.
    3. Print results/failures; checkpoint session via Storage if requested.
  • Outputs: updated wiring plan/evaluation state; printed results or Computer Failure.

Invariants

  • Safety: only recognised commands mutate state.
  • Determinism: given the same inputs and snapshots, the session evolves deterministically.
  • Failure purity: failures are surfaced as computer failures; state unchanged on failure.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: a terminal loop using computer recognition/evaluation/storage yields a CLI/TTY interface without extending kernel semantics.

title: Test Case id: test-case role: structure status: plan derived_from: math/structure/trace-carrier proof_refs: []

Test Case

Finite traces/contexts/program fragments built using the earned evaluation state and step operators. Each test case is a valid relational program fragment.

Definition

  • Carrier: a finite Trace Carrier fragment with associated contexts/program steps to execute for validation.
  • Fields: initial context/state, sequence of steps, expected observations or equivalences.

Invariants

  • Well-formed: steps are admissible under earned evaluation semantics.
  • Finite: bounded length to admit execution/verification.
  • Deterministic expectation: expected outcomes are defined up to earned equivalence.

Proof

  • Derived from: Trace Carrier, using its sequencing to define test traces.
  • Shows that: defining tests as finite trace fragments yields executable test cases aligned with earned evaluation semantics.

title: Testing Harness Program id: testing-harness-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Testing Harness Program

Executes tests against the kernel and program-layer services, wiring evaluation, storage, and policy checks into repeatable test runs.

Behaviour

  • Load test recognitions (expected behaviours, fixtures) as Computer Recognition.
  • Execute runbooks under policy/topology constraints; compare outputs.
  • Emit success recognitions or Computer Failure on test failure.

Invariants

  • Tests run against earned structures/operators only.
  • Results are traceable and reproducible.
  • Failures include origin and violated recognitions.

Proof

Refines the documentation interpreter by specialising to test execution wiring, preserving kernel semantics and failure handling.

title: Thermodynamic State id: thermodynamic-state role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Thermodynamic State

Fixed invariant state bundled with entropy (and optionally energy-like observables) to support thermodynamic reasoning.

Definition

  • Components: Fixed Invariant State, value of the Entropy Functional, and optional observables treated as energy constraints.
  • Structure: admissible flows that preserve invariance and evolve entropy monotonically toward minima.

Invariants

  • Entropy monotonicity: along earned flows, entropy is non-increasing and minimised at the fixed state.
  • Constraint preservation: observables/energy constraints remain satisfied under allowed flows.

Proof

  • Uses: Fixed Invariant State and Entropy Functional to package thermodynamic data.
  • Shows that: fixed states plus entropy/constraints form a carrier supporting thermodynamic interpretations of the earned stochastic dynamics.

title: Tiling Constraint id: tiling-constraint role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Tiling Constraint

Constraints on allowable hypertensor windows from aperiodic tiling/substitution acts.

Definition

  • Rules: local substitution/tiling rules on Hypertensor Window specifying admissible configurations.
  • Aperiodicity: rules exclude periodic tilings when required.

Invariants

  • Local rule completeness: every global section must satisfy all local rules.
  • Stability: constraints stable under refinement and window overlaps.

Proof

  • Uses: tiling/substitution acts to define admissible windows.
  • Shows that: windows obey earned local rules for quasicrystalline structure.

title: Time Slice id: time-slice role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Time Slice

Sheaf section paired with its saved memory carrier, representing a “moment” in relational time.

Definition

  • Components: Hypertensor Section and associated Memory Carrier.
  • Role: checkpoint for rollback or branching execution.

Invariants

  • Restoration: rolling back to this slice restores section up to nucleus equivalence and reinstates the memory carrier.
  • Isolation: morphisms crossing the slice must factor through its context or be discarded on rollback.

Proof

  • Uses: sheaf section and memory carrier to package a recoverable state.
  • Shows that: time slices can serve as rollback points preserving earned state and memory.

title: Timezone Profile id: timezone-profile role: structure status: someday derived_from: computer/type/computer-recognition proof_refs: []

Timezone Profile

Recognition of timezone rules (offsets, DST transitions) as parameters for normalizing calendar events.

Definition

  • Fields: zone id, base offset, DST rules/transitions, validity ranges, provenance.
  • Carrier: refinement of Computer Recognition scoped to timezone semantics.

Invariants

  • Transitions are ordered and non-overlapping.
  • Offsets apply deterministically for a given instant.
  • Traceability: retains provenance of rules.

Proof

  • Derived from: Computer Recognition.
  • Shows that: representing timezone rules as recognitions enables time normalization operators without extending kernel semantics.

title: Topology Integration Program id: topology-integration-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Topology Integration Program

Integrates topology/placement constraints (strata, coverage) across programs. Validates and distributes placement metadata to consumers.

Behaviour

  • Ingest topology recognitions; validate coverage/discipline filters via Computer Recognition.
  • Distribute placement/ordering constraints to dependent programs.
  • Emit Computer Failure on invalid constraints.

Invariants

  • Topology constraints respect earned tower/sheaf rules.
  • Distributed metadata is traceable and policy-controlled.
  • Failures are recorded as failure carriers.

Proof

Refines the documentation interpreter by specialising to topology constraint wiring, preserving kernel failure semantics.

title: Topos id: topos role: structure status: plan derived_from: [] proof_refs: []

Topos

This stub marks the intention to earn a topos structure (perhaps via the relational sheaf) with internal logic and categorical properties derived from acts. Further derivation is required to populate its components.

Proof

  • TODO: fill proof.

title: Tower Residue id: tower-residue role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Tower Residue

Information lost when collapsing a tower (modal/profile/hypertensor) to a lower level.

Definition

  • Components: residual recognitions not preserved by projection/collapse along the Modality Tower or similar towers.
  • Role: measures what collapses discard.

Invariants

  • Residuals vanish when projection is reversible.
  • Additivity: residues from successive collapses compose.

Proof

  • Uses: tower projections to identify discarded recognitions.
  • Shows that: tower collapses have well-defined residues capturing lost data.

title: Trace Carrier id: trace-carrier role: structure status: plan derived_from: math/acts/trace-step proof_refs: []

Trace Carrier

Earned sequencing backbone of traces with explicit indexing, used by trace, recognition, and task operators.

Definition

  • Carrier: ordered spine of trace positions earned by Start Trace, Advance Trace, Continue Trace, and Trace Step.
  • Structure: supports head/tail decomposition, renewal, and reindexing across the base, enabling trace-aware operators.

Invariants

  • Ordering: trace positions form a well-founded order with head/tail operations.
  • Renewal: advancing/continuing preserves earned sequencing constraints.
  • Reindexing-stable: trace structure remains valid under base morphisms.

Proof

  • Derived from: Trace Step, with the other trace acts providing constructors for the ordered spine.
  • Shows that: lifting the earned trace acts yields a parameterised trace carrier that can be composed with recognition and task operators and handed downstream.

title: Trace Renewal Program id: trace-renewal-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Trace Renewal Program

Maintains and renews traces over time: rolling windows, pruning, and re-attestation of recognitions.

Behaviour

  • Identify traces needing renewal; validate policy and topology scopes via Computer Recognition.
  • Re-attest recognitions, prune obsolete links, and persist updated trace snapshots.
  • Emit Computer Failure on inconsistencies or policy violations.

Invariants

  • Renewed traces remain consistent and traceable to acts/specs.
  • Policy and placement constraints are respected.
  • Failures are captured as failure carriers.

Proof

Refines the documentation interpreter by specialising to trace renewal wiring, preserving kernel semantics and failure handling.

title: Trace Shuffle Class id: trace-shuffle-class role: structure status: plan derived_from: acts/collapse-shuffle proof_refs: []

Trace Shuffle Class

Definition

  • Carrier: admissible shuffle classes Reorder(T_n) of completed traces.
  • Components: reorderings of trace progress recognitions that respect decomposition and recombination laws.
  • Source: earned from Collapse Shuffle, which fixes admissible shuffles after decomposition and shuffling.

Invariants

  • Closure: any permutation generated by admissible shuffles remains in the same class.
  • Equivalence: shuffles preserve trace observables; collapsed shuffles yield equivalent progress recognitions.

Proof

  • Derived from: Collapse Shuffle, which establishes admissible shuffles of traces.
  • Shows that: combining decomposition and shuffle acts yields well-defined equivalence classes of trace reorderings, forming the trace shuffle class carrier.

title: Traceability Service Program id: traceability-service-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Traceability Service Program

Provides APIs to query and update traceability DAGs, linking operations to earned acts/specs.

Behaviour

  • Ingest traceability queries/updates; validate auth and policy via Computer Recognition.
  • Resolve DAG nodes/edges; serve references to programs/services.
  • Emit Computer Failure on invalid queries or policy violations.

Invariants

  • Traceability data remains consistent and tamper-evident.
  • Access is policy-controlled and auditable.
  • Failures use failure carriers with provenance.

Proof

Refines the documentation interpreter by specialising to traceability wiring, preserving kernel semantics and failure handling.

title: Transition Law id: transition-law role: structure status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/transition-law proof_refs: []

Transition Law

The Transition Law maps fixed invariant states to successors via one flow–measure step.

Definition

  • Carrier: transitions earned by acts/transition-law over Fixed Invariant State.
  • Built from a flow step followed by measurement.

Invariants

  • Transitions respect the fixed invariant lattice order.
  • Flow then measure depends only on the current fixed invariant state.

Proof

Derived from the transition-law act, which composes flow/measure to yield a transition relation on fixed invariant states; lattice compatibility follows from the act.

title: Typed Judgment System id: typed-judgment-system role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Typed Judgment System

Typed judgments over hypertensor sections with derivation histories.

Definition

  • Components: typing contexts, Judgment Terms annotated with Hypertensor Section types, and Proof Traces.
  • Rules: inference rules assigning types to terms/sections; derivations recorded as proof traces.

Invariants

  • Soundness: only derivable judgments are admitted.
  • Preservation: evaluation preserves types.
  • Progress: well-typed terms either evaluate or are values.

Proof

  • Uses: judgment terms, section types, and proof traces to define the typing system.
  • Shows that: typing is coherent and supports derivation tracking for DSL specs.

title: Uncertainty Profile id: uncertainty-profile role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

Uncertainty Profile

Measures spread/instability of recognitions under flow via entropy/variance.

Definition

  • Components: an Epistemic Profile with attached entropy or variance scores from the Entropy Functional.
  • Tracks how recognitions disperse under flow iterations.

Invariants

  • Monotonicity: uncertainty decreases when informative observations applied.
  • Flow sensitivity: evolves according to flow-induced dispersion.

Proof

  • Uses: epistemic profile and entropy to quantify uncertainty.
  • Shows that: uncertainty can be treated as a first-class structure for flow analysis.

title: Virtual Machine Program id: virtual-machine-program role: structure status: someday derived_from: computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Virtual Machine Program

Program-level VM that executes bytecode/runbook wiring plans over the computer kernel carriers, providing scheduling and persistence.

Definition

  • Inputs: compiled wiring plan/bytecode expressed as a Computer Wiring Plan plus initial Computer Evaluation State.
  • Execution: step the plan with Evaluate Step, updating the evaluation state and respecting failure outputs.
  • Persistence: checkpoint/resume via Computer Storage and Computer Snapshot.
  • Outputs: updated wiring plan (e.g., with dynamic bindings) and evaluation state/failure carriers.

Invariants

  • Acyclic, well-typed plans only; enforces plan validity before execution.
  • Subject reduction/progress inherited from computer evaluation.
  • Crash safety: checkpoints preserve state; failures leave state unchanged.

Proof

  • Derived from: Computer Kernel.
  • Shows that: executing wiring plans via computer operators yields a VM that respects kernel semantics and exposes only computer carriers.

title: Void and Presence Witnesses id: void-presence role: structure status: plan derived_from:

  • acts/relationlessness
  • acts/nothing
  • acts/something proof_refs: []

Void and Presence Witnesses

Structure capturing the initial empty trace (Relationlessness) and its complementary witnesses (Nothing, Something).

Components

  • Empty trace witness (Relationlessness).
  • Absence witness (Nothing).
  • Presence witness (Something).

Proof

Sketch: from Relationlessness, forcing self-observation yields Something; forcing absence yields Nothing. These are the minimal witnesses for absence/presence.

title: Web Interface Knowledge Service Program id: web-knowledge-service-program role: structure status: someday derived_from:

  • computer/type/documentation-interpreter proof_refs: []

Web Interface Knowledge Service Program

A program to wrap the relational computer’s knowledge layer with a web-facing service for sites and federation (RDF/OWL, ActivityStreams/ActivityPub).

Separation

  • Knowledge Service: the internal knowledge graph (recognition carriers + edges, policy, topology, measurement) as runbooks.
  • Web Interface: thin translation layer (HTTP/HTML/JSON-LD/ActivityPub) that maps requests/responses to knowledge runbooks without inventing new storage.

Goals

  • Use the relational computer as the canonical store for concepts/notes/posts.
  • Align internal knowledge with RDF/OWL semantics; align activities with ActivityStreams/ActivityPub.
  • Keep the web interface minimal: only translate formats, never bypass runbooks.

Internal view

  • Knowledge resources: recognition carriers for concepts, notes, bibliographic entries, posts, external resources.
  • Relations: edge records (cites, references, expands-on), tags/metadata for classification.
  • Provenance: trace IDs, snapshots, audit logs, policy/topology annotations.

External correspondences

  • RDF/OWL: assign stable IRIs, emit classification/data/object triples from recognitions and edges; correspond internal knowledge graph fragments to RDF.
  • ActivityStreams/ActivityPub: map posts/actors/activities to internal recognitions; mediate inbox/outbox by translating ActivityStreams JSON to/from the knowledge graph.

Realisation path

  • Internal theorems: relational knowledge graph; relational activity streams.
  • External theorems: RDF/OWL correspondence; ActivityStreams correspondence.
  • Specs: knowledge graph simulation; ActivityPub simulation.
  • Program runs atop the kernel with runbooks for ingest/export and federation.

Proof

  • Derived from: Documentation Interpreter.
  • Shows that: this program instantiates the documentation interpreter to realise the described behaviour without extending machine semantics.

title: Wiring Plan id: wiring-plan role: structure status: plan proof_refs:

  • connection-graph
  • extract-graph-ports
  • extract-graph-edges
  • are-graph-ports-well-typed
  • are-graph-edges-well-typed
  • is-graph-acyclic
  • get-graph-entry-steps
  • get-graph-exit-steps

Wiring Plan

Wiring plan refining the connection graph into a concrete plan over earned operators and ports.

Definition

  • Steps: finite set of operator invocations drawn from Recognition, Evaluation Step, Storage, and Wiring.
  • Ports: typed input/output ports for each step, labelled by earned carriers (RecognitionCarrier, EvaluationState, Snapshot, WiringPlan, FailureCarrier).
  • Edges: directed connections from output ports to input ports respecting the carrier types and acyclicity (no cycles).
  • Entry/exit: designated source steps with no inputs and sink steps whose outputs are observed externally.

Invariants

  • Type safety: every edge connects ports with identical carrier types.
  • Acyclicity: the directed graph over steps has no cycles; supports a topological order.
  • Total wiring: every non-entry input port is driven by exactly one upstream edge.
  • Failure propagation: any edge carrying FailureCarrier may short-circuit downstream steps according to the evaluation-state rules.

Proof

  • Uses: Connection Graph plus extraction/typing/acyclicity checks (extract-graph-ports, extract-graph-edges, are-graph-ports-well-typed, are-graph-edges-well-typed, is-graph-acyclic) to validate ports/edges and entry/exit steps.
  • Shows that: a wiring plan is a typed, acyclic refinement of the connection graph with designated entry/exit, ready for evaluation.

title: Wiring Structure id: wiring-structure role: structure status: plan derived_from:

  • system/structure/connection-graph proof_refs: []

Wiring Structure

The Wiring Structure is the system-level graph of ports with compatibility relations describing allowed connections.

Definition

  • Ports: the set of interface ports from interface-port, tagged with trace, storage, policy, and measurement expectations.
  • Connect: possible edges between ports, requiring matching carriers, trace consistency, storage channels, and satisfiable policy/measurement constraints.
  • Compatible: relation on ports indicating they can be wired respecting evaluation, dataflow/storage, and tower/measurement coherence.

Invariants

  • Connections respect layer semantics (evaluation, storage, policy, measurement).
  • Compatibility is preserved under restriction to subgraphs.

Proof

Refines the connection-graph/interface-port structures into a single wiring structure capturing port compatibility per the earned stacks (evaluation, intake/persistence, measurement, tower interaction).

title: World Morphism id: world-morphism role: structure status: plan proof_refs: []

World Morphism

Morphisms between relational worlds that preserve geometry, logic, and the flow–nucleus adjunction.

Definition

  • Data: functor between the underlying profile toposes of the source/target Relational Worlds, plus maps on geometry and flow/nucleus consistent with the Flow–Nucleus Adjunction.
  • Preservation: carries truths, admissible traces, and geometric relations forward without violating earned invariants.

Invariants

  • Logical preservation: subobject classifiers and modal structure are preserved.
  • Geometric preservation: context-geometry maps commute with world morphism.
  • Adjunction compatibility: flow/nucleus structure is respected by the morphism.

Proof

  • Uses: Relational World carriers and Flow–Nucleus Adjunction to constrain morphisms.
  • Shows that: world-to-world maps maintain earned logical/geometric structure, enabling sums/products and multi-world reasoning.
